$schema_notes: Canonical scenario catalogue for the ir:onboard-agent skill. Two parallel axes: 'scenarios' for prompt-driven agent adapters (claudecode/codex/pi) and 'orchestrator_scenarios' for polling-based orchestrator adapters (gastown). Agent scenarios are agent-agnostic — each declares 'requires' capabilities, and 'by_adapter' holds the per-adapter prompt/settings that elicit the behavior. An adapter whose Capabilities (declared in core/adapters/inbound/agents/*/config.go) do not satisfy 'requires' is excluded from that scenario (matrix cell = N/A). A scenario may also declare 'requires_transport' (a list of transports: 'line_based' for FilesUnderRoot/FilesUnderCWD adapters, 'structured_store' for ProcessOwnedStore adapters like opencode) — an adapter whose capabilities.json 'transport' is not in that list is N/A too. This expresses constraints 'requires' cannot, e.g. oversized-transcript-line stresses the daemon's bufio line-scanner and is inherently N/A for a structured-store adapter that never flows through it (#496 RC7). If the capabilities match but no 'by_adapter' entry exists, the matrix cell is 'missing-prompt' — actionable by adding one here. Orchestrator scenarios are driven by canned 'gt' CLI responses + seeded GT_ROOT sidecars and verified via snapshot golden compare in core/adapters/inbound/orchestrators/gastown/replay_test.go.
$catalog_notes: Public catalog of all scenarios in the overview matrix. Mirrors the canonical id+section+feature list from .specs/agent-scenarios.md (gitignored, maintainer-owned). Repo-tracked so the dashboard can build the matrix without depending on .specs/. Order is significant — it determines the section.index code (e.g. 1.1, 2.3). 'scenarios' below is the recipe registry; entries here without a corresponding recipe render with no recipe authored.

scenarios.name: basic-turn
scenarios.description: Smallest possible round-trip. Verifies session detection and a clean ready→working→ready cycle.

claudecode.prompt: Reply with exactly the word: ok

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver launches `codex --no-alt-screen` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits for the TUI banner + MCP boot before sending the prompt
Each send bumps the expected-turn counter; wait_turn blocks until a new event_msg/task_complete line appears in the rollout, so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession_created (proc-<PID>) event in ready within 1000 ms of agent launch
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the rollout session with a distinct session_id, born ready
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the rollout session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the rollout session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same rollout session_id once the turn completes (the interactive REPL returns to ready, unlike headless codex exec which exits)
the rollout session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains a process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown
no state_transition to waiting appears for the rollout session at any point

pi.prompt: Reply with exactly the word: ok

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured provider/model (auth is set up out-of-band; the rig's precheck delegates auth to the user)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it stays alive across the turn
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` in a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in .aider.chat.history.md before sending the prompt

aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD), so the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message` (drift fix, same shape as the sibling full-lifecycle-toolcall + pi cells): aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run exits ~2.6s after the working flip — BEFORE that settle fires — so the daemon never observes the terminal working→ready edge and turn_end + teardown can't anchor (the prior 3/5 result). Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker), so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn; the 8s trailing sleep covers aider's ~1.5s IdleFlush quiet window plus the 5s working-refresh poll (one full idle-flush cycle plus slack) so the working→ready settle is observed before teardown

The send is a no-tool single turn — aider is asked to reply with exactly one word, so the working span carries no auto-executed tool call (tool_calls_max: 0) and the session never enters waiting (aider has no permission hook)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new + presession_created for the proc-<PID> session born ready within ~1s of REPL launch
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the same proc-<PID> session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the proc-<PID> session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn)

events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same proc-<PID> session_id once aider's turn-end is inferred (the `> Tokens:` line closes the model call and IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done after ~1.5s of quiescence, clamped to the 5s working-refresh poll) — observed within the 8s trailing sleep before teardown
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting for the session at any point (no tool call, no permission gate)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the proc-<PID> session at REPL teardown

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the default is a local LM Studio model so no remote key is needed)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the assistant turn finishes, so wait_turn is a no-op

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns (opencode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs)

The send is a no-tool single turn — opencode is asked to reply with exactly one word, so the working span carries no auto-executed tool call (tool_calls_max: 0) and the session never enters waiting (no permission gate fires)

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleep and timeout are generous: a slow turn must not trip a false timeout, and the trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so working→ready is observed

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id once the turn completes (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done closes the turn)
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point (no tool call, no permission gate)
the session's final live state is ready

scenarios.name: full-lifecycle-toolcall
scenarios.description: Tool use triggers a working↔waiting cycle via the PostToolUse hook. Mirrors the shape of the committed 10-full-lifecycle-* fixture. The session ends in waiting (not ready) because the hook keeps the session permission-pending after the tool completes; that's the behavior we want to lock down.

aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD), so the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message` (drift fix, same shape as the pi cell): aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run can exit promptly after the reply, so the daemon misses the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited and truncates the arc. Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker), so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn; the ≥6s trailing sleep is one full idle-flush cycle plus slack so the working→ready settle is observed before teardown

The send self-contains the fixture: aider is asked to create hello.txt then reply done, forcing exactly one auto-executed file-edit tool call inside the working span. Under --yes-always the file-add confirmation auto-resolves with no permission window, so the session never enters waiting (aider has no permission hook)

scenarios.name: agent-question-pending
scenarios.description: Agent's reply ends in a question mark, signalling it's waiting for user input. Validates the trailing-? path through the state classifier (session.IsWaitingForUserInput → state_classifier.go decision 2a): at turn end the session transitions working→waiting instead of working→ready because the last assistant message ends in `?`. This is the text classifier path, not a structured AskUserQuestion tool. For codex it is observable only via the interactive TUI (the live REPL session stays alive past turn end); headless single-shot CLIs that exit at turn end lose the waiting episode to a process_exited race.

scenarios.name: multi-turn-conversation
scenarios.description: Three consecutive user prompts in one live interactive REPL session, validating that state resets to ready between turns (ready→working→ready three times on one session) and per-turn Contribution accumulates. WHY interactive (not a headless single-shot run): single-shot modes end in process_exited after one reply, so they can't show turn-to-turn state reset; the interactive driver keeps the session alive across all three turns via send+wait_turn, then tears the tmux REPL down once. WHY the inter-turn sleeps between wait_turn and the next send: they sit past the classifier's debounce window so each turn's working→ready flip is observed cleanly before the next prompt re-enters working; without them the next send can land before the prior turn settles and the arcs blur. The required sleep length is adapter-specific — transcript-marker adapters (codex/pi) settle quickly (~3s), but aider has NO turn-end marker and synthesizes turn-end via IdleFlush behind a ~5s SessionDetector poll, so its recipe needs ≥7s inter-turn / trailing sleeps. HOW runs differ: exact assistant wording, per-turn token counts, and sub-second timing vary (model-output variance) — only the structural three-turn arc, distinct-session count, and final ready state are stable. WHERE to look if it breaks: the per-adapter transcript (codex rollout under ~/.codex/sessions; pi rollout under ~/.pi/agent/sessions; aider .aider.chat.history.md in the run cwd); a missing arc usually means a wait_turn timed out or a sleep was too short for the settle window.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers each prompt.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it can send three prompts to one long-lived session
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the first prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears

Each send bumps the turn counter; wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears (signals aider finished one model call). aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector poll (~5s effective latency)
After the third wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session

events.jsonl contains three state_transition→working / →ready arcs on the SAME session_id, one per prompt — the per-turn working→ready flip is synthesized by IdleFlush and must be observed before the next prompt re-enters working (this is why the inter-turn sleeps are ≥12s: a fast model like gpt-5-nano answers in well under aider's 1.5s IdleFlush + the daemon's 5s detector-poll settle, so a 7s gap can merge the next turn into the prior one — turn 3 coalesced into turn 2 in the prior recording; 12s leaves clear daylight so all three turns produce distinct ready→working→ready arcs, matching the sibling token-accounting/model-switch cells that hit 7/7 with 12s)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session, not three exits)
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point

per-turn token contribution (TotalTokens > 0) accumulates from each `> Tokens:` snapshot — observable in the parser's assistant_message events but NOT expressible in the expected.jsonl phase DSL (no metric form); aider's cost_tracking is false so no provider dollar cost is carried

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)

scenarios.name: interrupted-turn
scenarios.description: User interrupts the agent mid-turn (interrupt key sent before the LLM round completes — Ctrl-C for aider, Escape for claudecode/codex/pi). The interrupted turn never produces a turn-done marker, so each adapter's parser must reset cleanly on the next user prompt.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers the long-essay prompt so the turn stays in working long enough to interrupt mid-flight.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it can send a prompt, deliver a Ctrl-C interrupt, then send a recovery prompt to the same long-lived session
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line before sending the first prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears

TIMING (model is fast — gpt-5-nano): the first prompt MUST elicit a genuinely long/slow generation (an exhaustive multi-thousand-word essay) and the pre-interrupt sleep MUST be SHORT (~2s) so the Ctrl-C lands while the model is still streaming. A long sleep before the interrupt would let a fast model finish the turn first (writing a `> Tokens:` line), so the Ctrl-C would interrupt nothing — no real mid-turn abort.

The interrupt step sends tmux Ctrl-C. A Ctrl-C-cancelled turn writes NO `> Tokens:` line, so the parser emits no turn-closing assistant_message and the interrupt is NOT followed by wait_turn (it would hang). The driver decrements nothing here — the cancelled turn simply never completes a model call.

End-of-turn has no transcript marker in aider; the interrupted turn is closed by IdleFlush, which synthesizes a bare turn_done after 1.5s of transcript quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector staleWorking poll (~max(1.5s, 5s) effective latency) — this is why the sleep AFTER the interrupt is ≥6s (covers IdleFlush + the staleWorking poll so the post-interrupt working→ready settles before the recovery prompt) and the trailing sleep after the recovery wait_turn is ≥8s
After the recovery wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session

Do not summarize or stop early — keep writing continuously until you have covered everything in full depth.
Reply with exactly: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the live aider rollout session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the rollout session_id when the long-essay prompt lands (the first working in the whole recording — the pre-session never enters working)

after the Ctrl-C interrupt, events.jsonl shows a state_transition→ready on the SAME rollout session_id within the IdleFlush + 5s staleWorking window — the cancelled turn writes no `> Tokens:` line, so this ready is synthesized by IdleFlush from transcript-quiet alone, NOT by observing the interrupt (irrlicht has no abort-marker visibility for aider); state must NOT stick in working post-interrupt
the recovery prompt drives a second clean state_transition→working then →ready arc on the same rollout session_id, proving the parser reset after the interrupted turn
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session, not two exits)
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point

the cancelled turn contributes zero tokens/cost — no `> Tokens:` line was written for the interrupted model call, so the partial generation aider keeps in its conversation is invisible to the token/cost chips (accurate: no completed API call was reported)

scenarios.name: model-switch
scenarios.description: User invokes /model mid-session to swap the LLM, then runs a second turn on the new model. Validates that the parser's per-turn model metadata follows the latest model selection so the dashboard's ModelName updates to the new model. Aider exposes this as a one-shot `/model <name>` slash; codex exposes it as an interactive two-step picker (model list, then reasoning level) driven via `keys` steps.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a provider out-of-band; the driver does not handle a login/OAuth prompt.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it can send two prompts to one long-lived session, switch the model between them with `/model`, and tear it down cleanly.
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

BOTH models reachable on the account: turn 1 runs on aider's boot/default main model (model A, set deterministically via IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL=gpt-5-nano so it does not depend on ~/.aider.conf.yml) and the `/model gpt-4o-mini` switch target (model B) must resolve on the same OpenAI key. The concrete model values are environment-dependent (issue #440): the durable assertion is that the model CHANGES between turns and a non-empty model_name (model B) is present at session end, NOT a hardcoded pair. This recording used gpt-5-nano → gpt-4o-mini on an OpenAI key.

feature-invisible caveat: aider's `> Weak model:` line is intentionally NOT matched by the parser's modelRE (only `Model:`/`Main model:` drive ModelName), so a switch that changed only the weak/summary model would be invisible; this recipe switches the MAIN model, which is observable.
setup: SHARES coverage cell with aider/model-switch-midsession (same coverage_id model-switch-midsession, same primitive); this row exists so the `model-switch`-named matrix entry is populated for aider.
The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) launches aider in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt.

.aider.chat.history.md (FilesUnderCWD) materializes lazily once the first user input lands; before that the live REPL is observable as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner). The daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session.

The `/model gpt-4o-mini` slash uses aider's one-shot inline `/model <name>` form: cmd_model raises SwitchCoder(main_model=…), the new coder emits a `> Main model: <name> …` blockquote (parser modelRE → latest-wins ModelName), and NO assistant turn is produced — so no `keys` picker and no `wait_turn` after the slash.

Turn 1 runs on model A; the /model slash switches to model B; turn 2 runs on model B. message-level ModelName follows latest-non-unknown-wins so SessionMetrics.ModelName promotes to B by t2_done, and token snapshots accumulate across the switch.

After both turns + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the proc-<PID> session (a model switch does NOT mint a new session).

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> session born ready within 2s of launch (the lazy transcript has not materialized yet).
events.jsonl shows TWO ready→working→ready arcs on the SAME proc-<PID> session_id — turn 1 (model A) and turn 2 (model B) — with no state_transition to waiting between them.
the /model slash produces NO additional working/waiting transition between t1_done and t2_start — it writes only a `> Main model:` blockquote with no `> Tokens:` close (no assistant turn).
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered bound to the proc-<PID> session, and exactly one process_exited on it at REPL teardown (a model switch does NOT create a second session).
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point; final live state is ready before teardown.

the .replay.json.golden carries summary.model_name == model B (gpt-4o-mini, latest-wins) — NOT model A (gpt-5-nano) and NOT 'unknown'; this is where the model A→B switch is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value is environment-dependent (issue #440); assert change + presence, not a hardcoded string.
aider.notes: SHARES coverage_id model-switch-midsession with aider/model-switch-midsession (same Metrics → Model switch mid-session primitive); this row populates the `model-switch`-named matrix entry for aider. RECORDABILITY (no driver_gap): aider's `/model <name>` accepts an INLINE ARGUMENT — cmd_model (aider/commands.py:87, "Switch the Main Model to a new LLM") raises SwitchCoder(main_model=…) and switches DIRECTLY, no arrow-key picker — so the switch is sendable via the existing `slash` step (NOT a `keys` gap, unlike the codex two-step picker recipe for this same scenario). The aider interactive driver implements send/slash/wait_turn/sleep (case `send|slash)` types text + Enter), so every step type this recipe needs is supported. WHY interactive (not headless `aider --message`): the /model slash only exists inside the REPL and the scenario needs two turns; the interactive driver keeps the REPL alive across both turns and kills the tmux session once → a SINGLE process_exited (headless aider is single-shot and would exit after one reply, missing the per-turn reset and the terminal working→ready settle). WHY NO wait_turn after the /model slash: the driver's `slash` step bumps the expected-turn counter exactly like `send`, but a /model switch writes a `> Main model:` blockquote with NO `> Tokens:` close (no assistant turn), so a wait_turn after it would hang on the unchanged turn count; the recipe covers the switch with a fixed 8s sleep instead. AIDER TURN-END IS SYNTHESIZED, NOT MARKED: the markdown transcript has no turn-end marker — `> Tokens:` closes one model CALL, not the turn; end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after ~1.5s quiet, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll, so the EFFECTIVE working→ready latency is ~5s. The 7s inter-turn sleep (after turn 1) and 8s sleeps (after the slash, and trailing) let each turn's working→ready flip fire AND be observed before the next prompt re-enters working; with shorter sleeps the next `####` prompt lands before IdleFlush and the parser opens the new turn WITHOUT an intervening turn_done, losing the per-turn reset (TestParser_NewUserPromptAfterMultiCall_StaysWorking). The model A→B assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (no model field), so expected.jsonl asserts only the two-turn lifecycle skeleton and the switch is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden plus the aider parser unit test TestParser_MainModel_AfterSlashCommand (parser_test.go:214), which drives this recipe's exact `> Main model: openai/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored …` line. SESSION IDENTITY: aider stores its transcript in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD), so the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> — no separate UUID rollout session and no handoff phase; the turn arcs, pid_bind, and teardown all pin to session_birth via same_session_as. What differs between runs: token counts, PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with `Reply with exactly the word: first/second` to keep turns short and deterministic), and the working→ready edge lands with ~5s idle-flush latency. What must NOT differ: two working→ready arcs on ONE proc-<PID> session, summary.model_name == model B in the golden, and a single process_exited at teardown. multi_model_orchestration=yes. RECORDING DEFERRED: validated when aider auth is available in-env — at authoring time there is no provider key present and headless aider hangs on OpenRouter OAuth, so this recipe + spec are the DOCUMENTED TARGET pending a recording.

Both target models are reachable on the account: codex 0.130.0 boots on gpt-5.4 (model A) and the /model picker offers gpt-5.3-codex (model B). If your codex build's picker entries/order differ, re-probe and adjust the `keys` navigation — the Down-count is positional, not a model name.
The dev daemon is running with --record (run-cell.sh --attach attaches to it).

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> pre-session born ready within 1s of launch, then the rollout session reconciles to a new session_id (also born ready).
events.jsonl shows TWO working→ready arcs on the SAME rollout session_id — turn 1 (model A) and turn 2 (model B) — with no state_transition to waiting between them.

transcript.jsonl carries per-turn turn_context events: payload.model = gpt-5.4 for the turn-1 context and gpt-5.3-codex for the turn-2 context (the /model switch landed). session_meta.model is null in this codex version — the model lives only on turn_context.

The replay summary's model_name reflects the LATEST turn's model (gpt-5.3-codex) — the .replay.json.golden is where the A→B switch is asserted; expected.jsonl only covers the lifecycle skeleton because the events.jsonl DSL has no model field.
codex.notes: Codex /model is an interactive TWO-STEP picker (model list, then reasoning-level list), NOT a one-shot `/model <name>` like aider — so the recipe drives it with `slash /model` to open it, then `keys` steps to navigate. Probed live on codex 0.130.0: the model list is 1=gpt-5.5(default) 2=gpt-5.4(current/boot-default, highlighted) 3=gpt-5.4-mini 4=gpt-5.3-codex 5=gpt-5.2, so `Down Down Enter` from the boot-highlighted entry 2 lands on gpt-5.3-codex (entry 4); the reasoning sub-screen then opens with Medium(default) highlighted, so a bare `Enter` confirms gpt-5.3-codex medium (footer flips to `gpt-5.3-codex medium`, rollout writes `• Model changed to gpt-5.3-codex`). The two `keys` steps are split with a 2s sleep because the model-confirm Enter and the reasoning-confirm Enter hit DIFFERENT screens that need a render gap; the `keys` step does NOT touch the turn counter, so wait_turn stays correct. Model A is gpt-5.4 (the boot default in this codex build — NOT gpt-5.5 as in the original assessment text). What differs between runs: assistant token counts/cost, exact rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, and the assistant's wording (we pin it with `Reply with exactly the word: first/second` to keep turns short and deterministic). What must NOT differ: two working→ready arcs on one session, the model_name landing on gpt-5.3-codex, and process_exited at teardown. If the picker navigation drifts (codex changes the model list order), the Down-count is wrong — re-probe `/model` and recount. multi_model_orchestration=yes.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured models out-of-band)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs pi in a tmux REPL so it can send two prompts to one long-lived session, switch models between them, and tear it down cleanly
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

TWO models authenticated on the account: model A is pinned via a leading slash (openai-codex/gpt-5.5) and model B is a distinct switch target (openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini). Both must resolve to a UNIQUE exact match for the canonical provider/id form — if either model is unavailable, the /model switch silently falls back to the interactive picker and the script-driven switch fails.

The concrete model values are environment-dependent (issue #440): the durable assertion is that the model CHANGES between turns and a non-empty model_name (model B) is present at session end, NOT a hardcoded pair. The recipe pins both for determinism.
setup: EQUIVALENT FIXTURE to pi/model-switch-midsession (same coverage cell, same primitive) — `model-switch` is the duplicate scenarios.json entry sharing coverage_id model-switch-midsession; this row exists for matrix completeness.
The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt

pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy); the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback binds the UUID from line 1 (no wait_turn is used — see notes)

FIRST slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.5` PINS model A deterministically: pi persists the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so the boot model varies run-to-run; pinning A up front makes turn 1 reliably attributed to A regardless of what the previous session left as the default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix.

Each `/model <provider>/<id>` uses the CANONICAL provider/id form so pi's handleModelCommand resolves an exact single-model match and calls session.setModel() DIRECTLY — no arrow-key picker, no `keys` step. pi writes a model_change(modelId) line to the rollout (parsed for ModelName, no turn produced)

Turn 1 runs on model A (gpt-5.5); the SECOND slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` switches to model B; turn 2 runs on model B. message.model = A then B, so MergeMetrics' latest-non-unknown-wins promotes SessionMetrics.ModelName to B by t2_end and TotalTokens accumulates across both models

Both turns are driven by a fixed trailing sleep rather than wait_turn (the leading /model pin would desync the wait_turn counter — see notes); a one-word reply completes well within the 18s window (verified live)
After both turns + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — pi exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the rollout session (a model switch does NOT mint a new session)

verify: events.jsonl shows two ready→working→ready arcs on the SAME rollout session_id (turn 1 on model A, turn 2 on model B); the interactive REPL returns to ready after each, unlike headless pi --print which exits

events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered bound to the rollout session_id (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear born ready at launch; the turns and pid_discovered land on the UUID-keyed rollout session)
the /model slash produces NO additional working/waiting transition between t1_end and t2_start — it is a direct setModel() with no LLM round-trip and writes only a model_change line
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown (a model switch does NOT create a second session)
no state_transition to waiting appears for the rollout session at any point; final live state is ready before teardown

the .replay.json.golden carries summary.model_name == model B (gpt-5.4-mini, latest-wins) — NOT model A and NOT 'unknown'; this is where the model A→B switch is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value is environment-dependent (issue #440); assert change + presence, not a hardcoded string
pi.notes: EQUIVALENT to pi/model-switch-midsession (both share coverage_id model-switch-midsession; same Metrics → Model switch mid-session primitive). This row exists so the `model-switch`-named matrix entry is populated for pi; it is not a distinct user-observable behavior. CRITICAL FIRST CHECK RESULT (recordability): pi 0.70.0's `/model <provider>/<id>` accepts an INLINE ARGUMENT — handleModelCommand → findExactModelMatch → session.setModel() switches DIRECTLY with no arrow-key picker when the reference resolves to exactly ONE model (dist/modes/interactive/interactive-mode.js ~L3216, dist/core/model-resolver.js findExactModelReferenceMatch ~L53). Only an empty/ambiguous term opens the selector UI. So the switch is sendable via the existing `slash` step in SCRIPT mode — NO driver_gap (no `keys` primitive needed, unlike the claudecode/codex picker recipes for this same scenario). MUST use the full canonical provider/id form; a bare fuzzy term like `mini` may match multiple models and fall back to the picker. WHY a LEADING /model pin (gpt-5.5) before turn 1: pi PERSISTS the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so a bare `pi` launch boots on whatever the previous session left behind — NON-deterministic. Pinning model A explicitly at the top makes the A→B switch a real change every run regardless of the persisted default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix. WHY NO wait_turn anywhere (both turns driven by trailing sleeps): the driver's `slash` step bumps the expected-turn counter exactly like `send`, but a /model switch produces a model_change line with NO assistant turn (turn_count only counts stopReason==stop). With a leading /model pin, EXPECTED is already 1 before turn 1, so a turn-1 wait_turn would block waiting for count≥2 while only 1 turn exists → driver timeout. The grammar has no 'slash that doesn't bump' primitive, so the recipe drops wait_turn entirely and lets a fixed 18s trailing sleep cover each one-word reply; the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback still binds the UUID. Same family of compromise as the pi synchronous-slash-command recipe. WHY the 2s/3s sleeps after each slash: they let the model_change line land and the classifier settle before the next prompt. The model lives on each assistant message.model (parser.go L103-105) and on the model_change line at modelId (L39-44), both normalized into ev.ModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName (latest-non-unknown-wins). The model A→B assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL, so expected.jsonl asserts only the two-turn lifecycle skeleton and the model switch is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden plus the pi parser unit tests. C7/SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; session_birth legitimately matches it. t1_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working (necessarily the rollout); t1_end/t2_start/t2_end, pid_bind, and teardown then pin to t1_start's session via same_session_as. What differs between runs: token counts/cost, rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording, and pi may flush a single turn as more than one working→ready burst (spec asserts ≥2 arcs, not an exact count). What must NOT differ: model A (gpt-5.5) on turn 1 and model B (gpt-5.4-mini) on turn 2 (pinned), two working→ready arcs on ONE rollout session, summary.model_name == model B in the golden, and a single process_exited at teardown.

opencode.notes: scope_note: opencode driver has no per-step model selection / mid-session switch primitive — gap:model-select. drive-opencode-interactive.sh drives headless `opencode run` and implements only send/wait_turn/sleep; the `send` arm hardcodes `opencode run --format default [--session] -- text` with NO --model flag, so every turn uses the single config default model (lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored). No send-only switch path exists: the in-TUI /model picker is an arrow-key overlay (not drivable headlessly, and no `keys` step), and inline `/model <id>` is NOT processed by `opencode run` — proven live (2026-05-26): a send-path run of `opencode run -- "/model ionos/meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct"` produced a session whose only message still carried model.modelID=gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored/providerID=lmstudio (the /model text went to the LLM as content; the model did not switch). NOT an agent or daemon limitation — opencode supports model switching (multi_model_orchestration:true) and the daemon reads per-turn model from its SQLite store (ProcessOwnedStore), so a genuine switch WOULD be observable. NOT a recipe-lint step-TYPE gap either (a recipe would use only handled types); the missing capability is model selection within the send arm, confirmed by behavioral probing. UNBLOCK: add a per-step model-select primitive to drive-opencode-interactive.sh (thread `opencode run -m <provider/model>`), with model A = config default and model B = a second reachable model (e.g. ionos/<model>; IONOS_API_TOKEN is set). expected.jsonl is retained as the documented two-turn lifecycle target; see replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/model-switch-midsession/assessment.json (driver_capability: gap:model-select). multi_model_orchestration=yes.

scenarios.name: permission-hook-denial
scenarios.description: A failing Bash invocation fires the PostToolUseFailure hook, which drives a working→waiting→working→ready transition arc. Mirrors the committed 15-permission-hooks-* fixture. Uses a runtime failure (cat on a nonexistent file), not settings-based denial — settings-based deny just removes the tool from claude's toolset and no hook fires at all.

claudecode.prompt: Use the Bash tool to run: cat /tmp/irrlicht-nonexistent-xyz-123. If it fails, just tell me the error.

scenarios.name: checkpoint-rewind
scenarios.description: User rewinds to an earlier auto-checkpoint, restoring files and/or conversation; subsequent turns start cleanly from the rewound point. Each adapter exercises this differently: claudecode rewinds IN PLACE (Esc+Esc shortcut / /rewind picker) and mutates its transcript backwards, keeping the SAME session_id — the goal there is to capture what irrlicht observes when the append-oriented transcript_tailer sees a truncation (session-removed-then-reborn vs. activity). codex instead FORKS (/fork): it spawns a NEW rollout with a fresh session_id carrying the replayed pre-fork history (the original lingers frozen), so irrlicht observes a new linked session row rather than an in-place truncation. Every variant drives two normal turns, rewinds, then runs a third turn from the rewound point.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
claude version with /rewind support (Esc+Esc shortcut) — confirmed in 2.1.x
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
no other claudecode session uses the same UUID
setup: Generate a fresh lowercased session UUID
Create an empty cwd under <staging>/cwd/

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: one
Reply with exactly the word: two
/rewind
Reply with exactly the word: three

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession_created for proc-<PID> within 1000 ms
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the UUID session within 500 ms of the first send
events.jsonl contains ≥ 2 state_transition working entries (one per pre-rewind turn)
events.jsonl contains ≥ 2 state_transition ready entries returning from those turns
After Esc+Esc, the transcript file is mutated (truncated or rewritten); inspect events.jsonl for transcript_removed / transcript_new on the SAME session_id vs. just transcript_activity
Post-rewind turn shows a clean ready → working → ready arc on the rewound session
Final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl shows turns 1/2 then a structural break (truncation or marker) then turn 3

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY) — verified codex 0.130.0
codex version with /fork support — verified in 0.130.0 (TUI prints 'Thread forked from <uuid>')
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

STALE-VERDICT OVERRIDE: the prior by_adapter.codex was applicable:false on the false belief codex 0.130 has no rewind. Codex DOES support rewind/fork: /fork clones the conversation into a NEW thread (fresh session id; original intact), and Esc-Esc on an empty composer walks the transcript back to edit a prior message. Feature requests #11626/#12558 ask codex to ALSO revert code edits — orthogonal to the conversation fork that already ships. See assessment.json.

PARTIAL observability (per assessment.json): codex forks rather than rewinding in place, so the observable is a NEW linked session_id (not the same session_id with a truncated transcript like claudecode). The codex parser skips session_meta lines, so the forked_from_id provenance is dropped and the daemon sees the fork as an independent fresh session, not a linked child. codex emits NO transcript_removed for the original at fork time (the old rollout lingers frozen until process_exited).

KNOWN CAPTURE GAP: drive-codex-interactive.sh caches the first rollout and writes a single session.uuid, so curate-lifecycle-fixture.sh filters the fixture to UUID-1 only. The v2_* (post-/fork) phases assert what the daemon observes LIVE; they may be absent from the single-session curated fixture, surfacing as validation drift until the codex driver tracks /fork-spawned sessions (a developer task). SAME gap as codex/session-reset.
setup: Driver launches codex in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (isolates the trust dialog).

Codex assigns its own session UUID per rollout; there is no --session-id flag. The first prompt materializes rollout-<ts>-<uuid1>.jsonl; /fork EAGERLY materializes a second rollout-<ts>-<uuid2>.jsonl carrying a fresh session_id, a session_meta forked_from_id=uuid1, and the replayed pre-fork history. UNLIKE /clear (lazy: second rollout appears only on the first post-clear prompt), /fork is eager — the new rollout appears the instant the command runs.

/fork is sent as a plain send/slash step (text + Enter): the codex driver handles send/slash as text+Enter. NO keys/restart/resume step types are used — the codex driver does not implement them. Esc-Esc rewind is deliberately avoided because the driver's interrupt step decrements the turn counter, which would desync wait_turn.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: one
Reply with exactly the word: two
Reply with exactly the word: three

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> pre-session entering ready, handing off to the FIRST rollout-keyed session in ready
events.jsonl shows the FIRST rollout session running two working→ready turns (turn 1 'one', turn 2 'two') — the checkpoints to fork back from

after /fork, a DIFFERENT rollout session_id appears in ready (new linked session row, restored from the rewound point) — the 'a linked new session_id if the adapter forks' signal from scenario-meanings, observed live on the dashboard
the post-fork turn's working→ready cycle is on the NEW rollout session, not the original — a clean ready → working → ready arc from the rewound point

codex does NOT emit transcript_removed for the original session at /fork time (the old rollout lingers frozen) — the original row stays ready until process_exited; do not assert a removal event, and do not assert a same-session_id in-place rewind (codex forks)
final live state is ready

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH (verified pi 0.70.0) and authenticated (provider API key in env)
pi version with /tree in-place rewind — confirmed 0.70.0 (interactive-mode.js /tree → showTreeSelector → session.navigateTree → branch())
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

PARTIAL observability (per assessment.json): the rewind event itself is invisible (branch() moves the in-memory leaf pointer and writes nothing at selection time); only the post-rewind turn arc on the SAME append-only session_id is observable
setup: Driver launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/.

pi writes one append-only JSONL session file (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy) under sessionsDir(); the same file + session_id persists across the rewind — branch() appends the next turn as a child of the rewound entry, it does not mint a new file.

/tree is sent as a slash step (text + Enter) to open the picker; the picker is then driven with `keys` steps (Up/Down to highlight the turn-1 checkpoint, Enter to confirm) using the raw tmux send-keys path provided by the `keys` step type.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: one
Reply with exactly the word: two
/tree
Reply with exactly the word: three

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> pre-session entering ready, handing off to the rollout-keyed session in ready
the rollout session runs two working→ready turns (turn 1 'one', turn 2 'two') — the checkpoints to rewind to

after /tree selection, NO transcript_removed and NO transcript_new appear — the rewind is invisible to file-watching (branch() writes nothing at selection time); the session stays ready and on the SAME session_id
the post-rewind turn shows a clean ready → working → ready arc on the SAME rollout session_id (in-place rewind, not a fork) — the 'same session_id if rewound in place' signal from scenario-meanings
exactly one session_id appears across the whole recording (no fork); a single process_exited at teardown
final live state is ready
pi.notes: pi 0.70.0 ships an in-REPL rewind via the /tree slash command: it opens an interactive tree-selector overlay whose handleInput consumes tui.select.up/down + tui.select.confirm; selecting an earlier entry calls session.navigateTree(entryId) → SessionManager.branch(branchFromId), which only moves the in-memory leafId pointer ("existing entries are not modified or deleted") and keeps the SAME append-only .jsonl session file under the SAME session_id — the next turn is appended as a child of the rewound entry. An in-place rewind, like claudecode (NOT a fork like codex). agent_supports=partial: pi restores conversation state but not the working-tree files (no shadow-git in stock pi). The rewind EVENT itself is invisible to file-watch — branch() writes nothing at selection time — so we assert it as a negative invariant over the dwell window between the /tree selection and turn 3. Recipe uses the `keys` step type (raw tmux send-keys) added in commit 88400160 to navigate the /tree picker: send /tree, sleep to let the overlay materialize, `Up Up` to walk back from the latest-highlighted entry to the turn-1 leaf, Enter to commit the rewind. If the live overlay layout differs (oldest-first instead of newest-first), adjust the `Up` count to whatever lands on turn-1. The picker shows newest-first highlighted at the latest entry; `Up Up` from (4/4) `assistant: two` lands on (2/4) `assistant: one` (the turn-1 leaf). The first Enter selects that leaf and pi pops up a `Summarize branch? → No summary / Summarize / Summarize with custom prompt` confirmation dialog; a SECOND Enter accepts the default `No summary` and surfaces `Navigated to selected point`. Only then is it safe to send turn 3 (otherwise the third send is consumed by the dialog).

aider.scope_note: Coverage matrix marks aider supports=partial, observes=no. /undo reverts the last aider git commit and surfaces as a normal chat turn in the transcript; there is no multi-point checkpoint picker. Capturing it requires aider to first make a file edit and commit, then /undo — a recipe shape that needs file-tool granularity the current driver doesn't deterministically produce. Recording deferred.

preconditions: opencode on PATH and authenticated (~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json) — verified opencode 1.14.50. The checkpoint-rewind arc is model-agnostic; the dev env's default is a local LM Studio model, which is fine.
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh isolated mode, or --attach against a daemon started with --record.

LIVE-TUI cell: drive-opencode-interactive.sh routes any recipe carrying a `slash` step to its tmux TUI path, because headless `opencode run` stores a slash command as literal prompt text (PROBED 2026-05-26: `opencode run "/undo"` would store `/undo` as a user message rather than performing the rewind). tmux must be on PATH.
opencode version with /undo support — confirmed v1.14.50 (command.session.undo, slash 'undo'; footer 'Use /undo to revert the last message and file changes').
setup: Driver launches the opencode TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; opencode keys sessions on the cwd in its SQLite store, isolating this run's rows even alongside other opencode use.

opencode mints its own ses_… for the session; all three turns AND the /undo rewind happen IN PLACE on that one ses_… (no fork — unlike codex's /fork). The driver enumerates the cwd's top-level ses_… rows; an in-place rewind leaves exactly one, so the multi-session contract (session.uuids / transcript.paths) carries a single session.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: one
Reply with exactly the word: two
/undo
Reply with exactly the word: three

verify: events.jsonl shows the ses_… session entering ready, then turn 1 (working→ready, 'one') and turn 2 (working→ready, 'two') on the SAME ses_…

after /undo the session stays ready — a rewind is not a turn; opencode sets the session.revert column and restores files via a git snapshot but writes no part rows, so no state_transition distinguishes the rewind from a dwell (assert a ready dwell, not a working/waiting flip)
the message/part rows are NOT deleted (revert is a column flip) — do NOT assert any message/part-count shrink; same ses_… throughout, no new session_id minted (in-place rewind, not a fork)
turn 3 ('three') is a clean ready→working→ready arc on the SAME ses_…, issued from the rewound point
exactly one process_exited on teardown; final live state is ready
opencode.notes: DEGRADED applicable:false again (2026-05-27, opencode v1.14.50, run-cell.sh COEXIST mode on :7838, local gemma-4-e2b model) after TWO consecutive recording attempts reproduced the SAME composer-coalescing defect — even with both prior driver fixes (f2cabf08 terminal step-finish counting + f5b15758 composer-flush confirmation) IN PLACE and live on HEAD. The first two turns serialize cleanly (terminal step-finish 0→1, 1→2; no premature completion), but the `slash` /undo step that follows turn 2 is SWALLOWED ENTIRELY and the turn-2 + turn-3 prompts COALESCE into one user message — both runs' staged transcripts show the identical merged literal 'Reply with exactly the word: twoReply with exactly the word: three' with NO /undo text and only 2 terminal step-finishes, so wait_turn for turn 3 times out (total stuck at 2). PRECISE REMAINING BLOCKER: under this slow local model (turn 1 ~100s to first token), f5b15758's per-send oc_flush capture-pane check is necessary but NOT sufficient across the send→slash→send sequence — the turn-2 send's composer is reported flushed (its turn fires) yet residual composer state lets the subsequent `slash` /undo + turn-3 send merge into the tail of turn 2, dropping /undo. The earlier 'live-verified' probe ended AT the /undo (send→wait_turn→send→wait_turn→slash) with no following turn-3 send, so it never exercised the slash→send hand-off that races here. This is a driver TUI-composer race (oc_send/slash do not reliably isolate the slash step + the turn after it from the prior send under slow-model latency), NOT a missing step type (recipe-lint passes; not a SKILL driver_gap) and NOT a daemon bug (the daemon faithfully recorded the 2 arcs that actually fired). Fix path: harden oc_flush so the `slash` arm and the send immediately AFTER a slash each confirm a SETTLED, empty composer (and that the prior turn's spinner cleared) before typing — e.g. wait for the spinner to vanish AND the composer prefix to clear, with a poll keyed to model latency, so /undo lands on its own and turn 3 is its own submission; then re-record. expected.jsonl stays as the documented in-place-rewind target (state/session_id/arc only — same ses_, revert set, no fork, clean post-rewind turn; never message/part counts).

scenarios.name: cloud-background-agent
scenarios.description: Stub entry — claudecode is a local CLI with no first-party cloud variant, so this scenario is not recordable for that adapter. Forward-looking for Cursor Cloud / Codex Cloud / Copilot Cloud / Devin adapters when they exist.

claudecode.notes: agent_supports=no per coverage matrix — claudecode is local-only. Translator stops here; no recipe.

opencode.notes: agent_supports=no per fresh reassessment — opencode has no cloud/hosted background-agent concept; sessions are a LOCAL ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) keyed to a local opencode process + cwd, so there is nothing PID-less/remote for irrlicht to observe. No recipe; pipeline frozen at Stage 1.

scenarios.name: session-reset
scenarios.description: Claude session runs a no-op turn, then the user invokes /clear. The old session row should disappear from the dashboard and a new session_id should appear in ready. Adapter-specific note: claudecode's /clear creates a new transcript file under the same project slug; the driver's reset_session step auto-detects the new UUID and re-points subsequent turns at it. Coverage notes a known partial caveat (#169 stale PID metadata) — if the daemon retains the old session_id past /clear, this recipe surfaces that as a fail on the old_session_ended phase.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated
irrlichd recording (--attach against --record daemon or isolated mode)
setup: Driver generates a fresh UUID for the first session; the reset_session step discovers the second UUID by scanning the project slug dir after /clear lands.

events.jsonl contains a DIFFERENT UUID session appearing in ready after /clear (new session_id, fresh row)
the second turn's working/ready cycle is on the NEW UUID, not the old one
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 2 user lines and 2 assistant lines (the two transcripts are concatenated by the multi-session curate path)

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.pi/ credentials) — verified pi 0.70.0, which ships the in-REPL `/new` reset (interactive-mode.js:2037 → handleClearCommand → runtimeHost.newSession → session-manager.js:484 newSession()).
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

FULL observability (per assessment.json yes/yes 0.72): pi /new is the codex supersession shape — newSession() mints a fresh createSessionId() UUID and a new <ts>_<uuid2>.jsonl under the same PID (parentSession:null, no UUID-1 history, file flushed lazily on the first post-reset message). The daemon (pi Source = FilesUnderRoot ~/.pi/agent/sessions) detects UUID-2 the instant its file appears: a fresh session_id in ready is the user-observable reset signal. The old UUID-1 row is removed via the #169 same-PID cleanup once UUID-2's PID is discovered (~ms after the new pid_discovered, NOT a readyTTL sweep); the old UUID-1 FILE lingers frozen on disk. No transcript_removed fires at the /new keystroke itself (reset is lazy — UUID-2 materializes on the first post-reset prompt).
setup: Driver launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/.

Pi mints its own session UUID per rollout. The first prompt materializes <ts>_<uuid1>.jsonl; the reset_session step sends pi's in-REPL `/new` (allocating a new driver slot reusing the same tmux/process), and the first prompt AFTER /new materializes a second <ts>_<uuid2>.jsonl with a fresh session_id and no UUID-1 history. The driver re-arms resolve_transcript onto UUID-2 and records BOTH UUIDs into session.uuids / transcript.paths so curate keeps both rollouts.

scenarios.name: session-resume
scenarios.description: Claude session is launched, runs one no-op turn, exits cleanly, then re-launches with the SAME --session-id and same cwd. The daemon should observe the same UUID re-appearing (not a new session row) and the second turn driving ready→working on that same session. Adapter-specific note: claudecode's resume mechanism is implicit — passing the same --session-id <UUID> at relaunch and using the same cwd causes claude to append to the existing transcript at ~/.claude/projects/<slug>/<UUID>.jsonl. The driver's new `resume` step does exactly that.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
no other claudecode session uses the recipe's UUID
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID; the resume step reuses it.
Driver creates one cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and reuses it across both launches — claude caches trust per directory, so the second launch skips the trust dialog naturally.

Reply with exactly the word: again

events.jsonl shows a SECOND pre-session entering ready (proc-<PID2>) after the first lifetime ends
the SAME UUID session_id re-appears in events.jsonl after the resume — NOT a new UUID. This proves the session row persisted across PID lifetimes (spec: 'Same session_id reappears')
second-lifetime working transition fires on the SAME UUID, then back to ready
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 2 user lines and 2 assistant lines — appended across both lifetimes since claude re-opens the existing transcript file

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (codex 0.130.0+ — `codex resume <uuid>` and `codex resume --last` are required; older builds lack the resume subcommand)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
drive-codex-interactive.sh exposes the `exit_clean` and `resume` step types (added in commit 8296cd0)
setup: Codex assigns its OWN session UUID per rollout under ~/.codex/sessions/ — the driver does not pass a preferred UUID; it discovers the live rollout and resumes by that UUID.

The `resume` step Ctrl-D's the running codex (graceful shutdown flushes the rollout → process_exited), kills the tmux session, then relaunches `codex resume <uuid> --no-alt-screen`. Codex APPENDS to the SAME rollout file, so the daemon-side session_id is stable across both lifetimes.
The driver keeps UUID/TRANSCRIPT unchanged across the resume and does NOT append a second session entry — the single-session curator captures both lifetimes because they share one session_id.

the SAME session_id re-binds to a new PID after the resume — NOT a new session_id. This proves codex reopened the existing rollout across PID lifetimes (spec: 'Same session_id reappears')
second-lifetime working transition fires on the SAME session_id, then back to ready
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl carries both turns appended to one rollout — codex resume reopens the same file rather than minting a new one
codex.scope_note: Re-assessed 2026-05-23: the prior applicable:false (driver lacked resume) is STALE. The codex interactive driver now exposes exit_clean + resume (commit 8296cd0), and codex 0.130.0's `codex resume <uuid>` reopens the SAME rollout file (verified empirically) — one session id across two process lifetimes. Same single-session-two-lifetimes shape as claudecode/session-resume, but codex resume is explicit (`codex resume <uuid>`) where claudecode re-passes `--session-id`. How it differs between runs: UUID/PID/timestamps/token counts vary; the structural arc (birth → turn1 working/ready → process_exited → same-id re-bind → turn2 working/ready → final ready) is fixed. If it breaks, check drive-codex-interactive.sh step_resume (UUID resolution + `codex resume <uuid>` relaunch) and confirm codex flushed the rollout on Ctrl-D before relaunch.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (auth set up out-of-band).
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record).
drive-pi-interactive.sh exposes the `exit_clean` and `resume` step types (added in commit e88e35b1).
No other pi session is being driven in the same cwd while this records (DiscoverPID matches a running pi to a session by CWD).
setup: Pi assigns its own session UUID per launch under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/--<cwd>--/ as <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl, created lazily on the first user message. The driver discovers that transcript during wait_turn.

The `resume` step Ctrl-D's the running pi (graceful shutdown flushes the transcript -> daemon emits process_exited), kills the tmux session, then relaunches `pi --session <transcript>` in the SAME cwd. Pi reopens and APPENDS to the same <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl, so the daemon-side session_id is stable across both process lifetimes.

The driver keeps UUID/TRANSCRIPT unchanged across the resume and does NOT allocate a second slot — the single-session curator captures both lifetimes because they share one session_id (session.uuids/transcript.paths each hold one entry).

No separate exit_clean step precedes resume: the resume step performs the clean Ctrl-D exit itself, which is what emits the first-lifetime process_exited. Adding a standalone exit_clean first would tear down the (only) tmux server, so resume's own `tmux send-keys` would then hit a dead server and abort the driver — drop it.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: fresh

verify: events.jsonl shows the first launch's session entering ready, then the working/ready cycle for turn 1.
events.jsonl contains a process_exited event for the session after turn 1 (the Ctrl-D clean exit performed by the resume step).
the SAME session_id re-binds to a new PID after the resume — NOT a new session_id. This proves pi reopened the existing transcript across PID lifetimes (spec: 'Same session_id reappears').
second-lifetime working transition fires on the SAME session_id, then back to ready.
state never enters working between the first-lifetime exit and the second turn (no premature/incorrect waiting state on resume).
final session state is ready.
transcript.jsonl carries both turns appended to one <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl — pi --session reopens the same file rather than minting a new one.
pi.scope_note: Re-assessed 2026-05-25 -> agent_supports:yes / daemon_capability:full / driver_capability:ready (0.78): the prior applicable:false (driver lacked exit_clean + resume) is STALE. The pi interactive driver now exposes exit_clean + resume (commit e88e35b1), and `pi --session <transcript>` reopens and APPENDS to the SAME <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl across two process lifetimes — one session_id, two lifetimes. Same single-session-two-lifetimes shape as claudecode/codex session-resume. How it differs between runs: UUID/PID/timestamps/token counts vary; the structural arc (v1 birth -> turn1 working/ready -> process_exited -> same-id re-bind in ready -> turn2 working/ready -> final ready) is fixed. Pi turns can be slow, hence generous wait_turn/sleep budgets. If it breaks, check drive-pi-interactive.sh step_resume (transcript resolution + `pi --session` relaunch in the same cwd so DiscoverPID re-binds the new PID) and confirm pi flushed the transcript on Ctrl-D before relaunch.

aider.scope_note: Aider has no formal session-id concept — relaunching in the same cwd auto-loads `.aider.chat.history.md`, which is closer to 'append to existing log' than true session resume. The coverage matrix marks aider supports=partial, observes=unknown for this scenario; the recipe is intentionally not authored until the semantics are nailed down.

scenarios.name: long-idle-live-session
scenarios.description: Claude session enters ready, sits idle past readyTTL, and resumes working on the next user prompt. The recording proves irrlicht's PID-liveness check keeps the session alive past the TTL cutoff (the session would otherwise be swept on idle). Note: the production readyTTL is 30 min; this recording uses IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s on the daemon so the assertion 'session stayed past readyTTL' is testable in ~45s instead of 32min. The shortened TTL only affects the recording — production behavior is unchanged.

Daemon's effective readyTTL is 15 s; the idle sleep (25 s) is calibrated to clearly exceed it even after aider's ~5 s idle-flush settle eats into the front of the window — so the clean ready dwell is ~20 s, well past the 15 s TTL

aider.scope_note: Proves aider's PID-liveness keeps a ready session alive PAST readyTTL: aider's PID is discoverable via the /aider command-line match, so the daemon's sweep skips deletion while kill(pid,0)==nil — exactly as for claudecode. Recording uses IRRLICHT_READY_SESSION_TTL=15s so the 'past TTL' assertion is testable in ~45 s. Aider's idle-flush window (~5 s) means the post-turn ready transition lags actual LLM completion; the recipe budgets a 25 s idle sleep (≥20 s, clearly past the 15 s TTL) and a 12 s trailing sleep (gpt-5-nano is fast + aider's ~5 s settle) so each per-turn working→ready settles cleanly and the dwell clearly exceeds the TTL. PROMPT DESIGN (resolved drift): both prompts are PURE-CONVERSATIONAL factual asks (capital of France / Japan) that explicitly forbid file creation/edits and SEARCH/REPLACE blocks — same forbid-edits shape as aider/turn-end-terminal-text. This avoids the earlier 4/7 drift where 'Reply with exactly the word: ok' was non-deterministically interpreted by gpt-5-nano as a file-edit request, producing a post-turn `Applied edit to notes/ok.txt` write that landed ~8 s into the idle dwell and flicked ready→working, failing idle_window. With no file edit, the transcript goes quiet during the dwell so the ≥15 s held-ready window holds.

codex.scope_note: All three variants are recorded back-to-back via drive-codex-interactive.sh: (1) clean Ctrl-D exit after a no-op turn, (2) SIGKILL while idle in ready, (3) SIGKILL mid-turn while still working. restart starts a fresh codex (new rollout, fresh cwd) between variants; sigkill targets the daemon's PID directly (the rollout's lsof write-FD holder, per codex/pid.go) since codex's uniform argv has no per-session marker to pgrep. Variant 3 sleeps 5 s before the kill (vs claudecode's 2 s): codex's ready→working promotion lags ~2.7 s behind first activity, so a 2 s window kills BEFORE working is observed and the variant collapses to ready→process_exited. 5 s clears the promotion latency while staying mid-essay.

Re-recorded from PROMPT mode because headless `pi --print` exits before the post-turn settle and a transient proc-<PID> pre-session row stole session_birth, breaking the same_session_as chain (the C7 anchor-to-start workaround that fixed multi-turn-conversation). SIGKILL-idle / SIGKILL-mid-turn variants stay out of scope — the pi interactive driver exposes send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep only (no sigkill/exit_clean), and natural teardown is the single correctly-modeled variant.

scenarios.name: auto-classified-permission
scenarios.description: Claude Code running in auto mode receives a prompt that triggers a benign tool call (Bash 'echo hello' or Read on a project file) which the in-product auto-classifier approves without firing a permission prompt. The daemon must see ready→working→ready with no transition to waiting at any point. Locks down the safe-path branch of the March 2026 Auto Mode feature; the risky-path branch is owned by tool-gate-permission-prompt.

Driver enables auto mode (Shift+Tab until 'auto mode on' appears in the footer) before the first send

The tool call MUST be one the classifier auto-approves — a benign Bash 'echo' or a Read of a project-local file. Do NOT target .git/.claude/.vscode/.idea/.husky or network-egress shells (those flip to the risky-path scenario)

codex.notes: applicable: n/a (assessment 2026-05-23: agent_supports=partial, irrlicht_observes=no). This scenario's defining signal is a per-call safe-vs-risky classifier that selectively prompts (working→waiting) for risky calls only — primitive permission_hooks, which codex has set false. Codex has a coarse sandbox/approval_policy that auto-runs benign commands and escalates others, but the codex parser exposes no safe-vs-risky classification: its only waiting paths are <proposed_plan>→ExitPlanMode (plan-mode-approval) and a generic open function_call at task_complete (open-tool-call/gate). The daemon therefore cannot observe the selective-gating signal that distinguishes this cell from auto-executed-tool-call. The only codex-observable half (a benign command auto-running with no waiting, ready→working→ready) is already fully captured by the committed codex × auto-executed-tool-call cell; recording it here would be a redundant near-duplicate that proves the wrong thing. No recipe, no recording. See replaydata/agents/codex/scenarios/auto-classified-permission/assessment.json.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated/configured (default model in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json; auth is out-of-band)
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh in isolated/coexist mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

the configured default model must be capable of function calling — if it emits no tool call this cell is unrecordable for a MODEL reason (opencode capabilities.json tool_calls:true), not an opencode/daemon incapability
setup: SAFE branch of opencode's config-driven permission classifier (the RISKY branch — a tool set to "ask" — is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell; it hangs forever headlessly, opencode #14473/#16367, so only the safe branch is recordable on the headless driver)

The recipe `settings` blob is seeded by drive-opencode-interactive.sh as a project-local opencode.json in the per-run cwd BEFORE the run. It pins `permission: {bash:"allow", edit:"allow"}` so the bash echo is config-CLASSIFIED-ALLOWED and auto-executes with NO prompt — this explicit allow policy is what gives the cell distinct permission-classification signal vs auto-executed-tool-call (which pins no policy and relies on the headless default). opencode loads project config before the global ~/.config/opencode config, so the policy applies to this run only and never mutates the user's global config

The driver launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ with NO --model flag (uses the config default); the send is its own subprocess that exits when the turn completes

No approve/deny/classify driver step is needed: the allow policy means the permission prompt never fires, so the existing send|wait_turn|sleep headless path records the safe arc. wait_turn is a no-op because `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends; the ≥4s trailing sleep gives the daemon's poller one full tick to observe the working→ready settle before the run process exits

Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db, ProcessOwnedStore), keyed on the UUID ses_… session; the bash call lands in the part table as a `tool` part (status pending→running→completed) which the parser maps to function_call / function_call_output (parser.go parseToolPart). The ProcessOwnedStore transport has NO waiting/permission source, so the no-waiting invariant holds by construction for the safe branch
The send is a crisp single-tool-forcing prompt so the small/slow local default model reliably emits exactly one auto-classified-allowed bash tool call inside the working span, then replies done

Distinct from full-lifecycle-toolcall, which deliberately exercises the PostToolUse hook to surface waiting; this scenario locks down the auto-approved happy path.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured provider/model (auth is set up out-of-band; the rig's precheck delegates auth to the user). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers the prompt so the file-edit tool call really runs.

setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in .aider.chat.history.md before sending the prompt

aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD, RawLineParser), so the daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session and no handoff/reconcile; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row. The transcript is LAZY (materializes on first input), but the proc-<PID> pre-session is observable from launch (that is where the spec's session_birth ‘ready within 1s’ is grounded)

No auto-mode / permission step is needed and there is no gate to enable: aider runs under --yes-always and has NO permission hook (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so the file-add confirmation auto-answers and the edit applies straight through with no permission window — unlike the claudecode recipe for this cell, which must enable auto mode to keep a Bash call from tripping a gate. The session therefore never enters waiting (parser.go appliedEditRE collapses the edit into a single tool_result; ShouldSynthesizeCollapsedWaiting never fires for an Edit/Bash marker)

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message` (drift fix, same shape as the pi/codex siblings for this coverage_id): aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run can exit promptly after the reply, so the daemon misses the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited and truncates the arc. Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker), so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn; the ≥8s trailing sleep is one full idle-flush cycle plus slack so the working→ready settle is observed before teardown
The send self-contains the fixture: aider is asked to create hello.txt then reply done, forcing exactly one auto-executed file-edit tool call inside the working span
