scenarios.name: background-subagent
scenarios.description: Parent claude session dispatches one or more FIRE-AND-FORGET background subagents via the Agent/Task tool with run_in_background:true, then FINISHES ITS OWN REPLY (acknowledges the launch and reports back) WITHOUT waiting for the children's results. The defining, user-observable shape (scenario-meanings.md#background-subagent, the unified rule): the parent's own reply reaches end_turn while a detached, named child keeps working, yet the SESSION stays working — it does NOT go ready when the parent's reply ends — because reevaluateParent holds it on hasActiveChildren; SubagentCount(parent) INCLUDES the live child (ComputeSubagentSummary); the session transitions working→ready ONLY after the last background child drains. This is the SAME daemon mechanism as foreground-subagent — the ONLY difference is the timing: here the parent end_turn lands BEFORE the child drain, where foreground's parent end_turn lands AFTER. Why this recipe shape: the single send launches three staggered run_in_background:true Explore agents (≈8/14/20s of work each) and instructs the parent to acknowledge immediately and report back without waiting, so the parent's end_turn lands mid-child; the ~18s sleep holds the children fresh UNDER the 30s SubagentQuietWindow so the daemon doesn't fast-forward a still-writing child out of the held-working window; the staggered child runtimes make the subagent-count decrement observable as a sequence rather than a single jump. How it differs between runs: child UUIDs, PIDs, token counts, exact assistant wording, and sub-second timing vary; the structural arc (parent ready→working, ≥1 parent_linked, held working across the child lifetime, parent working→ready only after the last child drains) is invariant. Where to look if it breaks: if validation shows the parent going ready while a child is still alive, that is a held-working daemon regression (session_detector_subagent.go reevaluateParent / hasActiveChildren); if the recording shows the parent blocked until the children finished (parent end_turn AFTER child drain), the prompt let the dispatch run foreground — re-craft the send to force run_in_background:true + report-immediately, do NOT accept it.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key). Verified claude 2.1.156, which exposes the Agent/Task tool's run_in_background:true parameter in the top-level REPL (gated only by CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS / in-process-teammate context, neither set here).
irrlichd is recording — --attach against a daemon started with --record (claudecode hooks POST to a hardcoded :7837, so coexist is refused for claudecode).
claude session can spawn background subagents (default model has the Agent tool with run_in_background exposed).
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (claude's trust dialog appears once and the driver auto-accepts).

No seed file is needed — the background Explore agents run read-only repo-survey tasks against the worktree the driver launches in; their runtime is paced by the prompt (≈8/14/20s) so the held-working window is observable and stays under the 30s SubagentQuietWindow.

script.text: Launch THREE background subagents in a SINGLE message using the Agent tool with subagent_type: Explore and run_in_background: true for every one of them. Agent 1: list every .go file under core/adapters/inbound/agents/ recursively and, before reporting, double-check your count by re-listing each subdirectory one at a time so the work takes at least 8 seconds. Agent 2: find every struct definition under core/domain/session/ and, before reporting, re-read each file a second time to confirm, so the work takes at least 14 seconds. Agent 3: search for every fmt.Errorf call under core/application/ and, before reporting, re-grep each subdirectory individually to verify the count, so the work takes at least 20 seconds. IMPORTANT: these are FIRE-AND-FORGET background agents. Immediately after launching all three in the one message — do NOT wait for any of them to finish, do NOT call BashOutput or read their results — reply with exactly: 'Launched 3 background agents.' Then end your turn. Do not mention or wait for their results.

verify: events.jsonl shows the parent UUID entering ready, then a single ready→working transition driven by the user prompt
transcript.jsonl contains tool_use blocks with name=Agent carrying run_in_background:true (the fire-and-forget Task dispatch; foreground-subagent omits run_in_background)
events.jsonl contains at least 1 parent_linked event pointing at the parent UUID — each background child sidechain transcript under <parent>/subagents/agent-*.jsonl was discovered and bound

the parent's OWN assistant reply reaches end_turn (transcript.jsonl: the parent assistant message with stop_reason end_turn / the 'Launched 3 background agents.' reply) BEFORE any background child transitions working→ready — this is the faithfulness gate distinguishing background from foreground

no state_transition to ready or waiting fires on the parent UUID between the first parent_linked and the final working→ready — the session is HELD working by the live background children even though the parent's own reply already ended

events.jsonl shows the background children transitioning working→ready over a staggered window (the dashboard subagent count steps down rather than dropping in one tick), and only AFTER the last child drains does the parent UUID transition working→ready exactly once
final session state for the parent is ready

opencode.notes: scope_note: opencode subagents are FOREGROUND task-tool children the parent AWAITS — opencode ships the `task` tool (opencode/foreground-subagent is recorded yes/full/ready) but no documented fire-and-forget BACKGROUND / async subagent dispatch (no run_in_background-style task that returns immediately while a detached child runs, no SendMessage-to-background-child primitive). agent_supports=unknown → STOP at Stage 1 (applicable:false), no recipe, no recording; the foreground case is already covered by opencode/foreground-subagent. Secondary blocker: even granting a hypothetical background primitive, the headless driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh, send|wait_turn|sleep over BLOCKING sequential `opencode run`) cannot drive or observe a concurrently-running background child mid-turn. See replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/background-subagent/assessment.json (unknown / n/a / n/a).

scenarios.name: background-process
scenarios.description: Claude runs a Bash command with run_in_background:true (`sleep 8 && echo done`); the tool returns immediately carrying toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId, then the agent polls the process via the BashOutput tool until it reports a terminated status. WHAT it captures: the user-observable arc that PR #450 (issue #445) added — ready → working at the prompt, then the session STAYS working past the turn's own end_turn while the detached shell process is alive, then a single working → ready once the process exits. WHY this exact shape: #450 detects the spawn from the structured backgroundTaskId and gates IsAgentDone on a live `lsof` probe of the tasks/<id>.output file, so the only way to make the daemon observe completion (rather than just letting the turn end) is to drive a BashOutput poll that reports `completed` — there is no async exit hook the tailer can see. The single prompt asks claude to spawn AND poll-to-completion in one turn so the driver's one wait_turn brackets the whole arc. HOW it differs between runs: how many BashOutput polls the model issues, exact token counts, and the precise working dwell length vary with model pacing — the spec's dwell assertion is a ≥4s lower bound (well inside the 8s sleep) so jitter never trips it; the structural arc (one ready→working, one working→ready, single session) is fixed. WHERE to look if it breaks: if the session flips to ready while the process is still alive, the daemon's lsof probe or backgroundTaskId extraction regressed (core/application/services/background_probe.go, core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/parser.go); if the session never returns to ready, the BashOutput-completed parse or the refreshStaleSessions re-probe regressed.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (v2.1.x ships the run_in_background Bash parameter plus the BashOutput and KillShell tools)

irrlichd is recording (--attach or isolated) and built from a tree that includes PR #450 — the daemon must have BackgroundProcessCount tracking and the lsof liveness probe, else it cannot observe the background process at all
lsof is on PATH (the daemon's authoritative background-process liveness check shells out to `lsof -t` on each tasks/<id>.output file)
setup: Driver launches claude in a fresh trusted cwd under <staging>/cwd as usual; no seed files needed — the background process is a self-contained `sleep && echo`.

script.text: Do exactly this and nothing else. (1) Use the Bash tool to run the command `sleep 8 && echo BG_DONE` with run_in_background set to true, so it launches in the background and the tool returns immediately. (2) Then repeatedly use the BashOutput tool to poll that background shell, waiting about 2 seconds between polls, until BashOutput reports the process status as completed. (3) Only after BashOutput shows it completed, reply with exactly the word: done. Do not run any other commands.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new + pid_discovered for the primary session UUID within ~2s of launch (session born in ready)
events.jsonl shows the primary session transition ready -> working when the prompt lands

the primary session stays working for at least ~4s after the turn-start working transition with NO state_transition to ready while the background process is alive — this is the #450 hold-past-end_turn behavior (without #450 the session would flip to ready as soon as the Bash tool_use returns)
transcript.jsonl contains a Bash tool_use whose input has run_in_background:true and a following tool_result whose toolUseResult carries a backgroundTaskId
transcript.jsonl contains one or more BashOutput tool_use blocks, and a BashOutput tool_result whose text reports a terminated status (e.g. <status>completed</status>)
events.jsonl shows exactly one working -> ready transition for the primary session, occurring AFTER the background process exits (not at the bare end_turn)
final session state for the primary session is ready

opencode.notes: applicable: false (daemon_capability=incapable). agent_supports=no — opencode's bash tool runs SYNCHRONOUSLY to completion within the single tool call: there is no run_in_background-style dispatch of a child process that outlives the turn, and no later out-of-band completion event (no BashOutput/KillShell-style poll analogue). daemon_capability=incapable (structural transport gap, NOT a bug): the daemon's entire background-process machinery is bound to the claudecode JSONL path — the parser recognizes a Bash tool_use run_in_background:true + sibling toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId and extracts each tasks/<bash_id>.output path, and background_probe.go's lsof liveness probe (anyLiveOutputWriter, #445/#450) pins the session working over exactly those parser-extracted paths; BackgroundProcessCount/Outputs are populated ONLY by the JSONL-tailer metrics adapter. opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter: its MetricsReader (querySessionMetrics, metrics.go) reads the SQLite store for tokens/tasks/tool-call/cost/context but NEVER sets the background fields, and the store carries no backgroundTaskId/output-file the lsof probe could watch — so a backgrounded-dispatch + later-completion is unobservable for opencode at the transport level. CATALOG DRIFT NOTE: the scenario's requires id `background_process` is one flagged missing from features.json this sweep — recorded here as a note only; features.json and the coverage rollup are maintainer-owned and left untouched. No recipe, no recording; pipeline frozen at Stage 1. UNBLOCK would need BOTH a real opencode backgrounded-process primitive (run_in_background-style dispatch + queryable later completion) AND an opencode/ProcessOwnedStore path that surfaces a liveness signal into BackgroundProcessCount/Outputs. See replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/background-process/assessment.json (no / incapable / n.a.).

scenarios.name: context-compaction
scenarios.description: Claude session runs one normal turn to populate context, then the driver triggers /compact to compact the conversation history, then runs a second normal turn to prove the session continues working after compaction. Recording may be brittle: newer Claude Code (2.1.x+) writes a NEW transcript file at the compaction boundary, which can register as a fresh session row to irrlicht depending on session-stitching (see session-resume cell). Per assessment.json, irrlicht's CompactionState domain field is dead code — the dashboard cannot distinguish compaction from a normal long turn. The recipe therefore asserts only the visible state arc (ready→working→ready on each side of /compact).

exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown; final session state is ready

transcript.jsonl is a SINGLE rollout file (one session id) containing the priming turn (user + assistant/stop), a {type:"compaction",summary} entry, and the post-compaction turn (user + assistant/stop); the compaction-vs-normal-turn distinction is not surfaced by the daemon
pi.notes: Pi difference vs claudecode: pi's /compact appends a single top-level {type:"compaction",summary,...} entry to the SAME rollout file (session-manager.js appendCompaction → _appendEntry on the current leaf — NO newSession()), so the whole scenario stays on ONE session UUID — there is no boundary transcript (unlike claudecode 2.1.x) and no supersession (unlike pi /new). The pi parser (parser.go:55-60) maps the compaction event to EventType:"assistant" (active model work, ready→working), so the daemon sees it as an ordinary working→ready arc on the same session. irrlicht cannot LABEL the turn as compaction (CompactionState is dead code; the parser collapses it into a generic assistant activity), so the recipe asserts only the visible state arc — priming working→ready, a compaction working→ready arc on the same session, post-compaction working→ready, no waiting. DRIVER-SYNC (load-bearing): /compact writes a `compaction` entry, NOT an assistant stopReason==stop message, so drive-pi-interactive.sh's turn-counter does NOT increment for it. A counted wait_turn after /compact would inflate EXPECTED_TURNS by 1 and force a driver timeout (proven by a standalone probe). So the recipe deliberately uses NO wait_turn after /compact: it sleeps 14s over the real ~8s compaction summarization round (separated from the priming ready by a 3s sleep so the compaction registers as its OWN working blip), then issues the post-compaction prompt and ends on a 7s trailing sleep (NO final wait_turn) to flush that turn's working→ready arc and the post-turn ready dwell before the tmux teardown. Only send/slash/wait_turn/sleep are used — all already implemented by the pi driver; no driver extension needed. WHY interactive (not headless pi --print): a slash command only exists inside the REPL; headless pi is single-shot. WHY manual /compact (not auto): automatic compaction fires only on context-window pressure and is not deterministically reproducible in a bounded recording; manual /compact produces the identical {type:"compaction"} entry deterministically. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: session_birth matches the transient proc-<PID> pre-session born at launch; prime_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working (necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working); compaction_start anchors to prime_end so it binds the post-priming working arc; every later phase pins to prime_start via same_session_as. Between runs: assistant wording, the summary text, per-turn/compaction token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ; pi may flush a turn as one OR two bursts — the structural baseline is a priming working→ready arc + a compaction working→ready arc + a post-compaction working→ready arc on one rollout session_id, no waiting, a single process_exited, final ready. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go (eventType=="compaction" → assistant) and whether a pi update changed where /compact writes the compaction entry (e.g. into a new session file).

preconditions: opencode on PATH and authenticated (~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json) — verified opencode 1.14.50. The compaction arc is model-agnostic; the dev env's default is a local LM Studio model (gemma-4-e2b), which is fine but slow (~30-60s/turn).
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh isolated/coexist mode, or --attach against a daemon started with --record.

LIVE-TUI cell: drive-opencode-interactive.sh routes any recipe carrying a `slash` step to its tmux TUI path (run_live), because headless `opencode run` stores a slash command as literal prompt text. /summarize must execute IN the live REPL to run the summarization round. tmux must be on PATH.
opencode version with /summarize manual compaction — confirmed v1.14.50 (command.session.compact + /summarize slash literal + session_compact keybind in the binary).
setup: Driver launches the opencode TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; opencode keys sessions on the cwd in its SQLite store, isolating this run's rows even alongside other opencode use.

opencode mints its own ses_… for the session; both priming turns AND the /summarize compaction round happen IN PLACE on that one ses_… (no new session — distinct from session-reset's /new). The driver enumerates the cwd's top-level ses_… rows; in-place compaction leaves exactly one, so the multi-session contract (session.uuids / transcript.paths) carries a single session.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: one
Reply with exactly the word: two
/summarize

verify: events.jsonl shows the ses_… session entering ready after launch, then two priming turns (working→ready, 'one' then 'two') on the SAME ses_…

after /summarize runs, events.jsonl shows ANOTHER ready→working→ready arc on the SAME ses_… (the summarization round is an in-place LLM turn that lands a step-finish reason=stop part — opencode compacts in place, NO new session)
no state_transition to waiting appears anywhere in the recording
after the compaction arc the session holds ready (no stuck working); final live state is ready
exactly ONE distinct ses_… session_id appears (in-place compaction mints no new session — distinct from session-reset's /new)
opencode.notes: opencode in-place compaction via the /summarize slash command. The whole observable is THREE working→ready arcs on ONE ses_…: two priming turns ('one','two') populate context, then /summarize runs an in-place LLM summarization round that surfaces as a third working→ready arc. opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter — the daemon reads per-turn state from the SQLite store (watcher.go scanParts), and parser.go's parseStepFinish maps the summarization step-finish (reason=stop) to turn_done, so the daemon sees an ordinary working→ready arc on the SAME session. irrlicht's CompactionState field is dead code, so the compaction-vs-normal-turn LABEL is not surfaced — the recipe asserts only the visible state arc. WHY a `slash` step and NOT a `send`: headless `opencode run -- "/summarize"` stores /summarize as literal user prompt text (no command effect); the driver routes any `slash` step to run_live() which delivers it to the live TUI via tmux send-keys -l + Enter (oc_flush composer-clear confirmation). WHY NO wait_turn AFTER /summarize: the driver's `slash` arm does NOT re-snapshot TURN_BASELINE (only `send` does), so a wait_turn after a slash would compare the terminal step-finish total against the SECOND priming turn's baseline — already exceeded — and return immediately (a no-op), never actually waiting for the summarization round. Worse, embedding a send-after-slash re-introduces the slash→send composer-coalescing race that degraded checkpoint-rewind to applicable:false. So /summarize is the LAST input and is followed by a generous 75s trailing sleep — long enough for the slow local model's summarization round to complete (~30-60s) and for the daemon to observe the full working→ready arc plus the post-compaction ready dwell before tmux teardown. WHY manual /summarize (not auto-compaction): auto-compaction fires only on context-window overflow (isOverflow) and is not deterministically reproducible in a bounded recording; manual /summarize runs the identical summarization round deterministically. SINGLE session, no multi-session chain (unlike session-reset's /new): in-place compaction leaves exactly one ses_… for the cwd. What differs between runs: PID/timestamps/token counts, the assistant wording of the priming replies, the summary text, and the per-turn wall-clock latency (~30-60s on the local model). What must NOT differ: three working→ready arcs on one ses_… session (two priming + one compaction), no waiting, ending ready. Where to look if it broke: core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/parser.go (parseStepFinish reason=stop → turn_done) and watcher.go (scanParts step-finish detection); if /summarize stopped producing a terminal step-finish (e.g. an opencode update routed it through CompactionPart without a stop reason), the compaction arc would not register and the cell would need re-assessment.

scenarios.name: foreground-subagent
scenarios.description: Parent claude session uses the Task (Agent) tool to launch one foreground subagent. The subagent reads a small file and reports a result; the parent waits for the subagent to finish, then replies 'done'. Verifies the parent's ready→working→ready arc wraps the subagent's lifetime, the child sidechain transcript materializes under <parent>/subagents/, and the daemon emits parent_linked binding child→parent. Predecessor recipe is the orchestrator-side subagent-spawn (3 parallel children); this scenario locks down the minimal 1-child shape per the agent-level coverage matrix.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
claude session can spawn at least 1 subagent (default model has Task/Agent tool exposed)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (claude's trust dialog appears once and the driver auto-accepts)
A README.md with a single H1 heading is seeded into the cwd so the subagent has a deterministic file to read

script.text: Use the Task tool to launch a general-purpose subagent that reads the README.md in the current directory and reports the first heading. Wait for the subagent to finish, then reply 'done'.

verify: events.jsonl shows the parent UUID entering ready, then a single ready→working transition driven by the user prompt
events.jsonl contains at least 1 parent_linked event pointing at the parent UUID — the child sidechain transcript was discovered and bound
transcript.jsonl contains a tool_use block with name=Agent (the Task tool invocation)
the subagent transcript is present under ~/.claude/projects/<slug>/<parent>/subagents/agent-<uuid>.jsonl
no state_transition to ready or waiting fires on the parent UUID between parent_linked and the final working→ready (parent stays working until the child reports back)
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition for the parent UUID at turn end
final session state for the parent is ready

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the dev env default is a local LM Studio model, so no remote key is needed). Verified opencode 1.14.50.
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated/coexist mode, or --attach against a daemon started with --record
opencode's `task` (subagent) tool is available — capabilities.json declares subagents:true; the built-in `general`/`explore`/`scout` subagents and `@<name>` invocation are on by default
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run --format default -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the parent assistant turn finishes — including the time the parent spends awaiting the foreground subagent — so wait_turn is a no-op
A README.md with a single H1 heading is seeded into the cwd so the subagent has a deterministic, tiny file to read and report back (keeps the child turn short and the recording cheap)

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files. The parent invokes the `task` tool, which calls Session.create(parentSessionID, …); the child session row carries `parent_id = parent.id`. The daemon's ProcessOwnedStore watcher polls the session table, surfaces the child as transcript_new, and the detector emits parent_linked. For opencode the daemon-recorded parent_linked count IS the spawn proof (run-cell.sh:447-450) — NO separate child .jsonl file on disk is required (unlike claudecode's <parent>/subagents/ probe)

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s/turn), and a subagent run is two turns deep (parent + child), so the trailing sleep and timeout are generous: a slow parent+child sequence must not trip a false timeout, and the trailing sleep must outlast the parent's working→ready settle after the child returns

script.text: Use the task tool to launch a subagent (the built-in 'explore' agent) that reads the README.md file in the current directory and reports its first heading. Wait for that subagent to finish, then reply with exactly the word: done.

verify: events.jsonl shows the parent ses_… session entering ready, then a single ready→working transition driven by the user prompt

events.jsonl contains at least 1 parent_linked event whose parent_session_id is the parent ses_… UUID — the child session row (parent_id set) was discovered by the SQLite watcher and bound to the parent (this is opencode's spawn proof; no child .jsonl is expected on disk)
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the child session born after the parent turn started

no state_transition to ready or waiting fires on the parent ses_… between parent_linked and the final working→ready (the parent's outstanding `task` tool part holds it in working until the child reports back)
the child session transitions working→ready when its read/report work completes (dashboard subagent count drops 1 → 0)
events.jsonl shows a working→ready transition for the parent ses_… at turn end, after the child finishes
final session state for the parent is ready

scenarios.name: long-agentic-session-stress
scenarios.description: Compressed proxy for a real long session: 4 plain user prompts in one session. Spec asserts per-turn ready→working→ready arcs with no flicker WITHIN a turn, no stuck state. Tool calls were originally part of the recipe but the modern claudecode default (Sonnet 4.6 + high effort) refuses auto-mode for paid models, which stalls tool-call turns at the permission gate. Plain prompts keep the recording deterministic; the no-flicker-within-turn invariant is still meaningful because each turn still has multiple intermediate assistant chunks even without tool_use.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)

Claude Code launched in auto mode (Read/Bash/Write pre-approved) so no turn trips a permission gate — otherwise state would legitimately enter waiting and the no-flicker invariant would need to allow it
cwd under <staging>/cwd/ contains a CHANGELOG.md file (driver writes a stub if not present)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/
Driver seeds CHANGELOG.md in cwd with a small fixture (e.g. '## 0.4.0 - 2026-05-01\n- initial') so turn 1's Read finds something
Auto mode is enabled by the driver (Shift+Tab until 'auto mode on' appears in the footer) so Bash/Read/Write are auto-classified

Reply with exactly the word: beta
Reply with exactly the word: gamma
Reply with exactly the word: delta

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
for EACH of the 4 turns: exactly one ready→working transition, at least one tool_use event inside the working span, exactly one working→ready transition at turn end
across the entire session, NO state_transition to ready fires inside any turn's working span (no flicker between failing/succeeding tool_use and the next tool_use inside the same turn)
across the entire session, NO state_transition to waiting fires (auto mode means no permission gate)
final session state is ready

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver launches `codex --no-alt-screen` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits for the TUI banner + MCP boot before sending the prompt

No auto-mode step is needed: codex has no permission gate (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so the shell tool runs every command autonomously inside codex's sandbox — this is exactly why codex can capture the REAL many-tool-call single-turn burst, whereas the claudecode recipe for this scenario must fall back to 4 plain prompts (modern claudecode refuses auto-mode for paid models and stalls tool-call turns at a permission gate)

The prompt is fully self-contained — it tells codex to CREATE its own files and then run many commands on them, so the per-run cwd starting empty doesn't shrink the burst (an early attempt that only said 'ls then wc the existing files' produced just 2 calls because the fresh cwd had nothing to wc)

wait_turn blocks until a new event_msg/task_complete line appears in the rollout, so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn; the timeout is generous because this is a deliberately LONG turn

script.text:
Do all of the following in THIS single turn, using your shell tool, running each as a SEPARATE command one at a time (do not combine them, do not write a script, do not ask me anything): 1) run `pwd`; 2) create three files with `printf 'line1
line2
line3
' > a.txt`, then `printf 'x
y
' > b.txt`, then `seq 1 20 > c.txt` (three separate commands); 3) run `ls -la`; 4) run `wc -l a.txt`, then `wc -l b.txt`, then `wc -l c.txt` (three separate commands); 5) run `cat a.txt`; 6) run `head -3 c.txt`; then finally summarize in one short paragraph how many lines each file had. Run every numbered command individually so I can see each one execute.

transcript.jsonl contains MANY function_call / function_call_output pairs in the single turn (at least 5: pwd, ls -la, and ≥3 wc -l calls) — the high-volume burst
across the entire turn, NO state_transition to ready fires between successive function_call/function_call_output pairs (one contiguous working span, no flicker mid-burst)
across the entire turn, NO state_transition to waiting fires (codex has no permission gate; every command auto-executes)

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured model out-of-band)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs pi in a tmux REPL so it stays alive across all four turns
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

PARTIAL caveat (mirrored from coverage): if pi fires context compaction during the rollout, the compaction summary interleaves as assistant content and may add an extra working→ready→working flicker that is NOT a user turn boundary — compaction is not deterministic on a 4-turn proxy run, so the spec asserts the first four clean arcs and no waiting, not an exact arc count
setup: The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ inside a tmux session, waits for the REPL 'Ready' line, then sends each prompt; it keeps ONE rollout alive across all four turns and kills the tmux session exactly once at the end → a single process_exited

pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy); the driver resolves that file and extracts the UUID from line 1 (pi assigns its own UUID; it can't be pre-set)

WHY tool calls are KEPT here (unlike the claudecode sibling, which had to drop them): pi auto-executes its bash tool with NO permission gate, so the tool-call turns never stall in waiting. The four prompts each elicit a real bash tool call against a file the session builds for itself (create → list → grep → read back), exercising interleaved assistant+tool_use+tool_result content across a long rollout — the actual stress this scenario names

WHY interactive (not headless `pi --print`): headless pi is single-shot and exits after one reply, so it can't build a long multi-turn rollout; keeping pi alive in the REPL across four send+wait_turn cycles lets the daemon observe four clean working→ready settles on one session

WHY the 3s sleeps between wait_turn and the next send: they sit past the classifier's debounce window so each turn's working→ready flip is observed cleanly before the next prompt re-enters working; without them the next send can land before the prior turn settles and the arcs blur. The trailing 5s sleep lets the final settle land before the single tmux teardown

banana

Using your bash tool, run: ls -la — then reply with exactly: listed

events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the rollout session_id
for EACH of the four turns: exactly one state_transition→working / →ready arc on the rollout session_id, sustained across the auto-executed bash call (four clean arcs in order on one session)

transcript.jsonl contains four user lines and, across the rollout, four toolCall / toolResult pairs (the auto-executed bash calls: printf create, ls, grep, cat) — pi has no permission hook so each runs ungated
across the entire rollout, NO state_transition to waiting fires for the rollout session at any point (every tool call auto-executes; pi never gates)
the rollout session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at the single tmux teardown (not four — the REPL is long-lived)

ACCEPT-EITHER: if pi fires compaction mid-rollout an extra working→ready→working arc may appear; the recording still passes because the spec asserts the first four clean arcs and no waiting, not an exact arc count

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs ~5min on the OpenRouter OAuth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers each prompt and applies each edit. THIS CELL'S RECORDING IS DEFERRED: no provider key was present at authoring time, so the spec is committed as the documented target and the recording is captured once aider auth exists in-env.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it can send four prompts to one long-lived session
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

aider runs under --yes-always (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so every file-add / shell confirmation auto-answers and no tool-call turn stalls at a permission gate — without this, state would legitimately enter waiting and the no-waiting invariant would break
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the first prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears

Each send asks aider to make one small file edit; under --yes-always the edit auto-applies and the parser emits a collapsed tool_result from the `> Applied edit to <file>` marker. wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears (signals aider finished one model call). aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector poll (~5s effective latency)
After the fourth wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session

script.text: Create a file notes.txt containing exactly the line: alpha
Append a line to notes.txt containing exactly: beta
Append a line to notes.txt containing exactly: gamma
Append a line to notes.txt containing exactly: delta

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the live aider session_id

events.jsonl contains FOUR state_transition→working / →ready arcs on the SAME session_id, one per prompt — the per-turn working→ready flip is synthesized by IdleFlush and must be observed before the next prompt re-enters working (this is why the inter-turn sleeps are ≥12s, covering the ~5s effective latency with ample slack since fast models like gpt-5-nano otherwise outrun the idle window and coalesce turns; with shorter sleeps the next #### prompt opens a new turn with no intervening turn_done and the per-turn reset is lost — and the risk COMPOUNDS over four turns, the matrix's observes:partial caveat)

WITHIN each turn, state stays continuously working across the interleaved assistant fragments and the edit tool_result — NO state_transition to ready fires mid-turn (no flicker between the model fragments and the `> Applied edit` marker and the turn's end)

transcript.jsonl contains four user lines and at least four collapsed tool_result events (one `> Applied edit to notes.txt` per turn) — the contains_tool_call signal; the phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the tool call is asserted only via the working span it sustains
across the entire session, NO state_transition to waiting appears (aider runs under --yes-always, so every edit auto-applies and never gates)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session, not four exits)

per-turn token contribution (TotalTokens) accumulates monotonically from each `> Tokens:` snapshot — observable in the parser's assistant_message events but NOT expressible in the expected.jsonl phase DSL (no metric form); aider's cost_tracking is false so no provider dollar cost is carried

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured default model (~/.config/opencode/opencode.json; the local LM Studio model lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored at authoring time). The driver passes NO --model, so it uses that default
opencode's SQLite store exists at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db (created on first run); the driver queries it for the ses_… session id and exports the parts transcript
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: RUN_LIVE — the leading {"type":"live"} marker forces drive-opencode-interactive.sh onto its run_live path (router engages on any of reset_session/slash/interrupt/keys/restart/sigkill/mid_turn_send/live). run_live launches ONE opencode TUI under tmux in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and keeps that SAME process alive across ALL the sequential send/wait_turn turns; opencode auto-assigns the ses_… session id (no CLI-assignable id) and the driver discovers it from the SQLite session table keyed on that cwd

WHY run_live (not headless `opencode run`): headless spawns a FRESH subprocess per send that EXITS ~140–175ms before the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore poller reads its terminal `step-finish reason=stop`, so the final working→ready settle lands in the store AFTER process_exited and is missed (issue #492 — a DRIVER process-exit-before-poll race, NOT a daemon bug). A long-lived run_live process never exits at turn end, so the poller reads the terminal step-finish while the session is alive and the FINAL settle lands normally — this is the deliberate fix that lets the cell drop its prior known_failing

WHY a genuinely LONG multi-turn arc: the scenario stresses the daemon keeping ONE session coherently observed across MANY interleaved assistant-message / tool-call / tool-result iterations. Each of the six sequential turns gives opencode a small, fully-enumerated file task (create / write / read-back / list / append / summarize) so its agent loop chains real write/read/list tool calls, and the turns accumulate work across the run — a high tool-call volume and monotonic token accumulation across the whole session
WHY tool calls auto-execute without a gate: opencode runs its file/list tools inside its own sandbox with NO interactive permission prompt, so no turn enters waiting

WHY each turn is send → wait_turn → sleep: wait_turn blocks on run_live until the terminal step-finish total exceeds the pre-send baseline (the turn actually ended), and the inter-turn sleeps (5s) let each working→ready settle land before the next send re-enters working. The local gemma 2B default model is slow (~30–45s per turn) so the timeout is generous (900s). NOTE: the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore watcher observes the store via WAL-write-triggered scans with a 500ms minScanGap debounce, so back-to-back same-ses_ turns may COALESCE into one observed working→ready span — the spec asserts the SESSION-LEVEL arc + FINAL settle, not N separable per-turn arcs

script.text: Using your file tools, do all of this in THIS turn, one step at a time (do not ask me anything): create six files named step1.txt, step2.txt, step3.txt, step4.txt, step5.txt, step6.txt, and into each write exactly its step number on a single line (step1.txt contains 1, …, step6.txt contains 6). Then reply with exactly: created.
Using your file tools, read all six files step1.txt through step6.txt back and tell me the number each one contains. Then reply with exactly: read-back-done.

Using your file tools, list every file in the current directory, then create a file summary.md that lists one per line each stepN.txt filename followed by the number it contains. Then reply with exactly: summary-written.
Using your file tools, append a new line 'total=21' to summary.md, then read summary.md back and show me its full contents. Then reply with exactly: total-appended.
Using your file tools, create three more files notes1.txt, notes2.txt, notes3.txt, each containing a single line with its own name, then read all three back. Then reply with exactly: notes-done.
Using your file tools, list every file in the current directory one final time and tell me the total count of files you see. Then reply with exactly: all-done.

scenarios.name: mid-turn-message-queued
scenarios.description: Claude is given a slow count-to-10 prompt; while the agent is mid-turn, the driver types a follow-up message into the TUI which claudecode queues until a safe point. The daemon must see a single contiguous working span with no flicker to ready when the queued input lands, and the cycle must still end at ready once everything is consumed. Locks down the parser's transcript-driven state classification against a future regression where queued text appears as a synthetic user-line before consumption.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
Claude Code launched in auto mode so neither prompt trips a permission gate
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/
The first prompt must keep claude busy long enough (a few seconds) to type a follow-up into the TUI — a slow count-to-10 with explicit pauses is sufficient
The mid-turn follow-up is delivered via tmux send-keys ending in Enter; claudecode's TUI buffers the text and consumes it at the next safe point (post-tool boundary or turn end)

script.text: Count from 1 to 10, one number per line, with a brief pause between each. Then on the last line reply with exactly the word: done

script.keys: f o l l o w u p Space q u e u e d Enter

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID entering ready, then a ready→working transition for the first prompt
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready between the first ready→working and the queued-message consumption (the working span must be contiguous through the queued arrival)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point during the working span
events.jsonl eventually shows a working→ready transition; final session state is ready

transcript.jsonl contains at least 1 user line for the initial prompt; the queued follow-up may appear as a second user line (consumed as a new turn) or be folded into the first turn — either shape is acceptable per spec

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver launches `codex --no-alt-screen` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits for the TUI banner + MCP boot before sending the prompt (boot itself takes ~15-20s)

The first prompt keeps codex busy long enough to inject a follow-up while it is mid-turn — a slow count-to-20 that asks for a pause between numbers makes codex run a `sleep 0.5` shell call per number, extending the working turn to ~50-60s, giving a wide window to land the follow-up mid-turn

The mid-turn follow-up is delivered via raw `keys` steps, NOT a `send`: probing codex 0.130.0 live, a message submitted mid-turn is QUEUED — the TUI shows 'Messages to be submitted after next tool call' and folds it into the SAME turn, emitting only ONE task_complete for the whole arc. A `send` would bump the driver's expected-turn counter to 2, so the following wait_turn would block forever on a second task_complete that never arrives; raw `keys` submit the buffered text without touching the turn counter

The follow-up is split into TWO keys steps with a sleep between — first the literal text (no Enter), then a separate Enter — because codex's Ink input handler drops a batched text+Enter (the same render race step_send mitigates with a 0.3s pause): typing then pausing then pressing Enter lets the composer render the text before submit so the queue actually accepts it

The pre-injection sleep is 8s so the follow-up lands solidly inside the working turn (well after the ~50s+ count begins) rather than during boot, where it would be folded into the initial prompt and produce no observable queue

script.text: Count slowly from 1 to 20, one number per line. Pause about half a second between each number. After 20, reply with exactly the word: done

events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the rollout session with a distinct session_id, born ready, and a pid_discovered bound to it
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the rollout session_id when the slow-count prompt is sent

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready while the queued follow-up is buffered (the working span is contiguous through the queued arrival — codex folds it into the same turn, one task_complete)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point during the working span
events.jsonl eventually shows a single working→ready transition once the folded turn completes; final session state is ready, then process_exited at REPL teardown
transcript (rollout) shows ONE task_started/task_complete pair and TWO user_message lines (initial prompt + queued follow-up); the queued message does NOT introduce a second turn boundary

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured model out-of-band)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs pi in a tmux REPL so a follow-up can be typed while the first turn is still in flight
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

PARTIAL caveat (mirrored from the coverage assessment): pi queues the mid-turn follow-up (Enter steers/queues) and consumes it immediately after the in-flight turn — the queued user line is written to the transcript within ~1ms of the first turn's stopReason==stop and the next turn begins at once, so the daemon never observes an intervening ready and the two back-to-back transcript turns COLLAPSE into ONE contiguous working span (the canonical observable, same shape claudecode/codex produce). It is PARTIAL rather than yes because this single-span observability hinges on that sub-ms queue→consume timing; if pi's consumption were delayed past the classifier's debounce window the queued message could instead surface as a separate working→ready arc.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt
pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on first user input (FilesUnderRoot); the driver resolves that file and extracts the UUID from line 1

QUEUE MECHANICS via the driver's existing primitives (no new step type): the first send bumps the expected-turn counter to 1 and starts a slow count-to-10 that keeps pi busy for several seconds; a short sleep (NOT wait_turn) keeps the turn in flight; a SECOND send then types the follow-up while pi is still mid-turn — pi's REPL queues the buffered line (Enter steers/queues) and the second send bumps the expected-turn counter to 2; a single final wait_turn then blocks until the rollout shows ≥2 completed assistant turns (the in-flight turn THEN the queued message's turn)

WHY two sends rather than a `keys` injection: pi's interactive driver implements send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but not `keys`; pi consumes a queued message as a full extra turn (unlike codex which folds it into one task_complete), so two sends with one final wait_turn is the natural, bookkeeping-correct expression — the EXPECTED_TURNS counter resolves at count≥2
After the final wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — pi exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the rollout session

script.text: Count slowly from 1 to 10, one number per line, with a brief pause between each. After 10, reply with exactly the word: done
Also, after you finish counting, reply with exactly the word: followup

events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the rollout session_id

events.jsonl shows a single CONTIGUOUS working span on the rollout session: NO state_transition to ready between the first ready→working and the cycle's completion, even though the follow-up was typed (queued) mid-turn — pi folds the queued turn in within ~1ms of the first turn's stop so no intervening ready is observed
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point on the rollout session
the rollout session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down (the cycle ends at ready once both the count and the queued follow-up are consumed)
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session)

transcript.jsonl contains at least two user lines (initial prompt + queued follow-up) and two assistant stopReason==stop lines; the queued user line introduces no turn boundary itself — only stopReason==stop flips state to ready, so the queued arrival cannot inject a synthetic working→ready flicker

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (the dev env default is a local LM Studio model — no remote key needed). Verified opencode 1.14.50.
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled (run-cell.sh in isolated/coexist mode, or --attach against a daemon started with --record)
the local model must be loaded with a context window large enough to complete a multi-paragraph turn without an n_keep>=n_ctx overflow (the dev env uses gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored at 32768 context)
setup: This cell uses the live-TUI path (run_live): the mid_turn_send step routes drive-opencode-interactive.sh into the long-lived tmux opencode process instead of a blocking headless `opencode run`. A blocking run would complete turn1 before a second message could fire, so there would be no in-flight turn to queue against. The driver launches one `opencode` under tmux and holds it alive across both turns.

The first prompt asks for a ~400-word explanation, which keeps the ses_… turn IN FLIGHT (working) for ~30-45s on the slow local LM Studio model — a wide window to land the mid-turn message. The pre-injection sleep (12s) lands the second message solidly inside that working window (well after the step-start, before any terminal step-finish).

mid_turn_send types the SECOND message + Enter into the composer WITHOUT bumping the driver's turn baseline (oc_mid_turn_send): opencode silently QUEUES the message — its documented default (issues #21388/#17691/#12707) — holding it in the runtime/UI queue and NOT writing it to the SQLite part/message tables until consumed. So during the in-flight + injection window the daemon's last store event stays a turn-internal assistant_message/function_call and the session stays working with no →ready or →waiting flicker.

Two wait_turn steps follow the injection: the first rides the slow-prompt baseline and detects turn1's terminal step-finish (reason=stop); the second detects the QUEUED message running as turn2 (its own working→ready arc). opencode consumes the queued message as a NEW turn — a step-finish reason=stop closes turn1, then turn2 begins — which is legal per the claudecode anchor's note (fold-vs-new-turn both acceptable; only the no-flicker-on-arrival window plus the final ready are asserted).

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db); the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session/part tables and classifies the ses_… session on the LAST part's event type. The run_live epilogue enumerates the top-level ses_… under the run cwd and stages its transcript after the TUI is dropped. The trailing sleep (8s) outlasts turn2's working→ready settle so the final ready is observed before daemon shutdown.

Now also list three fun facts about giraffes.

verify: events.jsonl shows the ses_… session entering ready, then a single ready→working transition driven by the slow first prompt

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready in the window from turn1's ready→working through the queued-message arrival (the working span is contiguous through the injection — opencode holds the queued message in its runtime queue, unwritten to SQLite, so the daemon's last store event stays turn-internal)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point during the working span (no permission gate; the queued message does not flip the session to waiting on arrival)

events.jsonl eventually shows a working→ready transition on the same ses_… and the final session state is ready — the queued message runs and the cycle closes. The transcript carries TWO step-finish parts (turn1's tool turn + turn2's giraffe-facts answer) proving the queued message was consumed; whether the daemon surfaces that as a second working→ready arc OR coalesces both turns into one contiguous working span (the inter-turn step-finish→step-start seam debounced, no settled ready between them — observed in this recording) is both legal per the anchor (fold-vs-new-turn). Only the no-flicker-on-arrival window plus the final ready are asserted.
the recording contains exactly ONE top-level ses_… session — a single mid-turn injection adds a queued turn on the one session, not a new session

scenarios.name: model-identification

The state arc is the basic-turn arc; the assertion lives in events.jsonl payload.

Assertions on ModelName/ContextWindow are checked AFTER turn_end — ContextWindow may briefly read 0 between session detection and the first assistant event (per assessment caveat)

In codex 0.130.0 the model lives on per-turn turn_context.payload.model (NOT on token_count/usage events — that was the issue #361 regression, since fixed); extractCodexMetadata (parser.go:263-278) reads payload.model and model_context_window, and the tailer applies ModelName via applyModelMetadata even though turn_context is Skip=true. The model assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (no model field in the validator Phase struct), so expected.jsonl covers only the basic-turn lifecycle skeleton and the model presence is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden, which replay-fixtures.sh diffs. What differs between runs: the concrete model_name (account boot default, env-dependent per #440), token counts/cost, rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with 'Reply with exactly the word: ok'). What must NOT differ: one working→ready arc on one rollout session, a non-empty model_name in the golden, and process_exited at teardown.

pi.notes: Thin/near-trivial cell: structurally identical to pi/basic-turn (one send + wait_turn + trailing sleep) — the only extra intent over basic-turn is that the daemon extracted a model_name. WHY interactive (not headless pi --print): per concern C6 the headless single-shot driver exits too fast and drifts; the pi interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) keeps the rollout REPL alive via send+wait_turn and kills the tmux session once at the end. The model lives on each assistant message.model (parser.go L104) — and on model_change events at modelId (L41) — which the pi parser pulls through tailer.NormalizeModelName into ev.ModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName from turn 1. The model assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (no model field in the validator Phase struct), so expected.jsonl covers only the basic-turn lifecycle skeleton and the model presence is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (which replay-fixtures.sh diffs) plus the pi parser unit tests. C7/SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; session_birth (which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s') legitimately matches it. turn_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working in the whole recording (necessarily the rollout session — the pre-session never goes working); turn_end, pid_bind, and teardown then pin to turn_start's session via same_session_as. What differs between runs: the concrete model_name (boot default, env-dependent per #440), token counts/cost, rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with 'Reply with exactly the word: ok'). What must NOT differ: one working→ready arc on one rollout session, a non-empty model_name in the golden, and a single process_exited at teardown.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a provider out-of-band; the driver does not handle a login/OAuth prompt (headless aider hangs ~5min on OpenRouter OAuth if no key is present).
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it can send one prompt to a long-lived session and tear it down cleanly.
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

the launch model is reachable on the account AND present in the LiteLLM capacity table so ContextWindow resolves to the published window. Set IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL to a known model name (e.g. a mainstream claude-* / gpt-* the alias table covers) for a deterministic ModelName and a non-zero ContextWindow; for a model absent from LiteLLM, ModelName is still correct but ContextWindow resolves to 0 / ContextWindowUnknown=true (assessment caveat 1).

feature-invisible caveat: aider's `> Weak model:` line is intentionally NOT matched by the parser's modelRE (only `Model:`/`Main model:` drive ModelName), so the weak/summary model is never the asserted ModelName; this recipe runs on the MAIN model, which is what aider writes as `> Model:` on the first assistant block.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) launches aider in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session against the configured main model (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL) and waits for the REPL prompt.

.aider.chat.history.md (FilesUnderCWD) materializes lazily once the first user input lands; before that the live REPL is observable as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner). The daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session.

On the first assistant block aider writes `> Model: <name> with <edit format>`; the parser's modelRE captures the name → NormalizeModelName → a Skip event carrying ModelName, which applyModelMetadata stamps onto SessionMetrics.ModelName from turn one (never 'unknown' for a model aider names).

ContextWindow is NOT in aider's transcript — it is derived entirely from the capacity manager's LiteLLM alias-table fallback (ComputeContextUtilization → capacityMgr.GetModelCapacity(modelName)); a LiteLLM-known launch model yields the published window with ContextWindowUnknown=false.
Assertions on ModelName/ContextWindow are checked AFTER turn_end — ContextWindow may briefly read 0 between session detection and the first assistant block (per assessment caveat).
After the turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the proc-<PID> session.

script.text: What is the capital of France? Answer in one word, no file edits.

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> session born ready within 2s of launch (the lazy transcript has not materialized yet).
events.jsonl shows ONE ready→working→ready arc on that proc-<PID> session_id (the single no-op turn), with no state_transition to waiting.
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered bound to the proc-<PID> session, and exactly one process_exited on it at REPL teardown (a single-turn session does NOT mint a second session).

the .replay.json.golden carries a non-empty summary.model_name equal to the normalized launch model (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL), NOT 'unknown'; this is where model identification is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value is environment-dependent (issue #440); assert presence + non-'unknown', and for a LiteLLM-known model a populated (non-zero) ContextWindow.
SessionMetrics.ContextWindowUnknown is false after turn_end when the launch model is in the LiteLLM table (per assessment caveat 1; otherwise ContextWindow reads 0 while ModelName stays correct).
transcript (.aider.chat.history.md) contains a `> Model: <name>` blockquote on the first assistant block and ≥1 `> Tokens:` close.
final session state is ready before teardown.
aider.notes: Single no-op turn in ONE live aider REPL launched against a specific main model (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL): captures that the dashboard shows the launch model (ModelName != 'unknown') with a populated ContextWindow, not 'unknown'. TEMPLATED FROM claudecode/model-identification (same single send + wait_turn + trailing sleep shape) and adapted to aider's transport. RECORDABILITY (no driver_gap): the recipe needs only send/wait_turn/sleep — the aider interactive driver implements all three (case `send|slash)`, `wait_turn)`, `sleep)`), so there is no missing step type. WHY interactive (not headless `aider --message`): headless aider is single-shot and exits after one reply — on a short run the process can exit before the daemon classifies the working→ready settle and before the PID scanner ticks, truncating turn_end/teardown; the interactive driver keeps the REPL alive across the turn, then kills the tmux session once → a SINGLE clean process_exited. MODEL/CONTEXT ASSERTION IS OUT-OF-BAND: the events.jsonl phase DSL carries no model field, so expected.jsonl asserts only the basic-turn lifecycle skeleton (one ready→working→ready arc on one proc-<PID> session, a single process_exited, no waiting, final ready); the two user-observable signals (ModelName == launch model, ContextWindow == published window) are asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden plus the aider parser unit tests for the `> Model:`/`> Main model:` blockquote. SESSION IDENTITY: aider stores its transcript in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD), so the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> — no separate UUID rollout session and no handoff phase; the turn arc, pid_bind, and teardown all pin to session_birth via same_session_as, while session_birth itself is anchored to start UNPINNED so the transient proc-<PID> birth can't steal session identity. LAZY TRANSCRIPT: .aider.chat.history.md materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as the proc-<PID> pre-session born ready — session_birth matches that. AIDER TURN-END IS SYNTHESIZED, NOT MARKED: the markdown transcript has no turn-end marker — `> Tokens:` closes one model CALL, not the turn; end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after ~1.5s quiet, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll, so the EFFECTIVE working→ready latency is ~5s; the trailing 9s sleep (≥8s) lets the turn's working→ready flip fire AND be observed before daemon shutdown (a shorter tail would miss the terminal ready). PROMPT: a pure conversational question (`What is the capital of France? Answer in one word, no file edits.`) — gpt-5-nano writes `> Model: gpt-5-nano` on the first assistant block regardless of whether it edits, and a non-edit prompt avoids the post-turn `Applied edit` write that re-triggers transcript_activity inside the idle dwell (the long-idle cell's known gpt-5-nano edit-flicker). What differs between runs: the concrete model value (set IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL for a deterministic, LiteLLM-known name; otherwise issue #440 — assert presence, not a hardcoded string), token counts, PID/timestamps, assistant wording, and the working→ready edge lands with ~5s idle-flush latency. What must NOT differ: one working→ready arc on ONE proc-<PID> session, a non-empty summary.model_name == the launch model in the golden, and a single process_exited at teardown. multi_model_orchestration=yes; headless_mode=yes (the scenario's requires:). RECORDING DEFERRED: validated when aider auth is available in-env — at authoring time there is no provider key present and headless aider hangs on OpenRouter OAuth, so this recipe + spec are the DOCUMENTED TARGET pending a recording.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the worktree default is a local LM Studio model so no remote key is needed)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

the configured default model in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json is the one whose name the dashboard should report; the driver passes NO --model flag, so whatever default is set is what gets asserted as ModelName (not 'unknown')
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the assistant turn finishes, so wait_turn is a no-op

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns (opencode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs)

opencode records the active model on each message under message.data.model = {providerID, modelID}; the metrics path reads modelID (fallback top-level modelID) → tailer.NormalizeModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName, and ComputeContextUtilization derives ContextWindow from that model. The scenario is about the daemon extracting that name, not about which model produced the tokens

The send is a no-tool single turn — opencode is asked to reply with exactly one word, so the working span carries no auto-executed tool call (tool_calls_max: 0) and the session never enters waiting (no permission gate fires)

ModelName/ContextWindow are checked AFTER turn_done — ContextWindow may briefly read 0 between session detection and the first assistant event (per assessment caveat); the default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleep and 180s timeout are generous so a slow turn must not trip a false timeout and the trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so working→ready is observed

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl shows one ready→working→ready cycle on the ses_… session_id (the single assistant turn); no state_transition to waiting appears (no tool call, no permission gate)

the .replay.json.golden carries a non-empty summary.model_name (NOT 'unknown') — opencode's metrics path pulled it from message.data.model.modelID and normalized it. This is where model identification is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value follows the configured default (this worktree: the local LM Studio model normalizing to 'gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored'); assert presence + non-'unknown'
the session's final live state is ready
opencode.notes: Single no-op turn in one headless opencode session: captures that the dashboard shows the configured default model (ModelName != 'unknown') instead of 'unknown'. TEMPLATED FROM opencode/basic-turn (same single send + wait_turn + trailing sleep shape) — the only extra intent over basic-turn is that the daemon extracted a model_name. RECORDABILITY (no driver_gap): the recipe needs only send/wait_turn/sleep, all implemented by drive-opencode-interactive.sh. WHY a blocking `opencode run` (not a long-lived REPL with teardown, unlike codex/pi): opencode's driver launches a fresh blocking `opencode run` per send and chains --session; the run already blocks until the turn completes, so wait_turn is a no-op and there is no in-process REPL to tear down — hence no teardown/process_exited phase (the opencode sibling specs basic-turn and turn-end-terminal-text omit it for the same reason). WHERE THE MODEL CLAIM LIVES: opencode stores model = {providerID, modelID} in its SQLite store; metrics.go reads modelID → NormalizeModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName, and ComputeContextUtilization derives ContextWindow. The model assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (the validator Phase struct carries no model field), so expected.jsonl asserts only the basic-turn lifecycle skeleton (one ready→working→ready arc on one ses_… session, no waiting, final ready); the user-observable model signal is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (which replay-fixtures.sh diffs) plus the opencode metrics unit tests. SESSION IDENTITY: session_birth is anchored to recording start UNPINNED so a transient proc-<PID> pre-session can't steal identity; turn1_start (matching working) establishes the ses_… session identity that turn1_done chains off via same_session_as. MODEL note: the driver passes NO --model flag, so whatever default is configured in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json is reported — this worktree boots on a local LM Studio model whose modelID normalizes to 'gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored'; per issue #440 the concrete model string is environment-dependent, so assert presence (non-empty, not 'unknown'), with the value following the configured default. What differs between runs: the concrete model value (follows the configured default), token counts/cost, ses_… UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with 'Reply with exactly the word: ok'). What must NOT differ: one ready→working→ready arc on one ses_… session, a non-empty summary.model_name in the golden, and a final ready state. The local gemma model is small and slow (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing 6s sleep outlasts the turn_done settle and the 180s timeout absorbs a slow turn.

scenarios.name: model-switch-midsession
scenarios.description: Two no-op turns in a single claudecode session separated by a /model slash command that swaps the active model. Locks down (a) the parser's per-turn Contribution.Model attribution (turn 1 → model A, turn 2 → model B), (b) MergeMetrics' latest-non-unknown-wins rule promoting ModelName to model B by t2_end, and (c) the isLocalCommandContent filter dropping the /model wrapper so no state machinery fires during the switch window (no working/waiting flicker). TotalTokens accumulates across the two models (T2 > T1). NOTE: claudecode's /model picker is interactive; the recipe drives the TUI via send + keys + sleep. If the picker UX is unreliable across claude versions, the recipe author may need to extend the driver with model-picker-specific support (e.g. a dedicated model_switch step).

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a provider out-of-band; the driver does not handle a login/OAuth prompt.

tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it can send two prompts to one long-lived session, switch the main model between them MID-SESSION with `/model`, and tear it down cleanly.
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

BOTH models reachable on the account: turn 1 runs on model A = gpt-5-nano (pinned via IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL → aider --model at boot) and the `/model gpt-4o-mini` switch target (model B) must resolve. The concrete model values are environment-dependent (issue #440): the durable assertion is that the model CHANGES mid-session and a non-empty model_name (model B) is present at session end, NOT a hardcoded pair.

feature-invisible caveat: aider's `> Weak model:` line is intentionally NOT matched by the parser's modelRE (only `Model:`/`Main model:` drive ModelName), so a switch that changed only the weak/summary model would be invisible; this recipe switches the MAIN model, which is observable.
setup: Canonical agent-agnostic model-switch-midsession cell for aider; shares coverage_id model-switch-midsession with aider/model-switch (same /model main-model swap mechanism, same observability) — the DISTINCTION from that sibling is emphasis, not mechanism. This cell leans on MID-SESSION CONTINUITY: every turn phase in expected.jsonl pins same_session_as the proc-<PID> session_birth, so the recording proves the switch happened DURING one live session, in place, not via a restart.
The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) launches aider in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt.

.aider.chat.history.md (FilesUnderCWD) materializes lazily once the first user input lands; before that the live REPL is observable as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner). The daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session, so ONE session_id stays live across the mid-session switch.

The `/model gpt-4o-mini` slash uses aider's one-shot inline `/model <name>` form: cmd_model raises SwitchCoder(main_model=…), the new coder emits a `> Main model: <name> …` blockquote (parser modelRE → latest-wins ModelName), and NO assistant turn is produced — so no `keys` picker and no `wait_turn` after the slash. The /model re-banner can produce an extra working→ready arc in aider — that is expected and fine (spec asserts ordering + no-waiting + single proc-<PID>, not exact arc count).

Turn 1 runs on model A; the mid-session /model slash switches to model B; turn 2 runs on model B. message-level ModelName follows latest-non-unknown-wins so SessionMetrics.ModelName promotes to B by t2_done, and token snapshots accumulate across the switch.

After both turns + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the proc-<PID> session (a mid-session model switch does NOT mint a new session).

Reply with exactly the word: second

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> session born ready within 2s of launch (the lazy transcript has not materialized yet).
