the mid-session /model slash produces NO additional working/waiting transition between t1_done and t2_start — it writes only a `> Main model:` blockquote with no `> Tokens:` close (no assistant turn) and mints no new session. (NB: the /model re-banner can produce an extra working→ready arc in aider — that is expected and fine; the spec asserts ordering + no-waiting + a single proc-<PID>, not an exact arc count.)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered bound to the proc-<PID> session, and exactly one process_exited on it at REPL teardown (a mid-session model switch does NOT create a second session).
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point; final live state is ready before teardown.

the .replay.json.golden carries summary.model_name == model B (gpt-4o-mini, latest-wins) — NOT model A (gpt-5-nano) and NOT 'unknown'; this is where the model A→B switch is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value is environment-dependent (issue #440); assert change + presence, not a hardcoded string.
aider.notes: Canonical model-switch-midsession cell for aider — SAME mechanism as the aider/model-switch sibling (both share coverage_id model-switch-midsession); the emphasis here is MID-SESSION CONTINUITY: every turn phase in expected.jsonl is pinned same_session_as the proc-<PID> session_birth, so the recording proves the swap happened DURING one live session, in place, not via a restart. RECORDABILITY (no driver_gap): aider's `/model <name>` accepts an INLINE ARGUMENT — cmd_model (aider/commands.py:87, "Switch the Main Model to a new LLM") raises SwitchCoder(main_model=…) and switches DIRECTLY, no arrow-key picker — so the switch is sendable via the existing `slash` step (NOT a `keys` gap, unlike the claudecode/codex picker recipes for this same scenario). The aider interactive driver implements send/slash/wait_turn/sleep (case `send|slash)` types text + Enter), so every step type this recipe needs is supported. WHY interactive (not headless `aider --message`): the /model slash only exists inside the REPL and the scenario needs two turns; the interactive driver keeps the REPL alive across both turns and kills the tmux session once → a SINGLE process_exited (headless aider is single-shot and would exit after one reply, missing the per-turn reset and the terminal working→ready settle). WHY NO wait_turn after the /model slash: the driver's `slash` step bumps the expected-turn counter exactly like `send`, but a /model switch writes a `> Main model:` blockquote with NO `> Tokens:` close (no assistant turn), so a wait_turn after it would hang on the unchanged turn count; the recipe covers the switch with a fixed 8s sleep instead. AIDER TURN-END IS SYNTHESIZED, NOT MARKED: the markdown transcript has no turn-end marker — `> Tokens:` closes one model CALL, not the turn; end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after ~1.5s quiet, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll, so the EFFECTIVE working→ready latency is ~5s. The 12s inter-turn sleeps (after turn 1, after the slash, and trailing) let each turn's working→ready flip fire AND be observed before the next prompt re-enters working — bumped from the original 7s/8s because gpt-5-nano replies fast and aider's IdleFlush→poll settle is ~5s, so a too-short gap races the synthesized turn-end; with shorter sleeps the next `####` prompt lands before IdleFlush and the parser opens the new turn WITHOUT an intervening turn_done, losing the per-turn reset (TestParser_NewUserPromptAfterMultiCall_StaysWorking). The /model re-banner can also produce an extra working→ready arc — expected and fine (spec asserts ordering + no-waiting + single proc-<PID>, not exact arc count). The model A→B assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (no model field), so expected.jsonl asserts only the two-turn mid-session lifecycle skeleton and the switch is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden plus the aider parser unit test TestParser_MainModel_AfterSlashCommand (parser_test.go:214). model A = gpt-5-nano (via IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL at boot), model B = gpt-4o-mini (via the mid-session `/model gpt-4o-mini` slash). SESSION IDENTITY: aider stores its transcript in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD), so the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> — no separate UUID rollout session and no handoff phase; the turn arcs, pid_bind (anchored at start — aider binds the PID at REPL birth, matching aider/basic-turn), and teardown all pin to session_birth via same_session_as, which is what proves one session_id from birth to teardown across the switch. What differs between runs: token counts, PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with `Reply with exactly the word: first/second` to keep turns short and deterministic), and the working→ready edge lands with ~5s idle-flush latency. What must NOT differ: two working→ready arcs on ONE proc-<PID> session, summary.model_name == model B in the golden, and a single process_exited at teardown. multi_model_orchestration=yes. NOTE: the agent-scenarios-coverage.json cell currently reads irrlicht_observes:partial ("architect/editor pair detected per turn but inter-turn switch may not refresh"); the committed assessment.json reassesses this yes/yes (0.85), grounded in the aider parser's modelRE matching `> Main model:` and the pinning TestParser_MainModel_AfterSlashCommand. preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured models out-of-band)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs pi in a tmux REPL so it can send two prompts to one long-lived session, switch models between them, and tear it down cleanly
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

TWO models authenticated on the account: model A is pi's boot default (openai-codex/gpt-5.5 in the captured recording) and model B is a distinct switch target (openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini). Both must resolve to a UNIQUE exact match for the canonical provider/id form — if either model is unavailable, the /model switch silently falls back to the interactive picker and the script-driven switch fails.

The concrete model values are environment-dependent (issue #440): the durable assertion is that the model CHANGES between turns and a non-empty model_name (model B) is present at session end, NOT a hardcoded pair. The recipe pins both for determinism.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt

pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy); the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback binds the UUID from line 1 (no wait_turn is used — see notes)

FIRST slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.5` PINS model A deterministically: pi persists the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so the boot model varies run-to-run; pinning A up front makes turn 1 reliably attributed to A regardless of what the previous session left as the default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix.

Each `/model <provider>/<id>` uses the CANONICAL provider/id form so pi's handleModelCommand resolves an exact single-model match and calls session.setModel() DIRECTLY — no arrow-key picker, no `keys` step. pi writes a model_change(modelId) line to the rollout (parsed for ModelName, no turn produced)

Turn 1 runs on model A (gpt-5.5); the SECOND slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` switches to model B; turn 2 runs on model B. message.model = A then B, so MergeMetrics' latest-non-unknown-wins promotes SessionMetrics.ModelName to B by t2_end and TotalTokens accumulates across both models

Both turns are driven by a fixed trailing sleep rather than wait_turn (the leading /model pin would desync the wait_turn counter — see notes); a one-word reply completes well within the 18s window (verified live)
After both turns + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — pi exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the rollout session (a model switch does NOT mint a new session)

script.text: /model openai-codex/gpt-5.5
Reply with exactly the word: turn-one
/model openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini
Reply with exactly the word: turn-two

verify: events.jsonl shows two ready→working→ready arcs on the SAME rollout session_id (turn 1 on model A, turn 2 on model B); the interactive REPL returns to ready after each, unlike headless pi --print which exits

events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered bound to the rollout session_id (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear born ready at launch; the turns and pid_discovered land on the UUID-keyed rollout session)
the /model slash produces NO additional working/waiting transition between t1_end and t2_start — it is a direct setModel() with no LLM round-trip and writes only a model_change line
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown (a model switch does NOT create a second session)
no state_transition to waiting appears for the rollout session at any point; final live state is ready before teardown

the .replay.json.golden carries summary.model_name == model B (gpt-5.4-mini, latest-wins) — NOT model A and NOT 'unknown'; this is where the model A→B switch is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value is environment-dependent (issue #440); assert change + presence, not a hardcoded string
pi.notes: CRITICAL FIRST CHECK RESULT (recordability): pi 0.70.0's `/model <provider>/<id>` accepts an INLINE ARGUMENT — handleModelCommand → findExactModelMatch → session.setModel() switches DIRECTLY with no arrow-key picker when the reference resolves to exactly ONE model (dist/modes/interactive/interactive-mode.js ~L3216, dist/core/model-resolver.js findExactModelReferenceMatch ~L53). Only an empty/ambiguous term opens the selector UI. So the switch is sendable via the existing `slash` step in SCRIPT mode — NO driver_gap (no `keys` primitive needed, unlike the claudecode/codex picker recipes for this same scenario). MUST use the full canonical provider/id form (e.g. openai-codex/gpt-5.5, openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini); a bare fuzzy term like `mini` may match multiple models and fall back to the picker. WHY a LEADING /model pin (gpt-5.5) before turn 1: pi PERSISTS the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so a bare `pi` launch boots on whatever the previous session left behind — NON-deterministic. The first recording attempt booted on gpt-5.4-mini (left over from a probe) and 'switched' to gpt-5.4-mini → NO observable model change. Pinning model A explicitly at the top makes the A→B switch a real change every run regardless of the persisted default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix. WHY NO wait_turn anywhere (both turns driven by trailing sleeps): the driver's `slash` step bumps the expected-turn counter exactly like `send`, but a /model switch produces a model_change line with NO assistant turn (turn_count only counts stopReason==stop). With a leading /model pin, EXPECTED is already 1 before turn 1, so a turn-1 wait_turn would block waiting for count≥2 while only 1 turn exists → driver timeout. The grammar has no 'slash that doesn't bump' primitive, so the recipe drops wait_turn entirely and lets a fixed 18s trailing sleep cover each one-word reply (completes well within 18s, verified live); the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback still binds the UUID. Same family of compromise as the pi synchronous-slash-command recipe (slash + sleep, no post-slash wait_turn). WHY the 2s/3s sleeps after each slash: they let the model_change line land and the classifier settle before the next prompt. The model lives on each assistant message.model (parser.go L103-105) and on the model_change line at modelId (L39-44), both normalized through tailer.NormalizeModelName into ev.ModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName (latest-non-unknown-wins). The model A→B assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (no model field in the validator Phase struct), so expected.jsonl asserts only the two-turn lifecycle skeleton (ready→working→ready twice on one rollout, no waiting, single process_exited) and the model switch is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (which replay-fixtures.sh diffs: it pins model B) plus the pi parser unit tests. C7/SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; session_birth (which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s') legitimately matches it. t1_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working in the whole recording (necessarily the rollout — the pre-session never goes working); t1_end/t2_start/t2_end, pid_bind, and teardown then pin to t1_start's session via same_session_as. What differs between runs: token counts/cost, rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with 'Reply with exactly the word: turn-one/turn-two'), and pi may flush a single turn as more than one working→ready burst (spec asserts ≥2 arcs that reset cleanly, not an exact count). What must NOT differ: model A (gpt-5.5) on turn 1 and model B (gpt-5.4-mini) on turn 2 (pinned, so deterministic despite #440's general env-dependence note), two working→ready arcs on ONE rollout session, summary.model_name == model B in the golden, and a single process_exited at teardown. NOTE: the agent-scenarios-coverage.json cell still reads irrlicht_observes:no with a stale 'no model change detection between turns' note that predates the pi parser's model_change handling; this recipe + the committed assessment.json reflect the yes/yes reassessment (0.80). Surfacing the matrix drift here per the skill's 'don't edit the coverage matrix' rule — a maintainer should flip the cell.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: first

codex.notes: Canonical agent-agnostic model-switch-midsession cell — the DISTINCTION from the codex-specific `model-switch` sibling is emphasis, not mechanism (both share coverage_id model-switch-midsession; the matrix wants both cells). This cell's spec leans on MID-SESSION CONTINUITY: every turn phase in expected.jsonl is pinned same_session_as the rollout session_birth, and a dedicated switch_window dwell phase asserts the session stays ready (no working/waiting flicker, no new session) while the /model picker is open — so the recording proves the switch happened DURING one live session, in place, not via a restart. Codex /model is an interactive TWO-STEP picker (model list, then reasoning-level list), NOT a one-shot `/model <name>` like aider — so the recipe drives it with `slash /model` to open it, then `keys` steps to navigate. Probed live on codex 0.130.0 (2026-05-23): the model list is 1=gpt-5.5(default) 2=gpt-5.4(current/boot-default, highlighted) 3=gpt-5.4-mini 4=gpt-5.3-codex 5=gpt-5.2, so `Down Down Enter` from the boot-highlighted entry 2 lands on gpt-5.3-codex (entry 4); the reasoning sub-screen then opens with Medium(default) highlighted, so a bare `Enter` confirms gpt-5.3-codex medium (footer flips to `gpt-5.3-codex medium`, rollout writes `• Model changed to gpt-5.3-codex medium`). The two `keys` steps are split with a 2s sleep because the model-confirm Enter and the reasoning-confirm Enter hit DIFFERENT screens that need a render gap; the `keys` step does NOT touch the turn counter, so wait_turn stays correct. Model A is gpt-5.4 (the boot default in this codex build). What differs between runs: assistant token counts/cost, exact rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, and the assistant's wording (we pin it with `Reply with exactly the word: first/second` to keep turns short and deterministic). What must NOT differ: ONE rollout session_id from birth to teardown, two working→ready arcs on it, no flicker during the picker window, the model_name landing on gpt-5.3-codex, and process_exited at teardown. If the picker navigation drifts (codex changes the model list order), the Down-count is wrong — re-probe `/model` and recount. multi_model_orchestration=yes.

preconditions: opencode on PATH and authenticated — verified opencode 1.14.50. This cell exercises a per-turn model switch, so it needs TWO reachable models.
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh isolated/coexist mode (IRRLICHT_ONBOARD_HOME + alt port), or --attach against a daemon started with --record.

LM Studio (http://127.0.0.1:1234) serving BOTH the config-default model A (lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored, ~3.4GB) AND model B (lmstudio/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b, ~18GB at 16384 ctx). On a 32GB host both fit RESIDENT at once (~21GB total) so no mid-recording eviction/reload is needed — load both before recording (lms load google/gemma-4-26b-a4b --context-length 16384) so the 26B does NOT overflow opencode's ~14k-token system prompt (at 4096 ctx it errors n_keep>n_ctx and emits 0 tokens). Model B at 16384 ctx responds in ~3-4 min on this MoE; budget the timeout accordingly.

Model values are environment-dependent (issue #440): the recipe asserts a per-turn model CHANGE A→B, not a hardcoded pair. If LM Studio cannot host a viable second local model, model B may instead be an API model the env has a key for (e.g. ionos/<model> when IONOS_API_TOKEN is set) — declare it in `settings.provider` and reference it in turn 2's `model` field.
setup: Driver launches headless `opencode run` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; opencode keys sessions on the cwd in its SQLite store, isolating this run's rows even alongside other opencode use.

The recipe's `settings` blob is seeded as a project-local opencode.json in the run cwd (drive-opencode-interactive.sh): it declares the default model A and the lmstudio provider with BOTH models, so turn 2's `-m lmstudio/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b` resolves without depending on the operator's global ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json.

Turn 1 launches the session (config-default model A); the driver captures the minted ses_… and chains turn 2 with `--session <id>`. Turn 2's `send` carries a `model` field → the driver threads `opencode run -m lmstudio/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b` so that turn runs on model B IN PLACE on the same ses_… (a real mid-session switch, no /new, no restart).

turn 2 (model B) drives the SAME ses_… ready→working→ready again; still no waiting; final live state is ready

opencode.notes: opencode mid-session model switch via the headless `send` step's per-turn `model` field (gap:model-select unblock, driver commit bbf82830). The observable is TWO ready→working→ready arcs on ONE ses_… session: turn 1 runs on the config-default model A (lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored), then turn 2 carries model:lmstudio/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b so the driver threads `opencode run -m lmstudio/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b` and that turn runs on model B IN PLACE on the same `--session` chain — a genuine A→B mid-session switch. opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter: the daemon reads per-turn model from the SQLite store (metrics.go: message.data.model.modelID, with a top-level message.data.modelID fallback — the 26B writes the top-level form), setting ModelName = NormalizeModelName(modelID) latest-non-empty-wins, so by turn 2 ModelName follows to model B and TotalTokens accumulates across the switch. The events.jsonl phase DSL carries NO model field, so expected.jsonl asserts only the two-turn mid-session lifecycle SKELETON (ready→working→ready twice on one ses_…, no waiting, PID bound); the model A→B change is asserted GOLDEN-LEVEL at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (diffed by replay-fixtures), NOT in expected.jsonl. WHY the headless path (no `slash`/`reset_session` step): the in-REPL /models picker is an arrow-key DialogSelectModel overlay the opencode driver cannot navigate (no `keys` step) and inline `/model <id>` is a no-op on headless `opencode run` (proven by a live store probe — see assessment.json); the `-m` flag is the deterministic, navigation-free switch and keeps the cell on the simpler headless route. WHY a complete `settings` blob (not the usual empty {} for opencode cells): turn 2 references lmstudio/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b, which the operator's global config may not declare — seeding a project-local opencode.json that declares the default model A AND the lmstudio provider with BOTH models makes the run self-contained and re-recordable without editing the global config. WHY the 26B at 16384 context: opencode's system prompt is ~14k tokens; at the default 4096 ctx the 26B errors `n_keep>n_ctx` and emits 0 tokens (no real turn) — load it at >=16384 (lms load google/gemma-4-26b-a4b --context-length 16384). On a 32GB host model A (~3.4GB) + model B (~18GB) both stay RESIDENT, so there is no mid-recording model swap and timing stays deterministic; turn 2 still takes ~3-4 min on this MoE, hence timeout_seconds 480. WHY model values are not hardcoded as a contract (issue #440): the recipe and golden assert a per-turn CHANGE A→B and a non-empty model B, not a specific pair — a different env may substitute another reachable second model (e.g. ionos/<model> with IONOS_API_TOKEN). What differs between runs: the ses_… UUID/PID, timestamps, the assistant wording of the two replies, per-turn token counts and latency. What must NOT differ: two ready→working→ready arcs on ONE ses_… session, no waiting, ending ready, and (golden-level) summary.model_name = the normalized model B. Where to look if it broke: core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/metrics.go (modelID extraction + latest-wins ModelName) and drive-opencode-interactive.sh run_send (the `-m` thread + --session chaining); if a future opencode build stopped writing modelID on the assistant message, or the daemon's nested/top-level fallback no longer matched the store shape, summary.model_name would not follow the switch.

scenarios.name: multiple-agents-same-workspace
scenarios.description: DIFFERENT coding agents (claudecode + codex + pi) live in the SAME cwd at once. The daemon labels each adapter independently (disjoint ProcessMatchers + transcript roots; per-(adapter, session_id) state and metrics from the maps.go projections), so the single recording shows distinct sessions each with its own working->ready arc and no cross-contamination. Recorded by run-cell-multi.sh, which launches each listed adapter's interactive driver concurrently against one shared cwd under a single --record daemon, then curates one per-adapter fixture each (same workspace events.jsonl, that adapter's own transcript). Each by_adapter cell is driven by the SAME recording — each stores its adapter's transcript for the replay golden and shares the workspace events.jsonl. The spec is adapter-agnostic (agentA = first to enter working, agentB = the other), so any two of the coexisting sessions validate the two-distinct-independent-arcs assertion; a third coexisting adapter is tolerated (working anchors + new_session skip placeholders and already-matched sessions).

scenarios.name: oversized-transcript-line
scenarios.description: Claude is prompted to emit a single very long sentence (≥5000 words, no embedded newlines) so the transcript flushes one large JSONL line. Recording may be brittle: 5000 words is moderate (~30 KB) — well under irrlicht's 64 MB tailer cap (issue #270) and also under bufio.Scanner's old 2 MB limit, so the test is a sanity-check rather than a true cap-stress. The spec asserts only the visible state arc (ready→working→ready); the absence of a parser stall is INFERRED from the arc completing. Per assessment.json caveats, manufacturing a truly oversized (>2 MB) line from a real claude CLI is awkward — a follow-up cell can hand-craft a synthetic >2 MB line into the live transcript file if deeper cap-stress coverage is needed.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)

codex.notes: applicable:false — a live codex session cannot be deterministically driven to emit a >64 MB (or even >2 MB) single JSONL line; this is a shared-tailer robustness property, not a codex behavior. A 5000-word live recipe would only be a ~30 KB sanity-check, not true cap-stress, and we do NOT fabricate a 64 MB line into a fixture. expected.jsonl is retained as the documented state-arc target.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured provider/model (auth is set up out-of-band; the rig's precheck delegates auth to the user). NOTE: aider had NO auth in the environment this recipe was authored in (no provider key; headless aider hangs on the model-auth prompt), so the recording is DEFERRED — this is the documented target; record once a live model is reachable.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it stays alive across the large-reply turn and the daemon can observe the working→ready settle before teardown

setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` in a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in .aider.chat.history.md before sending the prompt

aider is FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser} — there is no transcript-UUID rollout session; the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> (the process scanner), so the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row (cf. the full-lifecycle-toolcall recipe)

WHY this exercises the oversized-line path: the prompt asks aider for ONE very long single-line sentence (≥5000 words, no embedded newlines), so aider appends one large `> …`/prose line to .aider.chat.history.md. The shared tailer reads it via readLineCapped(64MB cap, issue #270) BEFORE the isRawLine branch hands the trimmed line to aider's ParseLineRaw — i.e. aider's RawLineParser path is gated by the SAME oversized-line cap as the JSONL adapters. A ~30 KB line is far under the 64 MB cap, so it is buffered whole into assistantBuffer and the turn resolves via the usual `> Tokens:`/IdleFlush close. This is a sanity-check that the arc completes on a large single line, NOT a true >64 MB cap-stress (a live aider CLI cannot be coerced into a multi-megabyte single line — markdown prose wraps and tool output is multi-line `>` blockquotes); the genuine >64 MB cap behavior is pinned agent-agnostically by core/pkg/tailer/large_line_test.go

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message`: aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1500ms quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run can exit promptly after the reply, so the daemon misses the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited and truncates the arc. Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker), so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn; the ≥8s trailing sleep is one full idle-flush cycle plus slack so the working→ready settle is observed before teardown

opencode.notes: applicable:false — observed opencode INCAPABILITY (not a model weakness): a live opencode session cannot deterministically produce a single oversized (>=256 KB) transcript part, because opencode's tools cap their output before persisting it to the SQLite store. Verified empirically at record time (2026-05-26) on opencode 1.14.50 + the local default model: (1) the recipe's tool-induction path DID run cleanly — bash created a 300000-byte single-line big.txt and the read tool read it back, arc ready->working->ready completed twice on one ses_… session (run-cell STAGED) — but the read-tool-result part was only ~4.6 KB: opencode's read tool truncates each line to 2000 chars (the recorded output literally says 'line truncated to 2000 chars'). (2) A direct probe forcing ~300 KB of bash stdout produced part.data of only ~82 KB with state.metadata.truncated=1 and 'Full output saved to: ~/.local/share/opencode/tool-output/tool_…' — opencode stores a ~30-50 KB preview in the part row and overflows the full bytes to a SEPARATE sidecar file that the daemon never reads as a transcript part. So no tool path yields a single >=256 KB part.data row / >=256 KB exported transcript.jsonl line. This is a shared-tailer/store robustness PROPERTY, not an opencode feature, and opencode is structurally stronger here anyway: it is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (agent.go:41) — the live watcher reads part.data straight from SQLite via rows.Scan (watcher.go:456-499) and ComputeMetrics likewise (metrics.go:67-103) with NO bufio line-buffer cap, so even an oversized part would be read whole; the shared tailer's 64 MB readLineCapped guard (tailer.go, issue #270) never gates opencode store reads. The authoritative cap-stress coverage is the agent-agnostic unit test core/pkg/tailer/large_line_test.go (TestTailAndProcess_LargeToolResultLine + TestReadLineCapped_OversizedLineSkipped), which pins the 64 MB cap on the shared tailer. We do NOT fabricate a >256 KB part into a fixture. expected.jsonl is retained as the documented state-arc target (the assessment route was record; the live recording overrode it — see assessment.json and the implement observability_correction).

Locks down the parser's terminal-stop_reason gate (intermediate tool_use/tool_result pairs do not change state).

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured provider/model (auth is set up out-of-band; the rig's precheck delegates auth to the user). The live REPL needs a model that actually answers so the failing shell call and its corrective retry really run inside one turn; headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt.

tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it stays alive across the whole self-correction turn (the working→ready settle is idle-flush-bound and must be observed before teardown)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

observes:partial caveat (mirror of the coverage matrix): aider's reflection loop is genuinely WITHIN one turn (one `#### ` header, run_one's `while message:` loop, max_reflections=3), so the topology is one contiguous working span. The only thing that can break the no-flicker target is the 1500ms IdleFlush: if any single inter-reflection model call leaves the transcript silent for >~1.5s, IdleFlush synthesizes a premature turn_done (working→ready) and the next `> Tokens:` line reopens it (ready→working). A fast local model avoids this; a slow remote model can trip it. The root cause is timing, not topology.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in .aider.chat.history.md before sending the prompt

aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD, RawLineParser), so the daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session and no handoff/reconcile; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row. The transcript is LAZY (materializes on first input), but the proc-<PID> pre-session is observable from launch (that is where the spec's session_birth ‘ready within 1s’ is grounded)

WHY the retry is one turn: io.user_input() writes the `#### ` history header exactly once per top-level prompt, before Coder.run_one; run_one's reflection loop (max_reflections=3) sets reflected_message and calls send_message directly, never re-emitting a `#### ` header (base_coder.py:860/879/924-944). The loop is driven by --auto-test: after aider applies the edit, the test_cmd runs; a non-zero exit feeds its output back as reflected_message, so aider edits again and re-tests — repeating until the test passes or max_reflections is hit. The parser treats every `> Tokens:` line as assistant_message (NOT turn_done) so the turn stays open across the multiple model calls (parser.go:18-23,146-188), and the `> Running <test_cmd>`→tool_result markers never change state away from working — so the edit→test-fail→reflect→re-edit arc is one contiguous ready→working→ready span

HOW the reflection is elicited deterministically: settings.aider_extra_args launches aider with `--auto-test --test-cmd false`. `false` always exits non-zero, so EVERY edit aider makes inside the turn trips a test failure and forces at least one reflection — independent of what gpt-5-nano actually writes. The loop self-limits at max_reflections=3, so the turn always settles to ready; we never depend on the model eventually producing passing code. No permission gate is involved: aider runs under --yes-always with NO permission hook (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), the edit and the auto-test auto-execute, and the session never enters waiting

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message` (drift fix, same shape as the pi/codex siblings): aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run can exit promptly after the reply, so the daemon misses the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited and truncates the arc. Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker — one per model call). The recipe waits TWICE: the FIRST wait_turn returns on the initial edit's `> Tokens:`, the SECOND returns on the first reflection round's `> Tokens:` (the auto-test failed and aider re-called the model). This guarantees the within-turn reflection actually completed a model round before teardown — a single wait_turn returns too early (the reflection call is still in flight) and the turn gets truncated with no working→ready settle. The ≥8s (here 20s) trailing sleep then absorbs any remaining reflection rounds (max_reflections caps at 3) plus one full ~1.5s IdleFlush cycle, so the terminal working→ready settle is observed before the single tmux kill-session teardown

The send asks aider to create a small Python file (an edit aider can reliably perform), which is what arms --auto-test: the post-edit test (`false`) fails, driving the within-turn reflection loop. The prompt deliberately avoids requesting a shell command — aider's reflection loop fires on --auto-lint/--auto-test failures, NOT on a model-invoked shell tool's exit code, so the lint/test path is the only reliable in-turn self-correction trigger

script.text: Create a new file solution.py containing a single Python function add(a, b) that returns a + b. Make the edit now; do not ask me anything.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new + presession_created for the proc-<PID> session born ready within ~1s of REPL launch

transcript.jsonl shows the single working span spanning multiple `> Tokens:` model-call markers (the initial edit plus ≥1 reflection re-edit) — the within-turn self-correction (coarse-grained: aider's tool markers carry no args/result payload)

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready between the failing auto-test and the corrective re-edit — the session stays continuously working across the reflection loop (the parser holds working across `> Tokens:`/tool_result runs within one `#### ` turn)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point during the working span (the edit and auto-test auto-execute under --yes-always; aider never gates)
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at the single turn end (synthesized by IdleFlush ~1.5s after the last `> Tokens:`, after max_reflections is reached)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the proc-<PID> session at REPL teardown

KNOWN TIMING RISK (observes:partial): if any single inter-reflection model call is silent >~1.5s, IdleFlush can fire a premature working→ready then the next `> Tokens:` reopens working→working flicker — flag it as a known timing limitation, do NOT widen the spec to accept it
Do not assert the auto-test command's output text — assert only the state topology and the multi-call reflection span

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated/configured (the default model is set in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json; auth is out-of-band)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

the configured default model must be capable of emitting MULTIPLE tool calls in one turn — if the model will not iterate (write then read then edit) this cell is unrecordable for a MODEL reason (opencode capabilities.json tool_calls:true), not an opencode/daemon incapability
setup: The driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ with NO --model flag, so it uses the config default; the whole multi-tool self-correction turn rides ONE `opencode run` subprocess that exits when the turn completes

No auto-mode / permission step is needed: in headless default `opencode run` auto-executes tools inside opencode's own sandbox with no interactive gate, so every tool call in the iteration runs autonomously and the session never enters waiting — unlike the claudecode recipe, which must enable auto mode to keep a Bash call from tripping a gate

Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db, ProcessOwnedStore), keyed on the UUID ses_… session; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table. Each tool call lands in the part table as a `tool` part (status pending→running→completed/error) that the parser maps to function_call / function_call_output (parser.go parseToolPart). A tool-calls step-finish reason keeps the session working; only step-finish reason=stop → turn_done settles working→ready — so the write→read→edit sequence is one contiguous working span (parser.go parseStepFinish)

wait_turn is a no-op because `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends; the ≥4s trailing sleep gives the daemon's poller one full tick to observe the working→ready settle before the run process exits

The send is a crisp create-a-deliberate-bug-then-read-it-back-and-fix prompt so the small/slow local default model reliably emits MULTIPLE auto-executed tool calls (write → read → corrective edit/write) inside ONE sustained working span before replying done — this is the iteration the cell captures

WHY one send, not multiple: the whole self-correction is a SINGLE turn (try → observe problem → fix), so it must ride one `opencode run` to be one contiguous working span; splitting it into back-to-back sends would create separate working→ready arcs (and the known opencode poller nuance coalesces rapid same-session arcs anyway). Verify ≥2 tool parts landed before turn_done in the SQLite store at record time

script.text: Do this all in ONE turn, using your tools, and do not ask me anything: (1) use your write tool to create a file calc.py containing exactly `def add(a, b):\n    return a - b` — note the body deliberately SUBTRACTS, which is a bug; (2) use your read tool to read calc.py back; (3) notice add() should ADD, and use your edit/write tool to fix the body to `return a + b`. After all three steps reply with exactly: done

verify: events.jsonl contains the ses_… session born ready within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> presession may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the UUID-keyed ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id

events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the iteration prompt is sent, sustained across the write → read → corrective-edit tool calls (one contiguous working span)

the opencode SQLite store / transcript.jsonl contains at least TWO tool parts (function_call / function_call_output) inside the working span — the buggy write, the read-back, and the corrective edit (the auto-executed, ungated tool calls; opencode has no interactive gate in headless default)

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready between the buggy write and the corrective edit — the session stays continuously working across the retry cycle (a tool-calls step-finish reason does not close the turn)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting for the ses_… session at any point (every tool call auto-executes; no permission window)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id once the corrective edit resolves and the turn completes (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done)
final session state is ready

scenarios.name: shell-escape-command
scenarios.description: Driver types Claude Code's !cmd shell-escape (`!echo hello`) in the interactive REPL. claude runs the shell command locally without an LLM round-trip and writes <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrapper events to the transcript. Per parser.go:216-225, irrlicht's claudecode parser deliberately skips these wrapper events, so state must stay in ready throughout (no synthesized turn). The recipe then sends a normal user prompt to prove the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives the ready→working→ready arc. Verdict adopts the intent reading of the spec (no stuck in working) per assessment.json — the alternative literal reading would require a parser change to synthesize a transition arc.
