script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
during the ~5 s window after the !echo shell-escape, NO state_transition to working fires for the UUID (the parser must skip <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers)
during that window, NO state_transition to waiting fires
after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the UUID
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains the shell-escape wrapper lines (<bash-input> / <bash-stdout>) plus 1 user line and at least 1 assistant line for the final turn

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (codex 0.130.0; the !-prefix shell escape is a TUI affordance, confirmed in-binary: 'Prefix a command with ! to run it locally. Example: !ls')
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
codex is launched in interactive TUI mode (--no-alt-screen) so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — codex exec headless has no interactive ! shell mode
setup: Driver boots a codex TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits out the Booting MCP phase
Codex assigns its own rollout UUID; the driver discovers the rollout-*.jsonl on the first wait_turn
codex.recipe_notes: Each !cmd escape is typed as a keys step WITHOUT Enter, then a 1s sleep, then a separate keys:'Enter' step. The codex driver's keys handler (unlike step_send) does not pause before Enter, so a combined '! l s Enter' raced codex's Ink input render — Enter was silently dropped, the escapes accumulated in the input box, and the final send submitted ls/sleep/prompt as ONE shell command (collapsed into a single <user_shell_command>). Splitting typing from Enter with a render-settle sleep submits each escape on its own. The 9s sleep after !sleep 5 covers the command runtime plus the daemon settle.

script.keys: ! s l e e p Space 5

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the codex rollout session entering ready after launch

each !cmd shell escape drives a ready→working→ready arc for the session (codex synthesizes a turn: task_started / user_message<user_shell_command> / task_complete) — UNLIKE claudecode, which skips the wrappers and stays ready
no state_transition gets stuck in working: every working transition is followed by a working→ready before the next escape or the final prompt
after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the session, proving it is still alive
final session state is ready

NOTE (overlap + caveat): this is a USER !-escape, distinct from full-lifecycle-toolcall's model-driven exec tool (no function_call/function_call_output is written for a !-escape). The recorded working DWELL is debounce-bounded (~2 s), not command-bounded: codex's parser skips task_started and codex flushes the synthetic user_message only when the command exits, so the daemon cannot anchor working-start to command-start (see assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial). The spec therefore asserts the ready→working→ready topology and final ready, not a dwell length.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (PI_API_KEY or configured provider; verified against pi 0.70.0)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
pi is launched in interactive REPL mode so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — headless `pi --print` is single-shot and has no interactive ! shell mode

INTENT READING (per assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial): the pi parser deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131), so irrlicht does NOT reproduce the literal ready→working→ready arc the scenario-meanings Conceptual flow describes for the shell command. This cell asserts the safety invariant instead — the escape must not leave the session stuck in working and must not emit waiting; state stays ready across the escape. The literal arc would require a parser change.
setup: Driver boots a fresh pi REPL in a per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; pi assigns its own session UUID per rollout

The shell escape is issued via a `send` step typing the literal text `!echo hi` + Enter (the pi driver's send handler uses tmux send-keys -l, so the leading ! is sent verbatim and pi runs it as a local shell command); NO wait_turn follows the escape, since `!`-prefixed input produces a bashExecution rollout line, not an assistant stopReason=stop turn

Avoid !! only if you want context inclusion to differ — either ! or !! is skipped identically by the pi parser; this recipe uses single ! (output retained in context, no LLM round-trip at escape time)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
!echo hi

verify: events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working→ready cycle for the entire run, driven by the warm-up turn only (pi's first message drives working, stopReason=stop drives ready); pi may flush the single warm-up turn as one OR two working→ready bursts

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to working after the warm-up turn returns to ready — the !echo shell escape drives none because the pi parser skips the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point
the escape window (≥6s after the warm-up turn ends) shows the rollout session continuously ready — no transcript_activity is recorded for the !echo escape; the session is NOT left stuck in working

transcript.jsonl contains 1 user message line, 1 assistant stopReason=stop line (the warm-up turn), and 1 bashExecution line (the !echo escape — which does NOT appear as an assistant turn and is skipped by the parser)
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session; final session state is ready
pi.notes: Pi difference vs claudecode/codex: pi runs `!`-prefixed REPL input as a LOCAL shell command with no LLM round-trip (verified live against pi 0.70.0 — interactive-mode.js text.startsWith("!") → handleBashCommand → executeBash + recordBashResult; the `!!` variant additionally excludes output from context). Pi records the escape as ONE {"type":"message","message":{"role":"bashExecution",...}} rollout line. The pi parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go:128-131) deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution role (ev.Skip = true), so — UNLIKE codex, which synthesizes a working→ready arc for its !-escape, and LIKE claudecode, which skips its <bash-input> wrappers — the daemon sees no transcript_activity and the session stays ready across the escape. The whole observable is the ABSENCE of a second working transition plus the proof (warm-up arc) that a NORMAL prompt still drives state. RECIPE SHAPE: the warm-up turn comes FIRST (the escape SECOND) — opposite to the claudecode recipe — because pi creates ~/.pi/agent/sessions/.../<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl only after the first user message, so without a leading real turn the driver's resolve_transcript never binds a UUID. The escape uses `send` (not the claudecode `keys` step): the pi interactive driver supports send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but NOT keys, and step_send types the literal text (-l) then Enter, so `!echo hi` is delivered verbatim. NO wait_turn after the escape: it produces no assistant stopReason=stop, so wait_turn would time out; the trailing 6s sleep instead holds the recording open past the classifier debounce so the post-escape ready dwell is observed cleanly and the daemon-kill doesn't race the final-state emission. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (bridges the gap until the UUID rollout transcript appears); session_birth legitimately matches that pre-session, while the warm-up arc and post-escape dwell pin to the rollout via same_session_as (turn_start anchors to recording start with NO pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working — necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working). Between runs: warm-up assistant wording, token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ, and pi may flush the warm-up turn as one or two working→ready bursts; the load-bearing assertion is the silent ready dwell across the escape window. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go (case "bashExecution" → ev.Skip) and whether a pi update changed how `!` escapes are recorded in the rollout.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers the final normal prompt.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the shell-escape and the final prompt can both be issued to one long-lived session.
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

OBSERVES:PARTIAL (per assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial): aider's `/run <cmd>` (alias `!<cmd>`) is the direct analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape (agent_supports: yes), but irrlicht does NOT surface it as anything distinct from an ordinary turn. OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2 recording, supersedes the earlier doc-based `> Running`-fold prediction): aider 0.86.2's `/run` does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` line — it writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes, so runningRE never fires and ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. The claudecode-shaped top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 therefore DOES hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls), but for a different reason than claudecode. This cell asserts the ready→working→ready arc + final ready and asserts NO tool_result/Bash marker — the escape is observed only as the state arc; the absence of a distinct shell-escape event is the documented partial scope, not a defect.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the first step.

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears. There is no transcript-UUID rollout session — the whole arc lives on the one proc-<PID> row.

The shell-escape is issued via a `slash` step typing the literal text `/run echo hello` + Enter (the aider driver handles `slash` identically to `send` via tmux send-keys -l, so the inline argument is delivered verbatim; `/run` is an inline-arg slash, NOT an arrow-key picker, so no `keys` step is needed). `/run echo hello` writes a `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line (opens a turn → working) then aider 0.86.2 writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes — NOT a `> Running` line — so runningRE never fires and NO Bash tool_result is emitted (OBSERVES:PARTIAL correction). NO wait_turn follows the escape — it produces no `> Tokens:` line, so wait_turn would time out; a trailing sleep lets IdleFlush settle working→ready instead.

aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's ~5s SessionDetector working-refresh poll (so the working→ready settle trails the command exit by several seconds; this is why the post-escape sleep is ≥7s).
After the final wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session.

script.text: /run echo hello
Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)

the /run echo hello shell-escape drives a ready→working→ready arc on the bindable session: the `#### /run echo hello` line opens the turn (→working), aider runs the command locally, and IdleFlush settles the turn back to ready (~5s effective latency) — the escape does NOT stick in working and never enters waiting

NO-TOOL_RESULT (the observes:partial scope, observability correction vs the earlier doc-based prediction): aider 0.86.2 emits NO Bash tool_result for the `/run` escape. It writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, NOT a `> Running` line, so runningRE never matches and zero tool calls are recorded. The escape is observed only as the ready→working→ready state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. The top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 happens to hold (zero tool calls) but for a different reason than claudecode; this is documented scope, not drift, and NO tool_result/Bash marker is asserted.
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the bindable aider session_id

after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows a second ready→working→ready arc on the SAME session_id — proves the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives state after the shell-escape
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session); final session state is ready
aider.notes: aider vs claudecode/codex/pi for this scenario: aider's `/run <cmd>` (documented alias `!<cmd>`) runs a shell command locally with no LLM round-trip — the direct analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape (assessment.json agent_supports: yes). The recipe is templated from the claudecode shell-escape recipe but diverges on the observation shape (assessment.json irrlicht_observes: PARTIAL). claudecode's parser deliberately SKIPS the <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers so the escape synthesizes NO turn and state stays ready throughout (its top-level verify.tool_calls_max:0 holds). OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2 recording — supersedes the earlier doc-based prediction that aider folds the escape into a Bash tool_result): aider 0.86.2's `/run` does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote, so runningRE (^>\\s*Running\\s+, parser.go:69-70) never matches and p.toolCall("Bash") (parser.go:122-124) never fires — ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. aider 0.86.2 instead writes `> Add command output to the chat?` + `> Added N lines of output` confirmation blockquotes, which fall through to the generic ignored-blockquote branch. (parser_test.go TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell still pins the runningRE→Bash path for a LITERAL `> Running` line, but `/run` never produces that line.) What still holds: the `####` user-prompt line aider writes for `/run` opens a real turn (→working), and ~1500ms later IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done (working→ready, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s poll). So the observable is a genuine ready→working→ready arc (NOT claudecode's stay-in-ready) with NO tool call — the escape is observed only as that arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event; that indistinguishability is exactly why the cell is partial. verify.tool_calls_max:0 happens to hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls), but for a different reason than claudecode. RECIPE SHAPE: the escape comes FIRST (via `slash` — the aider interactive driver supports send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but NOT keys; `/run echo hello` is an inline-arg slash so `slash` delivers it verbatim, no `keys` gap), with NO wait_turn (it produces no `> Tokens:` line) followed by a ≥7s sleep so IdleFlush's working→ready lands before the next prompt re-enters working; then a normal `send`+`wait_turn` proves the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives the arc; a ≥12s trailing sleep holds the recording open past the final IdleFlush settle so the tmux kill doesn't race the final working→ready emission + process_exited (TEARDOWN RACE: an 8s tail let the kill land mid-working and lost the final ready + process_exited; ≥12s gives the ~5s SessionDetector poll + IdleFlush full room to settle). SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL surfaces a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; the whole arc lives on that one proc-<PID> row (no transcript-UUID rollout for FilesUnderCWD). In expected.jsonl, escape_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin (matcher picks the FIRST working — the pre-session never goes working), and pid_bind / escape_end / final_turn_start / final_turn_end / teardown pin via same_session_as so a co-occurring pre-session can never satisfy them. Between runs: the shell command stdout, exact final-turn assistant wording, per-turn token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/parser.go (the `####`→turn-open at parser.go:94-108 and the IdleFlush turn_done at parser.go:216-229) and whether an aider update changed how `/run` / `!` escapes are written into .aider.chat.history.md (e.g. a return of the `> Running` line, which would re-introduce a Bash tool_result). KNOWN FAILING (5/7, live aider 0.86.2): the load-bearing escape arc (escape_start→escape_end: the /run shell-escape does NOT stick in working) + session_birth/pid_bind/final_turn_start all PASS, but final_turn_end + teardown FAIL — aider does not reliably emit the FINAL turn's working→ready settle before the tmux kill. When the final turn's last transcript write coincides with the working transition (no later fswatcher activity to re-arm the tailer), the SessionDetector refresh-ticker IdleFlush (~5s poll) races process death and the settle is lost. Reproduced across 4 recordings (trailing sleeps 12/18/20s; both file-editing and plain-reply final prompts). This is the assessed irrlicht_observes:partial latency carve-out, not a daemon regression and not recipe-fixable; marked known_failing in expected.jsonl. PROMPT NOTE: the final prompt is "Reply with exactly the word: ok" (mirrors basic-turn), but gpt-5-nano sometimes still interprets the word as a filename and edits a file — harmless to the arc; the teardown gap is independent of whether the final turn edits.

opencode.notes: scope_note: opencode has no user-level shell-escape (`!cmd`) that runs outside a model turn; shell is the model-invoked bash tool, already covered by auto-executed-tool-call. agent_supports:unknown (per assessment.json) → applicable_false (#476): no recipe script, no recording. The headless `opencode run` driver also has no escape/`!` step, but that gap is downstream of the missing agent primitive (not a driver_gap).

scenarios.name: streaming-partial-writes
scenarios.description: Claude streams a multi-sentence prose response across several JSONL chunks. The parser must keep the session in working across all intermediate assistant_streaming events (non-terminal stop_reason) and only flip to ready when the terminal end_turn event lands. This locks down the Bug-D streaming-flicker family (issue #102): resolveAssistantStreaming (parser.go:288) downgrades non-terminal stop_reasons to assistant_streaming, which is excluded from IsAgentDone(). The streaming_window phase pins the no-flicker invariant.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/

script.text: Write a 5-sentence story about a robot. Each sentence on its own line.

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working transition for the UUID
across the streaming window (multiple assistant_streaming events with non-terminal stop_reason), NO state_transition to ready fires before the terminal end_turn line
NO state_transition to waiting fires during the streaming window
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at turn end
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 2 assistant lines (intermediate streaming chunks + terminal)

pi.notes: DEGRADED to applicable:false after a live recording proved the streaming-stability property is not observable for pi at the transcript layer. SCOPE_NOTE: pi writes its rollout JSONL session file ONCE, on turn completion — the assistant turn lands as a SINGLE consolidated `message`/assistant line carrying `stopReason=stop` and the whole multi-line reply in one content block (confirmed: a '6-line poem, one line at a time' prompt produced exactly 1 user line + 1 assistant(stop=stop) line). There are ZERO intermediate partial-assistant flushes in the transcript the daemon tails; pi's incremental streaming is purely a terminal-UI rendering concern that never reaches the JSONL. The spec's streaming window — sustaining `working` across multiple non-terminal partial flushes with no premature `→ ready` — therefore has nothing to assert over: the completed turn lands all-at-once, so the daemon emits ready→working→ready essentially instantaneously (working→ready at +0 ms because stopReason=stop is already present on the first activity). The live recording also exhibited a SECOND spurious working→ready arc ~2 s later from the daemon re-touching the already-completed file — a file-touch artifact, NOT streamed-content flicker, and unrelated to the property this cell targets. Validator result on the captured recording: 1/5 phases (turn_start 'no state_transition to ready' invariant violated at +0 ms). This is an adapter-transport limitation, not a flaky recipe: no live-CLI prompt can make pi emit intermediate partial-assistant JSONL flushes. The torn/partial-line concern lives in the separate oversized-transcript-line cell. expected.jsonl is retained as the documented target should pi ever switch to incremental rollout writes. Reassessment was yes/yes (0.72) on the PARSER logic (parser.go:78-83 sustains working on every non-stop assistant message; parser.go:55-60 maps compaction to assistant so even a mid-stream compaction can't flicker) — the parser would hold working correctly IF partial flushes existed, but pi's transport never produces them, so the cell is unrecordable as specified.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the default is a local LM Studio model so no remote key is needed)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

OBSERVABILITY CAVEAT (mirror of the spec's notes): opencode persists session parts to SQLite, observed by the daemon's WAL-fsnotify-driven ProcessOwnedStore watcher (scanParts, time_updated cursor). A no-tool prose turn run headlessly via blocking `opencode run` can flush its assistant text part(s) AND the terminal step-finish reason=stop to the WAL inside one scan window at turn completion — basic-turn collapsed working→ready in ~0.3ms for exactly this reason. The multi-line prompt aims to spread the assistant text across several WAL flushes so the working dwell is genuinely observable; but if opencode writes the whole reply atomically the streaming_window has nothing intermediate to assert over. If a live recording confirms the atomic-write behavior, that is an inherent ProcessOwnedStore observability gap (surface it as observability_correction → daemon_capability:incapable), NOT a flaky recipe and NOT a reason to rebase expected.jsonl.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the assistant turn finishes, so wait_turn is a no-op

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the part table (WAL-fsnotify-driven, debounced by minScanGap) and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns (opencode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs)

OPENCODE'S STREAMED UNIT IS THE ASSISTANT TEXT PART: parseTextPart maps every assistant text part to assistant_message (parser.go:178-197 — no turn boundary, excluded from IsAgentDone), and step-finish reason=tool-calls also maps to assistant_message (parser.go:155-157). Only step-finish reason=stop emits turn_done (parser.go:152-154) and settles the turn working→ready. So no intermediate part flush can flip the session to ready: the session holds working across every text/non-stop flush that reaches the WAL — the no-flicker invariant the streaming_window pins

ELICITING A MULTI-FLUSH WORKING DWELL: the send asks for a moderately long multi-line prose answer (a short numbered list, one item per line) so the assistant emits its content as several text parts that flush to the WAL across multiple scan windows, giving the daemon an observable working dwell BEFORE the terminal step-finish reason=stop. This is the no-tool analog of opencode/self-correction-iteration's multi-step turn — but with tool_calls_max:0 there is no permission gate, so the session never enters waiting

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleep and timeout are generous: a slow multi-line turn must not trip a false timeout, and the trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so the step-finish-driven working→ready is observed before teardown

script.text: Write a 5-step guide for making a cup of tea. Number each step 1 through 5, and put each step on its own line. Write at least one full sentence per step.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl shows exactly one state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the multi-line prompt is sent — the streaming window opens here

across the streaming window (opencode emits the assistant content as incremental text parts, each mapped to assistant_message — non-terminal), NO state_transition to ready fires before the terminal step-finish reason=stop; the session holds working solidly across all flushes (the no-flicker invariant)
NO state_transition to waiting fires during the streaming window (tool_calls_max:0 — no tool call, no permission gate)
events.jsonl shows exactly one state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id at turn end (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done closes the turn)
the session's final live state is ready

transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 1 assistant text line plus the terminal step-finish (if the reply lands as multiple text parts, multiple assistant lines appear — the observable multi-flush span)

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a model out-of-band (IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL plus the matching provider key/endpoint — e.g. a local LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible server). Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs on the model-auth prompt; the live REPL needs a model that actually answers the prompt and streams a multi-part reply.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the streamed turn is observed across its partial flushes while the process stays alive
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

OBSERVES:PARTIAL CARVE-OUT (mirror of the coverage note): aider's end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1500ms of transcript quiet (parser.go:54); a SLOW remote model call that leaves the history file quiet >1500ms BETWEEN flushes (mid-turn) can fire a premature turn_done → premature working→ready, flickering the no-flicker invariant this cell pins. The no-flicker property holds for normal back-to-back flushes (the common case) but is NOT airtight against a slow remote call; this recipe targets the no-flicker IDEAL, and the streaming_window invariant is what surfaces any such premature-ready drift in a recording.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears. aider is keyed by proc-<PID> — there is no transcript UUID rollout session; the whole arc stays on the one proc-<PID> row

AIDER'S PARTIAL-FLUSH UNIT IS THE PER-MODEL-CALL PROSE BLOCK, NOT TOKEN-LEVEL STREAMING: aider has no JSONL streaming event and the markdown history is written as completed message blocks (incremental token rendering is terminal-UI only and never reaches the file). The partial-flush analog is the per-model-CALL prose block closed by its own `> Tokens:` line. closeModelCall (parser.go:153-188) emits assistant_message — NOT turn_done — at each `> Tokens:` line and leaves the turn open, and IsAgentDone (session.go:461-496) does not treat aider's assistant_message as done, so no per-call flush can flip the session to ready: the session holds working across every partial flush (TestParser_MultiModelCall_KeepsTurnOpen)

ELICITING A MULTI-MODEL-CALL TURN DETERMINISTICALLY (the fix for the single-flush failure mode): a plain reply prompt — even a long 'walk through these files' one — can be answered by the model in ONE model call (one `> Tokens:` flush), so there are NO intermediate partial flushes to observe and the streaming_window has nothing to assert (the prior recording reproduced exactly this: one `> Tokens:` line, then a late --yes-always file-write reopened working AFTER the turn settled, exiting while working). Instead, borrow the aider/self-correction-iteration mechanism: settings.aider_extra_args launches aider with `--auto-test --test-cmd false`. The send asks aider to create one small Python file (an edit it can reliably perform); `false` always exits non-zero, so EVERY edit trips a test failure and forces a reflection re-call within the SAME `#### ` turn (run_one's max_reflections=3 loop, base_coder.py:860/879/924-944, never re-emits a `#### ` header). Each reflection round closes its own `> Tokens:` line, so one user turn deterministically produces ≥2 model calls — a genuine multi-flush span the session must hold working across, independent of what gpt-5-nano writes. The loop self-limits at max_reflections=3 so the turn always settles to ready; no permission gate (--yes-always, permission_hooks:false) so the edit + auto-test auto-execute and the session never enters waiting.

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears (one model call finished). The recipe waits TWICE: the FIRST wait_turn returns on the initial edit's `> Tokens:`, the SECOND returns on the first reflection round's `> Tokens:` (the auto-test failed and aider re-called the model) — this guarantees a genuine multi-call streaming span completed before teardown; a single wait_turn returns while the reflection call is still in flight and truncates the turn. aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet (parser.go:54,216-229), surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector poll (~5s effective latency); the trailing sleep (≥10s; here 20s) absorbs all reflection rounds (max_reflections=3 — gpt-5-nano needs >12s to complete them, so 12s teardown-interrupted an in-flight third round) plus one full ~5s IdleFlush cycle so the final working→ready settle fires AND is observed before teardown — a teardown race bit a sibling cell at 8s, so the final settle + teardown needs ≥10s of headroom before the tmux kill
After the trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session

script.text: Create a new file solution.py containing a single Python function add(a, b) that returns a + b. Make the edit now; do not ask me anything.

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession (proc-<PID>) event in ready within ~2s of REPL launch (aider's transcript is lazy, so the pre-session is the launch-time observable)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the live aider session_id
events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working transition for the session when the prompt lands — the streaming window opens here

across the streaming window (the --auto-test reflection loop drives MULTIPLE model calls within the one `####` turn — each per-model-call prose block closed by its own `> Tokens:` line emits assistant_message, not turn_done), NO state_transition to ready fires before the synthesized end-of-turn; the session holds working solidly across all flushes (the no-flicker invariant)
NO state_transition to waiting fires during the streaming window
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at the synthesized turn end (IdleFlush after the history goes quiet, ~5s effective latency)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the session at REPL teardown (one long-lived session)
transcript .aider.chat.history.md contains 1 `####` user line and at least 2 `> Tokens:` lines (the initial edit plus ≥1 reflection re-edit — the multiple partial flushes within the one turn)

scenarios.name: subagent-orphan-cleanup
scenarios.description: Parent claude session launches a foreground subagent via the Task tool; once the parent_linked event fires and the child sidechain transcript is visible, the recipe SIGKILLs the parent's process group, leaving the child orphaned. The daemon's pid_manager sweep must emit process_exited for both parent and child without a spurious state_transition to working for the orphaned child during cleanup. Regression coverage for issue #321 (zombie-on-restart). The spec describes a 5-child variant; this recipe records the minimal 1-child shape since the no-working-transition invariant is identical for any N≥1.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
the sigkill driver step kills the entire claude process group (not just the parent PID) so the child is left orphaned rather than reaped by its own clean exit
claude session can spawn at least 1 subagent (default model has Task/Agent tool exposed)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/
A README.md is seeded so the subagent has something to read while the parent is alive — the subagent must still be in flight when SIGKILL lands

script.text: Use the Task tool to launch a general-purpose subagent that reads README.md three times in a row and then waits 30 seconds before reporting back. Do not reply to me until the subagent is done.

verify: events.jsonl contains a parent_linked event for the child UUID pointing at the parent UUID — the child transcript was discovered before SIGKILL
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited event for the parent UUID
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited event for the child UUID
no state_transition to working fires for the child UUID after the parent's process_exited (issue #321 regression invariant)
no state_transition to waiting fires for the child UUID after the parent's process_exited
after readyTTL, both rows are removed via transcript_removed (cleanup-to-removal window may be ~30s depending on readyTTL)
transcript.jsonl contains the parent's user line plus at least one assistant tool_use=Agent, and the child sidechain transcript under <parent>/subagents/ is bundled into <fixture>.subagents/

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (the dev env default is a local LM Studio model — no remote key needed). Verified opencode 1.14.50.
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled (run-cell.sh in isolated/coexist mode, or --attach against a daemon started with --record)
opencode's `task` (subagent) tool is available — capabilities.json declares subagents:true; the built-in `general`/`explore` subagents are on by default

the sigkill driver step must kill the opencode process the daemon is tracking (the one whose cwd == the run cwd) so the child is left ORPHANED rather than reaped by its own clean exit — drive-opencode-interactive.sh's oc_opencode_pid resolves exactly that PID (cwd match), mirroring the daemon's DiscoverPIDByCWD
setup: This cell uses the live-TUI path (run_live): a `sigkill` step routes drive-opencode-interactive.sh into the long-lived tmux opencode process rather than a blocking headless `opencode run` (a blocking run would let the child finish before control returns, so there would be no in-flight child to orphan). The driver launches one `opencode` under tmux, holds it alive across the turn, then kill -9's it mid-turn.

The prompt asks the child to read README.md then run the shell command `sleep 30` before reporting — the `sleep 30` (not the file read, which may not exist: the pipeline seeds no README) is what keeps the child turn IN FLIGHT (working) when the SIGKILL lands. The spawn itself does not depend on the file (the parent invokes `task` regardless), as opencode/foreground-subagent proved with the same empty cwd.

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db); the `task` tool calls Session.create(parentSessionID,…) so the child `session` row carries parent_id = parent.id. The daemon's ProcessOwnedStore watcher polls the table, surfaces the child as transcript_new, and the detector emits parent_linked — opencode's spawn proof (no child .jsonl on disk, unlike claudecode).

Cleanup is process-exit-driven, NOT parser-driven. The child subagent runs IN-PROCESS, so both the parent ses_ and the child ses_ are bound to the SAME opencode PID (each gets its own pid_discovered for that one PID). When the kill lands, the daemon's PIDManager sees that PID vanish (CheckPIDLiveness → ESRCH) and HandleProcessExit records a process_exited for BOTH PID-bound sessions — the child's on the first liveness tick after the kill, then the parent's on the next. The reap issues NO SetState, so NO spurious state_transition to working/waiting fires during cleanup (issue #321 invariant). OBSERVED ORDERING (this recording): child working at kill time → child process_exited → parent process_exited, with zero state_transitions after the kill. The robustly-observable cleanup signal is therefore BOTH process_exited events plus the #321 negative invariant anchored on the child's process_exited (looking forward through the sweep).

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model: in opencode/foreground-subagent the parent took ~120s after birth to invoke `task` and fire parent_linked. So the sleep-before-sigkill is 150s — long enough for the parent to invoke `task`, the child ses_ row to surface with parent_id set, parent_linked to fire, and the child to enter its `sleep 30` — and the kill then lands while the child is still mid-turn. (Total ~150+kill+20s stays inside timeout_seconds=300; bump the sleep if a future model run shifts parent_linked later.)

script.text: Use the task tool to launch a subagent (the built-in 'explore' agent) that first reads the README.md file in the current directory, then runs the shell command `sleep 30`, then reports the file's first heading. Do not reply to me until that subagent is completely finished.

verify: events.jsonl shows the parent ses_… session entering ready, then a single ready→working transition driven by the user prompt

events.jsonl contains at least 1 parent_linked event whose parent_session_id is the parent ses_… UUID — the child session row (parent_id set) was discovered by the SQLite watcher and bound to the parent BEFORE the kill (opencode's spawn proof; no child .jsonl on disk)
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the child session born after the parent turn started

events.jsonl contains a process_exited event for the orphaned CHILD ses_… UUID after the kill — the child shares the parent's opencode PID, so the CheckPIDLiveness sweep reaps it on the first tick after the PID vanishes
events.jsonl contains a process_exited event for the parent ses_… UUID after the child's (the same kill, next liveness tick) — the kill -9 made the daemon observe the watched opencode PID vanish
no state_transition to working fires for the orphaned child after its process_exited (issue #321 regression invariant — the reap issues no SetState)
no state_transition to waiting fires for the orphaned child after its process_exited
no clean child turn_done precedes the reap — the kill, not a graceful exit, ended the child (its last live state was working, mid-turn, at kill time)

scenarios.name: synchronous-slash-command
scenarios.description: Claude session receives two synchronous slash commands (/cost and /help) that do not call the model, then a tiny normal prompt to prove the session is still alive. State must stay ready throughout the slash-command window — the parser's isLocalCommandContent filter drops the <local-command> wrappers so no state machinery fires. Locks down the regression where a future TUI change logs slash invocations as real user messages.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
The slash commands chosen (/cost, /help) must remain local-only in the running claude CLI version — if Anthropic ever routes /cost through the model the scenario premise breaks
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/
Avoid /clear (mints a fresh session UUID — different scenario) and /compact, /init (real model calls — different scenarios)

script.text: Reply with exactly: warm

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready at session birth and staying ready throughout the /cost + /help window (no state_transition to working fires until the final 'Reply with...' send)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point during the slash-command window
events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working→ready cycle for the entire run — driven by the final normal turn only
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 1 user line and 1 assistant line (the slash commands do NOT appear as user turns; their <local-command> wrappers are filtered by the parser)
final session state is ready

Avoid /clear (reset_session — mints a fresh rollout/session_id, a different scenario) and /compact (real model call, a different scenario)

script.text: Reply with exactly: warm
/status

verify: events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working→ready cycle for the entire run, driven by the warm-up turn only (codex's first-activity force drives working, task_complete drives ready)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to working after the warm-up turn returns to ready — /status drives none because codex writes no rollout line for it
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point
the slash window (≥4s after the warm-up turn ends) shows the session continuously ready — no transcript_activity is recorded for /status

codex.notes: Codex difference vs claudecode: a synchronous slash command writes NOTHING to the rollout (verified live against codex-cli 0.130.0 — /status appended 0 lines, task_complete count unchanged), so there's no <local-command> wrapper for the parser to filter; the daemon simply sees no activity. The warm-up turn FIRST is mandatory: codex creates rollout-*.jsonl only after the first user message, so without it resolve_transcript hangs. Only /status is exercised (single slash) because it's the cleanest verified no-LLM render; /help and /mcp are equally synchronous but /status was the one probed. Between runs: warm-up assistant wording, token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ; the state_transition set (ready, ready→working, working→ready) and its order are the structural baseline. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/codex/parser.go (task_complete→turn_done) and whether a codex update started logging slash invocations into the rollout.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (PI_API_KEY or configured provider; verified against pi 0.70.0)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)

/session must remain a local-only render in the running pi version — if pi ever routes it through the model the scenario premise breaks (verified live against pi 0.70.0: /session renders session stats in the TUI and appends ZERO lines to the rollout)
setup: Driver boots a fresh pi REPL in a per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; pi assigns its own session UUID per rollout
Avoid /clear and /new (session-reset — mint a fresh rollout, a different scenario) and /compact (real model call, a different scenario)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
/session

verify: events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working→ready cycle for the entire run, driven by the warm-up turn only (pi's first message drives working, stopReason=stop drives ready)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to working after the warm-up turn returns to ready — /session drives none because pi writes no rollout line for it
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point
the slash window (≥6s after the warm-up turn ends) shows the rollout session continuously ready — no transcript_activity is recorded for /session
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 1 user message line and 1 assistant stopReason=stop line (the /session command does NOT appear as a user turn)

pi.notes: Pi difference vs claudecode: a synchronous slash command writes NOTHING to the rollout (verified live against pi 0.70.0 — /session renders ID/message-counts/tokens/cost directly in the TUI and the rollout line count was unchanged before/after), so there's no <local-command> wrapper for the parser to filter; the daemon simply sees no transcript activity and the session stays ready. The whole observable is the ABSENCE of a second working transition. The warm-up turn FIRST is mandatory: pi creates ~/.pi/agent/sessions/.../<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl only after the first user message, so without it the driver's resolve_transcript never binds a UUID. /session was chosen because it's a verified no-LLM local command (pi's slash menu lists it as 'Show session info and stats'); /usage, /status, /help, /cost are NOT pi commands (the slash menu shows no match). C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (bridges the gap until the UUID rollout transcript appears); session_birth legitimately matches that pre-session, while the turn arc and post-slash dwell pin to the rollout via same_session_as (turn_start anchors to recording start with NO pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working — necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working). Between runs: warm-up assistant wording, token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ; the state_transition set (ready, ready→working, working→ready) and its order are the structural baseline. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go (message/assistant stopReason=stop → turn_done) and whether a pi update started logging slash invocations into the rollout.

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against a provider out-of-band; the interactive driver does NOT handle a login/OAuth prompt (headless aider hangs ~5min on OpenRouter OAuth when no key is present).
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the bare local slash is typed into a long-lived session and torn down cleanly.
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

The slash chosen (/tokens) must remain a bare, argument-less LOCAL command in the running aider version — /tokens reports the chat-context token count via local token_count with NO chat completion (aider/commands.py cmd_tokens). Only the bare no-arg local commands (/tokens, /help, /cost, /status) are LLM-free; /help WITH args spins up a help coder and DOES call the model, so the recipe must keep it bare.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) launches aider in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt.

.aider.chat.history.md (FilesUnderCWD) materializes lazily once the first user input lands; before that the live REPL is observable as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner). The daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session.
Avoid /clear and /reset (end/reset the session — session-reset scenario) and /compact, /commit, /run (real model calls or tool effects — different scenarios). Keep the slash bare and local-only.

After the slash + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the proc-<PID> session (a local slash does NOT mint a new session).

script.text: /tokens

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> session born ready within 2s of launch (the lazy transcript has not materialized yet).

events.jsonl shows EXACTLY ONE ready→working→ready arc on the proc-<PID> session — the bare `/tokens` slash is logged as a `#### /tokens` user prompt (aider has no <local-command> filter), so it drives ready→working even with no model call.
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point (the local slash buffers no assistant question).

transcript.jsonl contains the `#### /tokens` line and a `>` blockquote of the token report, but NO `> Tokens:` close line — so the parser emits no assistant_message and no tool_result (tool_calls_max 0 holds).
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered bound to the proc-<PID> session and exactly one process_exited on it at REPL teardown.
final session state is ready before teardown.
aider.notes: RECORDING DEFERRED: spec+recipe authored as the DOCUMENTED TARGET; not yet recorded because aider has no provider key in this environment (headless aider hangs ~5min on OpenRouter OAuth; the rig precheck delegates auth to the user — proven infra_fail). Validate when aider auth is available in-env. RECORDABILITY (no driver_gap): a BARE local slash like `/tokens` is sendable via the existing `slash` step (the aider interactive driver handles `send|slash)` identically — types the text + Enter); there is no inline picker and no arrow-key navigation, so this is NOT a `keys` gap. The driver implements send/slash/wait_turn/sleep, covering every step this recipe needs. WHY a single `slash` + sleep (no warm-up turn, no wait_turn): unlike claudecode (which needs a warm-up LLM turn first because its UUID transcript only materializes on a real model turn), aider's `#### ` history line materializes .aider.chat.history.md on ANY first user input — the bare `/tokens` line IS that first input, so it both materializes the transcript and drives the one working arc; a warm-up turn would just add a second, redundant arc and break the canonical [ready, working, ready] topology. NO wait_turn after the slash: a local /tokens makes no model call and writes no `> Tokens:` close, so there is no assistant turn to wait for — a wait_turn would hang on an unchanged turn count; the recipe covers the working→ready settle with a fixed 10s sleep instead. AIDER TURN-END IS SYNTHESIZED, NOT MARKED: the markdown transcript has no turn-end marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after ~1.5s quiet (parser.go aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter), clamped to the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll, so the EFFECTIVE working→ready latency is ~5s; the 10s trailing sleep lets that flip fire AND be observed before the daemon shuts down (with a shorter sleep the final ready transition is missed). BEHAVIORAL DIVERGENCE FROM THE CLAUDECODE FRAMING: the scenario-meanings 'User-observable signal' (state stays ready, no → working transition) is written from claudecode's filtered path; aider logs the slash as a normal `#### ` prompt and DOES flicker ready→working→ready. This still satisfies the canonical verify block (which explicitly lists [ready, working, ready], final ready, tool_calls_max 0, has_waiting_state false) — the user-observable OUTCOME (ends ready, zero tool calls, no waiting) is correct; only the intra-window topology differs by design (no <local-command> filter in aider). SESSION IDENTITY: aider stores its transcript in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD), so the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> — no separate UUID rollout session and no handoff phase; the working arc, pid_bind, and teardown all pin to session_birth via same_session_as. What differs between runs: PID/timestamps and the exact token figure in the `/tokens` blockquote; the working→ready edge lands with ~5s idle-flush latency. What must NOT differ: a single working→ready arc on ONE proc-<PID> session, zero tool calls, no waiting, and a single process_exited at teardown.

preconditions: opencode on PATH and authenticated (~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json) — verified opencode 1.14.50. The arc is model-agnostic; the dev env's default is a local LM Studio model, which is fine.
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh isolated mode, or --attach against a daemon started with --record.

LIVE-TUI cell: drive-opencode-interactive.sh routes any recipe carrying a `slash` step to its tmux TUI path (run_live, PR #495), because headless `opencode run` stores a slash command as literal prompt text and runs a model turn (PROBED 2026-05-26: `opencode run "/help"` stored /help as a user message and replied to it). tmux must be on PATH.
setup: Driver launches the opencode TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; opencode keys sessions on the cwd in its SQLite store, isolating this run's rows even alongside other opencode use.

The single warm-up prompt materializes one ses_… row under the TUI process; that row stays the only session for the whole recipe (a no-LLM /help mints no new session). The driver enumerates the cwd's top-level ses_… rows and emits the single-session contract for curate.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
/help

verify: events.jsonl shows the ses_… session entering ready, then ONE working→ready turn (the warm-up 'ok' turn that materializes the store row)

after /help is delivered to the live TUI, NO state_transition to working and NO state_transition to waiting appears for the ses_… session across the slash window — /help is a no-LLM LOCAL command that writes zero new part rows, so the part-driven watcher fires no activity event and the session stays ready
exactly ONE distinct ses_… session_id appears (the local slash mints no new session — distinct from session-reset's /new)
final live state is ready
opencode.notes: Synchronous no-LLM slash command on opencode (/help). The whole observable is the ABSENCE of a SECOND working transition: the warm-up 'ok' turn drives ready→working→ready to materialize the SQLite store row, then /help is delivered to the held-open TUI and the session STAYS ready — opencode's part-driven watcher (scanParts, watcher.go) emits EventActivity only when a NEW part row lands, and /help writes zero new parts (confirmed live 2026-05-26: parts/messages/step-finish all unchanged after /help). WHY a `slash` step and NOT a `send`: headless `opencode run -- "/help"` stores /help as a literal user prompt and runs a full model turn (ready→working→ready), inverting the scenario; the driver routes any `slash` step to run_live() (PR #495) which delivers it to the live TUI via tmux send-keys -l. WHY NO wait_turn after /help: a local /help makes no model call, so the step-finish count never increments — oc_wait_turn would hang/time out on an unchanged turn count (the race that bit checkpoint-rewind). The 6s trailing sleep is the dwell that lets the daemon observe the session HOLD ready across the slash window; expected.jsonl's slash_window phase asserts ready for duration_at_least_ms:4000 with invariants forbidding working/waiting. SINGLE session, no multi-session chain (unlike sibling session-reset, which uses /new to mint a fresh ses_…). What differs between runs: PID/timestamps/token counts and the warm-up turn's wall-clock latency (~30-45s on the local model). What must NOT differ: one working→ready arc on one ses_… session, then a held-ready slash window with no further state transition, ending ready.

scenarios.name: token-accounting
scenarios.description: Three back-to-back no-op turns ('Reply with the word: ok') in a single claudecode session. Exercises the per-turn UsageBreakdown extraction (input/output/cache tokens from raw.message.usage) and the monotonic accumulation into SessionMetrics.TotalTokens across multiple requestId boundaries. The state arc is unremarkable (three ready→working→ready cycles); the assertion lives in events.jsonl payload — each turn boundary emits a PerTurnContribution and TotalTokens is non-decreasing turn-over-turn. Token & cost extraction is already covered by basic-turn for a single turn; this recipe locks down the multi-turn accumulator.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured model out-of-band)

OBSERVES:PARTIAL scope_note — this cell proves only the OBSERVABLE half of token-accounting: the multi-turn TotalTokens accumulator (TotalTokens > 0 after turn 1, non-decreasing after turn 2). The cache-bucket distinction (CacheReadTokens > 0; fresh input = 0 on a fully-cached turn) is UNOBSERVABLE for aider and is deliberately NOT asserted: aider's `Tokens: … cache write … cache hit …` report goes to the terminal via io.tool_output, never to .aider.chat.history.md, and the parser's tokensRE captures only sent/received — so CacheRead/CacheCreation stay 0. See replaydata/agents/aider/scenarios/token-accounting/assessment.json.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the first prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears

Each send bumps the turn counter; wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears (signals aider finished one model call and wrote the per-turn token snapshot). aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after 1.5s quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector poll (~5s effective latency), which is why the inter-turn sleep is ≥12s and the trailing sleep ≥10s — gpt-5-nano answers fast enough that a shorter gap risks the next prompt re-entering working before IdleFlush fires (turn coalescing), losing the per-turn reset

Two turns give exactly one accumulation boundary (T1→T2) to witness the non-decreasing TotalTokens invariant — the minimum that exercises cross-turn accumulation. After the second wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session
No special model or context flags beyond IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL — the assertion is about token accumulation, not which model produced the tokens

After the first run returns the driver discovers the ses_… session id from SQLite (opencode exposes no CLI-assignable session IDs) and chains `--session` for turns 2 and 3 so all three turns land on one session

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleeps and timeout are generous: a slow turn must not trip a false timeout, and each inter-turn / trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so every working→ready is observed before the next prompt re-enters working
