scenarios.name: subscription-detection
scenarios.description: A normal Claude Code Pro/Max session runs one moderate turn, then sits alive long enough for at least one asynchronous statusline tick to arrive. The user-observable point is that a subscription (Claude.ai Pro/Max) session is detected as such: Claude Code's statusLine command POSTs a payload carrying a `rate_limits` block to the daemon (POST /api/v1/hooks/claudecode/statusline), and the daemon's statuslineToSnapshot() turns that into a non-nil RateLimitSnapshot with at least one Window. API-key/Bedrock/Vertex sessions get no rate_limits block and produce a nil snapshot — that absence-variant is the separate, deferred subscription-detection-apikey cell, not this one. Why this exact shape: the statusLine hook fires on the Claude Code CLI's own timer, NOT synchronously with the turn, so the recipe keeps the process alive with extra no-op turns and a generous trailing sleep (>=15s) after the last turn so at least one statusline POST lands and is recorded before teardown — a one-shot --print run would exit before any tick fired. The settings blob is intentionally empty `{}`: claude --settings MERGES with the developer's global ~/.claude/settings.json, whose statusLine.command tees the payload to http://localhost:7837/.../statusline, so the Pro/Max rate_limits POST happens with no per-scenario setup. How it differs between runs: the rate-limit UsedPercent / ResetsAt values are live account numbers and vary every run; token counts and assistant wording vary; the lifecycle state arc (ready->working->ready) and the presence of a non-nil snapshot do not. Where to look if it breaks: confirm the running daemon is on :7837 with --record (attach mode), that ~/.claude/settings.json still has the statusLine curl to /api/v1/hooks/claudecode/statusline, and that the session was Pro/Max (not API-key) — an API-key session legitimately yields a nil snapshot and belongs to subscription-detection-apikey.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated as a Claude.ai Pro/Max subscription session (NOT an API key) — the subscription is what makes the statusline payload carry a rate_limits block
irrlichd is recording on :7837 with --record (this cell records in --attach mode against the developer's running daemon, because claudecode's statusLine hook POSTs to a hardcoded localhost:7837)

the developer's global ~/.claude/settings.json defines statusLine.command teeing the payload to http://localhost:7837/api/v1/hooks/claudecode/statusline (claude --settings merges with global settings, so the empty scenario settings blob does not disable this)
no other claudecode session uses the same UUID
setup: Generate a fresh lowercased session UUID (run-cell.sh does this via uuidgen | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')
Create an empty cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (claude prompts for trust on first encounter — the driver auto-accepts)

script.text: List the first five prime numbers, one per line, and nothing else.
Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new event for the UUID session and a state_transition to ready (session detected in ready)
events.jsonl shows a ready -> working -> ready arc for the first turn (user prompt starts a turn, turn completes back to ready)
final session state is ready

the daemon ingested at least one rate-limit snapshot for this session during the run (subscription signal): the statusline receiver logged 'ingested rate-limit snapshot', i.e. a non-nil RateLimitSnapshot with at least one Window (five_hour and/or seven_day) was attached to the session — this is the presence-of-rate_limits subscription evidence

no API-key path: the run did NOT log 'received statusline tick with no rate_limits block' as the only statusline outcome (that would indicate the session was not a subscription, which belongs to subscription-detection-apikey)

scenarios.name: token-quota-exhausted
scenarios.description: Mocked 429 recording: a local Anthropic-compatible stub (tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-429/main.go) returns 200 + end_turn on the first /v1/messages request and HTTP 429 on the second. claude runs under --bare so it reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY + ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL from env (OAuth/keychain are bypassed). The bespoke recorder at tools/agent-onboarding/recording/record-token-quota-exhausted.sh handles launch, driving, and fixture staging — the standard driver-claudecode-interactive.sh does not pass --bare.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH
go on PATH (to build the mock)
irrlichd running with --record (recording is captured via attached daemon)
port 18765 available on localhost
setup: tools/agent-onboarding/recording/record-token-quota-exhausted.sh builds the mock, launches it, drives claude under --bare, and stages the fixture under .build/refresh/claudecode/token-quota-exhausted-<TS>/.
verify: events.jsonl shows session_birth → prompt1 working/ready arc → prompt2 working/ready arc
final state of the session is ready
no state_transition to waiting (no permission gates expected)
the second prompt's working→ready transition is the quota-exhausted code path

codex.notes: Not recordable: the quota-exhausted signal cannot be elicited deterministically live. Codex DOES emit it (v3 rate_limit_reached_type → RateLimitSnapshot.ReachedType, observed by irrlicht), but (1) the real account quota cannot be burned on demand, (2) there is no codex/OpenAI-compatible 429 mock — tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-429/main.go is Anthropic-only and launches claude --bare, with no codex equivalent, and (3) the codex interactive driver has no quota-exhausted-response injection primitive. The in-window observability is already validated by the sibling codex/quota-burndown cell (monotonic UsedPercent below the cap); this cap-hit ReachedType variant only becomes recordable if a codex 429 mock or a driver fixture-injection primitive is added (a developer task). See assessment.json.

opencode.notes: scope_note: NOT RECORDABLE — daemon observability gap (live-corrected from the doc verdict daemon_capability=full, "step-finish reason=error covers"). This is the SAME transport gap as the opencode/turn-aborted-by-error sibling: a quota/token-budget exhaustion is one error class of an error-aborted turn, and opencode 1.14.50 records ALL turn aborts on the message row (message.data.error), never as a step-finish part. Live store audit of ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db: step-finish reasons = stop(102)/tool-calls(165)/error(0) — ZERO error parts ever persisted; the closest live token-budget analog (context overflow, UnknownError "n_keep: 27429 >= n_ctx: 4096") plus quota/auth APIErrors all land on message.data.error. The watcher's scanParts JOINs message only for role context and discards it (watcher.go:498 `_ = msgData`); the terminal-turn decision (watcher.go:495) reads only isTerminalPart(partData), so no step-finish reason=error part means no turn_done — the session sticks in `working` until process_exited and never settles to `ready`. So the scenario's user-observable signal (final_state ready after the quota failure, no waiting) is unobservable from the store. NOT a driver_gap: the send-only driver is `ready` and a context-overflow prompt elicits the error live, but no observable part is written. Unblocks when opencode emits a step-finish reason=error part on quota aborts OR the watcher consumes message.data.error as a terminal signal (mirroring aider's flushErrorTurn, #262). No recipe, no recording, no expected.jsonl. See replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/token-quota-exhausted/assessment.json.
opencode.scope_note: UNELICITABLE IN THIS RIG — token/quota exhaustion is a remote-provider billing condition; the local LM Studio model (gemma-4-e2b) has no quota/billing, so no quota-exhaustion error can be triggered here. Groundwork is in place for when a billed remote model with a hittable quota is configured in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json: the error-export driver fix (ee477644) carries message.data.error into the fixture, and watcher.go isErrorMessage (#493) settles working->ready on it — but only when the errored message has >=1 part (the documented zero-part blind spot, watcher.go:524-526). Verdict: agent_supports=yes / daemon_capability=full (>=1-part errors) / driver_capability=ready (error-export ported). Shares the error-export primitive with turn-aborted-by-error.

pi.notes: scope_note: NOT RECORDABLE — no deterministic quota-exhaustion trigger, and no distinct quota observable even if one occurred. Reassessed partial/partial (0.55). (1) pi enforces NO self-imposed budget cap: pi --help 0.70.0 exposes no --max-tokens/--budget/--cost-limit/quota-cap flag (only model-cycling 'Limit' globs), so pi cannot self-abort a turn at a token budget on demand. (2) The only path to a quota episode is an upstream provider 429, which (a) cannot be induced on demand and (b) the project rule forbids faking/injecting into the transcript. (3) The claudecode mock-anthropic-429 path does NOT transfer: that mock launches `claude --bare` (no pi equivalent), pi's @mariozechner/pi-ai SDK auto-retries 429s transparently in its request layer, and — decisively — pi writes NOTHING distinct to its JSONL for a quota event. The parser (parser.go) recognizes only session/model_change/compaction/message(stop→turn_done, non-stop→assistant)/user/toolResult(isError); there is no quota/rate-limit/provider-error event kind. So even a real 429 would surface only as an ordinary working→ready arc indistinguishable from a normal turn end — the curated fixture would have no quota signal to assert. This is a cleaner case than the codex sibling (codex at least EMITS rate_limit_reached_type but still can't be driven live): for pi the quota observable does not exist in the transcript at all. expected.jsonl is committed as the DOCUMENTED TARGET (a baseline turn + a capped turn that resolves working→ready without sticking, no waiting) should pi ever ship a self-enforced budget cap OR surface a distinct quota signal in its transcript. No recording; no retry. See replaydata/agents/pi/scenarios/token-quota-exhausted/assessment.json.

aider.notes: scope_note: NOT RECORDABLE YET — assessed yes/partial (agent_supports:yes, observes:partial), so the cell is conceptually applicable, but it cannot be recorded in this environment because it is blocked on BOTH (a) auth and (b) a quota mock — NOT on a missing driver step type, so this is NOT a driver_gap. WHAT aider does: it stops cleanly on a 429/quota or budget cap — coders/base_coder.py's send loop doubles retry_delay until it exceeds RETRY_TIMEOUT (60s, models.py), then surfaces the cap (litellm RateLimitError 'check your quotas' / BudgetExceededError, both retry=True; or the non-retryable APIError+402 'Insufficient credits') via io.tool_warning+io.tool_error and breaks, ending the turn. The error lands in .aider.chat.history.md as a `> …Error…` blockquote (io._tool_message → append_chat_history(blockquote=True)), which irrlicht's aider parser matches with errorRE and turns into a synthetic turn_done via flushErrorTurn (the #262 path) → working→ready. observes:partial because (1) the capped turn is indistinguishable from any other turn-aborting LLM error — there is no quota/budget-specific event kind — and (2) the capped model call emits no `> Tokens:` line, so flushErrorTurn carries nil Tokens/Contribution (no per-turn cost recorded; capabilities.cost_tracking is false). WHY NOT RECORDABLE: (a) no aider auth in this environment — no provider key, headless aider hangs ~5min on provider OAuth, and the rig precheck delegates auth to the user (proven infra_fail). (b) NO deterministic aider quota mock exists: the claudecode mock-anthropic-429 path does NOT transfer — that recorder (tools/agent-onboarding/recording/record-token-quota-exhausted.sh) launches `claude --bare` with ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL pointed at an Anthropic-only stub, but aider's drivers (drive-aider.sh headless, drive-aider-interactive.sh) wire NO base-URL override and have no `--bare` equivalent, and aider routes every provider through litellm — so even WITH auth there is no deterministic way to elicit a 429/BudgetExceededError on demand. A recording becomes possible only once BOTH auth AND an aider-side quota mock (or a driver fixture-injection primitive) exist — a developer task. expected.jsonl is committed as the DOCUMENTED TARGET (ready→working→[`> …Error…` blockquote aborts the turn]→ready within the daemon sweep, single proc-<PID> session, final ready, never waiting). See replaydata/agents/aider/scenarios/token-quota-exhausted/assessment.json.

scenarios.name: tool-gate-permission-prompt
scenarios.description: Claude invokes the Bash tool; a PreToolUse hook returns permissionDecision=ask, deterministically opening the permission prompt. Validates the rule-0 (PermissionPending=true → waiting) path in state_classifier.go, which is the most authoritative gate and predates HasOpenToolCall — defends against the race fixed in #108 where the hook fires before the JSONL tool_use line lands. Recipe captures entry into waiting and stops; the second-scenario variant (late PostToolUse after ready) is a no-op in the classifier and not exercised here.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
irrlicht's Claude hook installer has run (hookinstaller.go) so PermissionRequest / PostToolUse hooks route to the daemon
no other claudecode session uses the same UUID
setup: Generate a fresh lowercased session UUID
Create an empty cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (claude prompts for trust on first encounter — the driver auto-accepts)
Project-local settings.json includes the PreToolUse 'ask' hook below so Bash invocations open the permission prompt deterministically (no reliance on claude's allowlist heuristics)

script.text: Run bash 'echo hello' using the Bash tool.

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession_created for proc-<PID> within 1000 ms
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the UUID session within 500 ms of the first send
events.jsonl contains a state_transition to working for the UUID after the prompt lands
events.jsonl contains a state_transition to waiting for the UUID once the PreToolUse hook fires (PermissionPending=true → rule 0)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready for the UUID after the waiting transition (recipe terminates while session is in waiting)
final session state is waiting
transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 1 assistant line; one assistant message has a tool_use with name=Bash that is gated by the permission prompt

opencode.notes: driver_gap (gap:permission-approval). agent_supports:yes — opencode declares permission_hooks:true and its interactive TUI gates tool execution behind an approve/deny permission overlay. NOT recordable: opencode's permission gate is TUI-only, and the rig drives opencode via headless `opencode run`, which AUTO-EXECUTES tools with NO permission prompt — PROVEN this sweep by the sibling opencode/auto-executed-tool-call recording, where gemma's `write` tool ran automatically inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state (ready→working→ready, no waiting). So the `waiting` episode this cell asserts cannot arise on the headless driver. Even if it did, drive-opencode-interactive.sh drives BLOCKING `opencode run` subprocesses and implements ONLY send / wait_turn (no-op) / sleep in its `case "$TYPE"` dispatch — there is NO approve/deny (or keys) step to RESOLVE a prompt, so the waiting → working/ready resolution half is unreachable too. Eliciting AND resolving a permission gate needs a live-REPL opencode driver (permission-approval primitive); recording deferred — a developer task, out of scope for implement. NOT a recipe-lint step-TYPE gap (the recipe is left applicable:false with no script, so there is no step type to reject) — same shape as the opencode user-esc-interrupt (gap:interrupt) / model-switch-midsession (gap:model-select) cells. daemon side is UNPROVEN: opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (agent.go) and its store-reading parser/watcher have no permission/waiting branch, so whether a permission waiting episode is observable from the SQLite store cannot be confirmed until a live-REPL driver elicits one (a likely observability_correction if/when recorded). expected.jsonl documents the full target arc (ready→working→waiting→working→ready, resolved by approval); assessment.json carries the yes / full / gap:permission-approval verdict. permission_hooks=yes (TUI). UNBLOCK: replace/augment drive-opencode-interactive.sh with a live-REPL driver (tmux-based, like claudecode/pi/aider) that launches the opencode TUI, sends a tool-inducing prompt to open the permission overlay, then fires an approve/deny step.

scenarios.name: turn-aborted-by-error
scenarios.description: Agent's model call fails mid-turn (network error, provider 5xx, timeout). No normal turn-end signal is emitted. Expected arc per spec: ready → working, then → ready within the sweep interval after the agent gives up. Failures split into three buckets: Bucket A (transcript epilogue with synthesized error text → observable via classifier rule 2b — CAPTURED via tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-5xx/main.go), Bucket B (pure mid-stream network failure with NO transcript write → currently UNOBSERVABLE because there is no inactivity sweep on the working state since PR #106 removed the stale-tool timer), Bucket C (system/turn_duration on abnormal exit → observable via turn_done — not yet captured separately).

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH
go on PATH (to build the mock)
irrlichd running with --record (recording is captured via attached daemon)
port 18766 available on localhost
setup: tools/agent-onboarding/recording/record-turn-aborted-by-error.sh builds tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-5xx/main.go, launches it, drives claude under --bare against the mock, and stages the fixture under .build/refresh/claudecode/turn-aborted-by-error-<TS>/. The mock streams message_start + a partial content_block_delta then emits SSE `event: error` (overloaded_error); claudecode appends a synthesized 'API Error' assistant message with a terminal stop_reason and IsAgentDone() fires.
verify: events.jsonl shows session_birth → ready
events.jsonl shows pid_discovered within 1000 ms of birth
events.jsonl shows ready → working when the user prompt lands
events.jsonl shows working → ready after the mock's mid-stream error → synthesized assistant epilogue with terminal stop_reason (Bucket A path)
session state does NOT stick in working — recovery is event-driven (not via sweep, which doesn't exist today)

Bucket B (silent network drop with no transcript epilogue) and Bucket C (system/turn_duration on abnormal exit) are intentionally NOT exercised by this recipe — they remain documented gaps in assessment.json

codex.notes: No deterministic way to elicit a mid-turn provider error against codex with any recipe primitive. The claudecode cell injects a 5xx via tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-5xx/main.go behind an Anthropic base-URL override (tools/agent-onboarding/recording/record-turn-aborted-by-error.sh); codex has no equivalent — the mock tooling is Anthropic-API-specific, and neither drive-codex.sh (headless `codex exec`) nor drive-codex-interactive.sh wires an OPENAI_BASE_URL override or any error-injection step. The only primitive that emits codex's turn_aborted marker is `interrupt` (Escape), which is the user-cancel path (the distinct user-esc-interrupt scenario), not a provider error. A real network/5xx error is non-deterministic and can't be a committed fixture. Independently, even a hand-induced abort would be UNOBSERVABLE today: the codex parser maps only task_complete→turn_done and skips turn_aborted (empty `grep turn_aborted core/`), and there is no working-state idle sweep (pid_manager TTL-cleans only ready/PID-dead sessions), so an aborted turn sticks in working until process_exited — identical to the committed codex/interrupted-turn cell (known_failing, 2/5) and the frozen codex/user-esc-interrupt cell. Unblocks when both (a) a codex/OpenAI error-injection driver path exists and (b) the codex parser maps turn_aborted→turn_done. See assessment.json.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH (1.14.50) and authenticated against its default model. The cell uses opencode's configured default (lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored, a local LM Studio model) so no remote key is needed.

OPENCODE-SPECIFIC TRIGGER (deterministic, no mock/proxy/remote provider): the local LM Studio model MUST be loaded at n_ctx 22528 BEFORE recording — just above the ~21.4k prompt opencode builds for the headless run in run-cell.sh's NON-git staging cwd (opencode enumerates the directory tree when there is no git repo, inflating the system prompt to ~21.4k; a git-init'd cwd would instead be ~14.4k — the harness cwd is NOT a git repo, so size for the non-git case). 22528 leaves ~1.1k OUTPUT headroom, so a 12000-word essay request overflows the window mid-generation and opencode persists a terminal `step-finish` part with reason="length". Load it out-of-band: `lms load gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored --context-length 22528 --identifier gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored -y` (ensure NO second gemma instance at a different context is loaded, or LM Studio may route to it). Verified reproducible in the exact non-git staging-depth cwd (2026-05-27, opencode 1.14.50: input 21426 + output 1102 = 22528 → reason=length). RESTORE n_ctx 32768 after recording so the other opencode cells (which need full context) work.
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and, because the script carries a `{type:live}` marker, takes the LONG-LIVED TUI path: it launches ONE persistent `opencode` TUI under tmux, types the prompt, and keeps that process alive across the turn. This is REQUIRED here (not the headless `opencode run` path): a headless run self-EXITS the instant the turn ends, so even though it writes the terminal step-finish reason=length, the process_exited fires before the daemon's SQLite poll observes the working→ready settle — the recording then shows only ready→working then process_exited (the turn appears to stick in working). The persistent TUI outlives the settle window: oc_wait_turn detects the length turn via the unified oc_turn_total counter (reason IN stop/interrupted/length/error/content-filter), the trailing sleep lets the daemon record working→ready, and only THEN does the driver tear the tmux session down (one process_exited).

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns.

WHY reason=length (not a provider error or message.data.error): a context-OUTPUT overflow makes opencode emit a clean terminal `step-finish reason=length` part — parser.go maps it to turn_done (parser.go:179) and the watcher's isTerminalPart accepts it (watcher.go:435), so the parts-only transcript export already carries the terminal signal end-to-end (daemon AND replay) with no `_error` carrier needed. CRITICAL: n_ctx must be ABOVE the prompt size (22528 > ~21.4k non-git prompt). If n_ctx is BELOW the prompt (e.g. 15360 against the non-git ~21.4k prompt), opencode errors at PROMPT processing → message.data.error ('n_keep > context length') with only a bare step-start and NO step-finish; the headless `opencode run` then self-exits so fast the daemon poll observes ready→working but process_exited BEFORE the working→ready settle — the turn appears to stick in working. The reason=length path keeps the process streaming long enough for the daemon to observe the settle.

The prompt forbids tools and demands immediate continuous prose: the small local model will otherwise call the `task` subagent tool (whose child runs in its own smaller context and never overflows the parent) or stop naturally well under the headroom. Forbidding tools + 'begin writing immediately' keeps generation in a single streaming step that overflows to reason=length.

script.text: Write an extremely long, exhaustive 12000-word essay about the complete history of the Roman Empire. Do NOT use any tools. Do not stop, do not summarize. Begin writing the prose immediately and keep writing paragraph after paragraph until you reach 12000 words.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl shows a state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn begins streaming)

events.jsonl shows a state_transition→ready on the SAME ses_… session_id after the turn aborts: the model output overflows n_ctx and opencode persists a terminal step-finish reason=length → turn_done closes the turn → working→ready; the session does NOT stick in working (recovery is event-driven via the terminal part, not a sweep)
events.jsonl never contains a state_transition to waiting for the session (the aborted turn never reaches a tool/permission gate)
the session's final live state is ready

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated for the user's default provider (the cell uses that provider with an UNSUPPORTED model id, so auth must already work for normal pi recordings)
irrlichd recording — run-cell.sh in isolated mode or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

PI-SPECIFIC: the abort is elicited DETERMINISTICALLY and WITHOUT a mock/proxy/faked transcript line. The headless driver launches `pi --print -p <prompt> --model <bogus-model>`; the provider returns an 'unsupported model' error whose message does NOT match pi's retry regex (_isRetryableError in dist/core/agent-session.js, which retries only overloaded/5xx/rate-limit/network/timeout-class messages up to retry.maxRetries). pi therefore does NOT retry, persists a single assistant message with stopReason='error' (appendMessage fires on message_end, BEFORE the agent_end retry gate), and exits non-zero. Do NOT use a 5xx/rate-limit class trigger — pi would silently retry it and the abort would be non-deterministic.
setup: Model override flows through the cell's settings.model: run-cell.sh writes the settings blob to a path, and drive-pi.sh reads `.model` from it and passes `--model <bogus>` to pi (mirrors drive-aider.sh's IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL convention; IRRLICHT_PI_MODEL is the env escape hatch). Fully reproducible from this committed recipe — no out-of-band env needed.

pi writes one JSONL rollout file per invocation on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy), so the daemon's first scanner tick keys the real UUID transcript — a SINGLE rollout session, no separately-matchable proc-<PID> pre-session phase (same shape as pi/basic-turn).

events.jsonl shows a pid_discovered for the rollout session

events.jsonl shows a state_transition to ready on the rollout session carried by the ERROR epilogue: pi's parser maps stopReason='error' to EventType='assistant', and IsAgentDone()'s assistant-fallback (no open tool call) flips working→ready via classifier rule 2b — state does NOT stick in working (there is no working-state sweep; recovery is event-driven, same as the claudecode row)
events.jsonl never contains a state_transition to waiting
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session (pi exits non-zero after the aborted turn); the live state immediately before teardown is ready
final session state is ready

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated for the model the bogus id routes through (e.g. ANTHROPIC_API_KEY / OPENAI_API_KEY, or a reachable OpenAI-compatible endpoint). The cell deliberately requests an UNSUPPORTED/INVALID model id, so auth must already work for normal aider recordings — the provider must accept the connection and then reject the model, not hang on a missing key. Headless aider with no reachable provider hangs ~5 min on the model-auth prompt (e.g. OpenRouter OAuth); that is an infra/auth failure, not a cell failure.
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so the process outlives the post-error settle window (see AIDER-SPECIFIC note in setup)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

AIDER-SPECIFIC ELICITATION (mirrors the pi sibling — a bogus model id, NO mock/proxy/faked transcript line): the operator must export IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL=<bogus-model> when invoking run-cell.sh. The driver passes it via `aider --model <bogus>`; the provider returns an unsupported/invalid-model error whose litellm message does NOT match aider's retry class, so aider does NOT retry, prints a `> …Error`/`> …Exception` blockquote to .aider.chat.history.md, and the turn aborts with no `> Tokens:` end-of-call line. Do NOT use a 5xx/rate-limit/timeout-class trigger — litellm would retry it and the abort would be non-deterministic. The claudecode mock-anthropic-5xx path does NOT transfer here: that path is `claude --bare` + ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL against a local Go mock; aider routes through litellm and needs the bogus-model trigger instead.
setup: WIRING NOTE (differs from the pi sibling): drive-aider-interactive.sh reads the bogus model ONLY from the IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL env var — it accepts the settings-blob path for ABI parity but ignores it, unlike drive-pi.sh which reads `.model` from the blob. settings.model below is recorded for documentation parity, but the OPERATIVE mechanism for aider is the env var; export IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL=<bogus-model> before run-cell.sh.

The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore --model <bogus>` inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line before sending the prompt.

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears.

INTERACTIVE not headless: aider's working→ready error settle rides flushErrorTurn (the error blockquote) with IdleFlush (1500ms transcript-quiet) as the inactivity backstop, behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector staleWorking poll. A headless `aider --message` would exit non-zero the instant it writes the error blockquote and truncate that settle and the teardown observation. The REPL must outlive the window — hence the trailing sleep ≥6s after the prompt before the driver tears the tmux session down once (one process_exited).

OBSERVABILITY CAVEAT (assessed observes:partial): aider's markdown transcript carries NO end-of-turn token (no stop_reason / task_complete; structured_output_jsonl=false). The named terminal-token mechanism this cell pins is ABSENT for aider — irrlicht synthesizes turn_done via Parser.IdleFlush after ~1.5s of transcript quiescence, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector poll (~5s effective latency). Final state is correct (ready); the gap is the absent in-band token plus the lag.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in the transcript before sending the prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears

The single send bumps the turn counter; wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears (signals aider finished the model call). aider has NO turn-end transcript marker — end-of-turn is synthesized by IdleFlush after ~1.5s quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s poll

The prompt explicitly forbids file edits / SEARCH-REPLACE output: under `--yes-always`, aider auto-accepts any edit the model proposes, and small models (gpt-5-nano) reflexively answer even a plain question by writing a file in diff edit format. A file write lands as a SECOND transcript-activity burst AFTER the turn already settled to ready, producing a spurious extra ready→working transition that breaks the 'exactly one working / exactly one ready' assertion (the edit is invisible as a tool_use — aider's RawLineParser emits none — but the re-activation is observable). The forbid-edits framing keeps the turn a single tool-less round-trip

The trailing sleep is 11s — one full IdleFlush cycle (~1.5s) plus the 5s staleWorking refresh poll plus generous slack — so BOTH the synthesized working→ready settle AND the subsequent teardown process_exited are captured before the driver kills the tmux session

Recipe captures only entry into waiting; the approve/reject continuation is structurally identical to other waiting-resume paths.

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY)
codex 0.130.0+ — Plan Mode is the `/plan` collaboration mode (TUI only; no headless `codex exec` plan entry, no `-c collaboration_mode` config key)
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver launches `codex --no-alt-screen` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits for the TUI banner + MCP boot before the first step

Step 1 is a `/plan` slash that switches the session into Plan Mode. Unlike a prompt, `/plan` toggles a mode and does NOT start a turn (no task_complete), so it is followed by a fixed `sleep`, not `wait_turn` — wait_turn would over-count against the EXPECTED_TURNS bump every send/slash applies and time out.

Plan Mode's developer prompt encourages a PHASE-1 exploration pass; running in an empty cwd plus an explicit 'emit the plan now' instruction keeps the plan turn to a single short reply that wraps a <proposed_plan> block.

The codex parser synthesizes a SYNTHETIC ExitPlanMode tool_use from the <proposed_plan> span (parser.go parseCodexMessage); that open call survives task_complete so the session sits in waiting (the plan-review gate) until the user approves. The interactive driver keeps the rollout session ALIVE across both turns; the REPL is torn down only at script end, so the daemon observes the full waiting→working→ready recovery on one live session.

The approval turn (step 4) MUST do enough real work to span several wall-clock seconds with intermediate tool calls — it tells codex to create a.txt/b.txt/c.txt and run `ls` as distinct steps before its final summary. WHY: the daemon coalesces transcript activity per session in a 2s debounce window (session_detector_activity.go), and a turn-end (Terminal) event short-circuits the window. A trivial text-only recovery reply (the original 'confirm in one sentence' prompt) landed its user message and the agent's whole reply in a single sub-2s burst, so the FIRST activity event already saw a completed turn and the classifier went waiting→ready directly — the brief working blip was never emitted (debounce-coalesced). Spreading the recovery across several tool calls means the first post-approval activity event observes an IN-PROGRESS turn → working, and the terminal task_complete arrives seconds later → ready. That produces the OBSERVABLE waiting→working→ready arc the spec asserts. This is the same longer-interactive-turn fix that made basic-turn capture a distinct working→ready pair. codex runs in its default workspace-write sandbox with approval_policy=on-request, so creating files inside the fresh cwd and running `ls` there are autonomous workspace operations that do not raise a command-approval prompt (which would itself be a different waiting gate). If a future codex default DID gate one of these commands, the recovery arc would show an extra waiting episode — re-check the sandbox/approval config in that case.

script.text: /plan

We are in Plan Mode. Do NOT explore the repo and do NOT ask any clarifying questions — there is nothing to explore here. Immediately produce your final plan for this trivial task: add a file hello.py containing a single line `print("hello world")`. Output ONLY a single <proposed_plan> block (opening tag on its own line, closing tag on its own line) with a short Summary, Key Changes, and Test Plan. No other text.

Approved — proceed with implementation now. Do NOT emit another <proposed_plan> block and do NOT ask any questions. Execute this multi-step task in order: (1) create a file a.txt containing the single line `alpha`, (2) create a file b.txt containing the single line `beta`, (3) create a file c.txt containing the single line `gamma`, (4) run `ls` in the working directory, then (5) reply with one short plain sentence summarizing which files now exist. Do the file creations and the `ls` as distinct steps so the work is visible.

AIDER-SPECIFIC (observes: partial): aider's cancel key is Ctrl-C, not ESC — aider has no ESC-to-interrupt binding (scenario-meanings names Ctrl-C for aider; ESC is for claudecode/codex/pi). The ready→working→ready arc IS observed, but ONLY via the generic turn-end path: a Ctrl-C-cancelled turn writes NO `> Tokens:` line and no error blockquote, so the parser's explicit turn-closers never fire and IdleFlush synthesizes a bare turn_done after 1.5s of transcript quiet — the SAME path every clean aider turn takes. irrlicht therefore observes the interrupt only as a GENERIC turn-end; there is NO interrupt-IDENTITY signal. aider's markdown has no '[Request interrupted by user]' marker (that is claudecode-specific) and the aider parser sets no LastWasUserInterrupt, so classifier rule 3 ('user ESC interrupt') is never engaged. Unlike the claudecode row above, do NOT verify an interrupt marker or a rule-3 transition; the interrupt is invisible AS an interrupt.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` (plus --model if IRRLICHT_AIDER_MODEL is set) inside a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line before sending the prompt

aider's transcript .aider.chat.history.md is LAZY (FilesUnderCWD): it materializes only once the first user input lands, so the live REPL is observable from launch as a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready (the process scanner) until the per-cwd transcript appears

TIMING (model is fast — gpt-5-nano): the prompt MUST elicit a genuinely long/slow generation (an exhaustive multi-thousand-word essay) and the pre-interrupt sleep MUST be SHORT (~2s) so the Ctrl-C lands while the model is still streaming mid-turn. A long sleep before the interrupt would let a fast model finish the turn first (writing a `> Tokens:` line), so the Ctrl-C would interrupt nothing — no real mid-turn abort. Same approach as aider/interrupted-turn.

The interrupt step sends tmux Ctrl-C. A Ctrl-C-cancelled turn writes NO `> Tokens:` line, so the parser emits no turn-closing assistant_message and the interrupt is NOT followed by wait_turn (it would hang). The cancelled turn simply never completes a model call.

End-of-turn has no transcript marker in aider; the interrupted turn is closed by IdleFlush, which synthesizes a bare turn_done after 1.5s of transcript quiet, surfaced as working→ready behind the daemon's 5s SessionDetector staleWorking poll (~max(1.5s, 5s) effective latency) — this is why the trailing sleep AFTER the interrupt is ≥10s so the post-interrupt working→ready settle lands well before teardown
After the trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — aider exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the session

opencode.notes: scope_note: the opencode TUI bare-Escape does NOT elicit a step-finish reason=interrupted on the local LM Studio provider, so a faithful interrupt recording still cannot be captured — even with the `interrupt` step now ported (23ab346e: oc_interrupt sends a bare Escape via run_live, oc_turn_total counts reason=interrupted). FOUR live attempts total, ALL ending on a NON-interrupted terminal: (1,2) 2026-05-27 — 14s/30s ESC, the model either stalled in a pre-stream tool-call (ESC no-op) or completed a full essay on reason=stop. (3,4) 2026-05-28 (this author, lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored @32768 ctx, run_live + ported interrupt, recipe = send long essay → sleep 20s → interrupt → sleep 5 → recovery `Reply ok` → wait_turn): attempt 3 — the essay prompt made the model call the built-in `task` SUBAGENT tool; it stalled ~122s pre-stream, then ended on step-finish reason=tool-calls with a MessageAbortedError on the message (the driver's oc_interrupt TIMED OUT, total stayed 0). Attempt 4 — prompt amended to FORBID tools/delegation and write directly; verified in opencode.db (ses_1944fcac…): the model STILL stalled ~125s pre-stream, then streamed a 14547-char essay and ended NATURALLY on step-finish reason=stop; the ~20s ESC landed in the pre-stream dead zone (no in-flight stream to cancel) and was a no-op. BOTH step-finish parts read reason=stop; ZERO reason=interrupted anywhere. ROOT CAUSE: this model's ~2min pre-stream latency means a bounded ESC can never land mid-stream, and when streaming finally begins the essay completes on `stop`, not `interrupted`. DRIVER FALSE-POSITIVE persists: oc_interrupt settles on ANY terminal step-finish crossing the baseline (reason IN ('stop','interrupted')) — it reported 'settled' on attempt 4's natural `stop`. Per the pipeline (do NOT promote a recording whose turn ended on `stop` — it proves the wrong thing) NEITHER attempt was promoted. The recipe shape above (long essay, no tools, sleep→interrupt→recovery) is RETAINED in this note as the documented target; expected.jsonl is kept as the ready→working→[interrupt]→ready + recovery spec. OBSERVABILITY: daemon_capability is genuinely full (parser.go:158 maps reason=interrupted→turn_done, test-covered) and the driver step is ported (driver code ready) — the blocker is the MODEL/PROVIDER: a bare-Escape mid-stream abort never PERSISTS a step-finish reason=interrupted on this local provider. UNBLOCK: (a) a provider/model that begins streaming promptly AND whose TUI cancel writes step-finish reason=interrupted, and (b) harden oc_interrupt to assert an ACTUAL `interrupted` part (not merely any terminal step-finish) so a no-op ESC fails loudly instead of false-settling. See assessment.json (LIVE EVIDENCE 2026-05-28 caveat).

The live REPL needs a model that actually answers so the reflection loop really iterates inside one turn; headless aider with no reachable provider hangs ~5min on the model-auth/OAuth prompt — which is why a recording for this cell is deferred until auth is available in the environment.

agent_supports:partial scope_note (mirror of the coverage matrix): aider's ONLY autonomous self-prompting loop is the reflection loop in coders/base_coder.py run_one() — it auto-re-prompts the model on auto-lint/auto-test/edit failure and is hard-capped at max_reflections=3 (base_coder.py:101). It is partial (not yes) because the cap is a hardcoded constant with NO --max-reflections/--max-iterations CLI/env/settings override (so the recipe must DRIVE the loop to its cap via repeated lint/test failures, it cannot pre-lower the cap), AND it is a reflection/error-recovery loop rather than an open-ended goal-pursuit mode like claudecode's /goal — aider iterates only when its own output fails validation.

irrlicht_observes:partial scope_note (mirror of the coverage matrix): irrlicht observes the graceful-termination SHAPE (working→ready, no stuck working) but CANNOT distinguish a cap-hit from a normal turn-end — both terminate via the identical 1500ms IdleFlush turn_done. The cap warning `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` IS written to the transcript but the parser's errorRE matches only Error/Exception (not 'stopping'), so it is ignored; no cap-specific daemon signal is emitted. The recipe therefore asserts only the user-observable termination shape, not 'cap reached' specifically.

KNOWN TIMING RISK (observes:partial): the reflection loop is genuinely WITHIN one turn (one `#### ` header, run_one's `while reflected_message:` loop, max_reflections=3), so the topology is one contiguous working span. The only thing that can break the no-flicker target is the 1500ms IdleFlush: if any single inter-reflection model call leaves the transcript silent for >~1.5s, IdleFlush synthesizes a premature turn_done (working→ready) and the next `> Tokens:` line reopens it (ready→working). A fast local model avoids this; a slow remote model can trip it. The root cause is timing, not topology — do NOT widen the spec to accept the flicker.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches aider in a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in .aider.chat.history.md before sending the prompt

HOW the reflection is elicited deterministically (mirror of the aider/self-correction-iteration sibling that validated 6/6): settings.aider_extra_args launches aider with `--auto-test --test-cmd false`. `false` always exits non-zero, so EVERY edit aider makes inside the turn trips a test failure and forces a reflected_message — independent of what gpt-5-nano actually writes. The loop re-prompts until it hits max_reflections=3, then stops with `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` and returns; the turn always settles to ready, so we never depend on the model producing passing code. The driver appends these flags to its fixed arg set (--no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore [+ --model]); the step grammar (send/wait_turn/sleep) is fully supported (no driver_gap). The send asks for a small file edit aider can reliably perform, which is what arms --auto-test; it deliberately does NOT request a shell command — aider's reflection loop fires on --auto-lint/--auto-test failure, not on a model-invoked shell tool's exit code.

aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD, RawLineParser), so the daemon keys the WHOLE session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session and no handoff/reconcile; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row. The transcript is LAZY (materializes on first input), but the proc-<PID> pre-session is observable from launch (that is where the spec's session_birth 'ready within 1s' is grounded)

WHY the capped loop is ONE turn: io.user_input() writes the `#### ` history header exactly once per top-level prompt, before Coder.run_one; run_one's reflection loop sets reflected_message and calls send_message directly, never re-emitting a `#### ` header (base_coder.py:860/879/924-944). The parser treats every `> Tokens:` line as assistant_message (NOT turn_done) so the turn stays open across the multiple capped model calls (parser.go:18-23,146-188), and `> Running <cmd>`→tool_result markers never change state away from working — so the whole capped reflection chain is one contiguous ready→working→ready span

No auto-mode / permission step is needed and there is no gate to enable: aider runs under --yes-always and has NO permission hook (capabilities.json permission_hooks:false), so any shell-command confirmation auto-answers and the session never enters waiting — unlike the claudecode sibling for this cell, which must enable auto mode to keep its Stop-hook loop alive without tripping a gate

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message`: aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run can exit promptly after the reply, so the daemon misses the terminal working→ready settle before process_exited and truncates the arc. Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker — one per model call inside the reflection loop). The recipe waits TWICE (same as the self-correction sibling): the FIRST wait_turn returns on the initial edit's `> Tokens:`, the SECOND returns on the first reflection round's `> Tokens:` (the auto-test failed and aider re-called the model). This guarantees the reflection loop actually entered before teardown — a single wait_turn returns too early (the first reflection call is still in flight). The ≥20s trailing sleep then absorbs the remaining capped reflection rounds (max_reflections=3) plus one full ~1.5s IdleFlush cycle so the terminal working→ready settle is observed before the single tmux kill-session teardown

script.text: Create a new file loop.py containing a single Python function add(a, b) that returns a + b. Make the edit now; do not ask me anything.

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new + presession_created for the proc-<PID> session born ready within ~1s of REPL launch
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the same proc-<PID> session_id

events.jsonl shows exactly one state_transition→working on the proc-<PID> session_id when the prompt is sent, sustained across the capped reflection iterations (auto-lint/auto-test keep failing → up to max_reflections=3 re-prompts, all inside one turn)

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready between reflection iterations — the session stays continuously working across the loop (the parser holds working across `> Tokens:`/`> Running` runs within one `#### ` turn)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point (aider has no permission hook; runs under --yes-always)

events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at the single turn end (synthesized by IdleFlush ~1.5s after the last `> Tokens:` when the loop hits its cap and stops self-prompting) — the user-observable signal: state reaches ready even though the goal was not fully met, and does NOT stick in working
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the proc-<PID> session at REPL teardown

OBSERVES:PARTIAL — the cap-hit is NOT distinguishable from a normal turn-end: the `> Only 3 reflections allowed, stopping.` warning is written to the transcript but the parser ignores it (errorRE matches only Error/Exception), so do NOT assert a 'cap reached' signal — assert only the graceful working→ready termination shape

KNOWN TIMING RISK (observes:partial): if any single inter-reflection model call is silent >~1.5s, IdleFlush can fire a premature working→ready then the next `> Tokens:` reopens working→working flicker — flag it as a known timing limitation, do NOT widen the spec to accept it
Do not assert the auto-lint/auto-test output text — it is model/tool/OS-dependent; assert only the state topology and the single graceful turn end

opencode.notes: scope_note: opencode has no autonomous self-prompting loop, so no iteration-limit stop to observe; presupposes the autonomous-loop primitive opencode lacks. Stock opencode is turn-based (a turn ends at session.idle awaiting the next user message), with no /goal, /loop, --auto, or max-iterations cap; self-prompting is plugin-only (oh-my-opencode Ralph Loop / Ultrawork) and the upstream built-in /loop request (anomalyco/opencode#23578) is still open. With no native loop there is no iteration cap to hit. agent_supports=unknown (see assessment.json).

scenarios.name: task-list
scenarios.description: Agent maintains a todo/task list within its own session — no subagents involved. The driver asks the agent to create three task items and walk each one pending → in_progress → completed. The point of the scenario: the items surface in the session API's `tasks` field with matching subjects and status, while SubagentCount stays 0 and state follows a normal ready → working → ready arc. Claude Code drives this via the Task tool (TaskCreate/TaskUpdate); OpenCode via todowrite (full-list replace). Distinct from foreground-subagent / background-subagent, which spawn real child sessions via the Agent tool.

claudecode.prompt: Use your task-list tool to create exactly three todo items, in this order: 'read README', 'summarize README', 'reply done'. Then work them one at a time: mark each item in_progress and then completed via the task-list tool before moving to the next. Do NOT use the Task/Agent tool to spawn any subagent — keep everything in this one session. When all three items are completed, reply with exactly the word: done
verify: transcript.jsonl contains three TaskCreate tool_use blocks (the three todo items) plus the TaskUpdate blocks that drive each to completed
transcript.jsonl contains NO tool_use block with name=Agent (no subagent spawned)
no parent_linked events appear and no subagent transcripts are written (SubagentCount stays 0)
the session API's tasks field lists the three items with matching subjects, all ending completed
state follows ready -> working -> ready; final session state is ready

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated (run a single message in opencode TUI to confirm)
irrlichd is recording (--attach or isolated) with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled
the default opencode model exposes the `todowrite` tool (most chat models do; lightweight 'codex' models may not)
setup: Driver runs `opencode` in tmux against <staging>/cwd so each scenario gets a fresh project directory. OpenCode appends to the global ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db; sessions are isolated by their session id, not by cwd.

script.text: Use your todo list (the todowrite tool) to track three short work items, updating each item's status (pending → in_progress → completed) as you make progress. Do NOT spawn any subagent. Then reply with exactly the word: done

verify: events.jsonl shows the session entering ready, then a single ready->working transition
the session's transcript (SQLite parts) contains at least one tool part with tool=todowrite carrying the three-item snapshot
no child session rows appear in ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db with parent_id = this session id (SubagentCount stays 0)

the session API's tasks field lists the three tracked items with their statuses surfacing (the committed recording ends with one completed, one in_progress, one pending — exercising all three statuses)
events.jsonl shows a working->ready transition at turn end; final session state is ready

orchestrator_scenarios.name: agent-discovery
orchestrator_scenarios.description: Two rigs declared in rigs.json; gt rig list returns the same set. Verifies that both rigs surface as codebases with their default workers (witness, refinery), and that mayor/deacon appear as idle global agents when no sessions match.

orchestrator_scenarios.name: role-assignment
orchestrator_scenarios.description: Two sessions are active with CWDs under <rig>/witness and <rig>/refinery. The poller must populate worker.SessionID for those roles via deriveRole/CWD matching.

orchestrator_scenarios.name: state-aggregation
orchestrator_scenarios.description: Daemon reports running=false and the rig is degraded. Verifies that orchestrator.State.Running and Codebase.Status reflect aggregated multi-source state.

orchestrator_scenarios.name: agent-join-leave
orchestrator_scenarios.description: Polecat workforce churns over 3 polls: tick-001 has 1 polecat, tick-002 adds a second, tick-003 removes the second. Verifies that the snapshot stream reflects each join/leave on its own poll cycle.

orchestrator_scenarios.name: polling-lifecycle
orchestrator_scenarios.description: gt CLI is healthy on tick 1, fails completely on tick 2 (poller falls back to cached rigs.json sidecar; polecats/dogs/boot become empty since they have no fallback), then recovers on tick 3. Verifies the production fallback path through the rigs.json sidecar.
